title: Phased GPU Activation Scenarios by Cloud Provider and Latency SLOs
preview:
Purpose:
This working sheet sketches three activation scenarios for NA / EU / APAC across our three primary cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure). Focus: align GPU provisioning cadence with model-latency SLOs, warm-pool sizing, and fallback windows to minimize user-visible regressions while containing cost.

High-level constraints and assumptions:
- Target quarters: 2026 Q3 -> 2027 Q1.
- Primary model classes: embed-small (128 tokens), gen-medium (512 tokens), gen-large (2k tokens).
- Preferred hardware mix: A100-80GB for gen-large, A10G/RTX variants for gen-medium, T4-style for embed workload where supported.
- Cold-start penalty: 40-120ms p50 per model variant when warm-pool not present.
- Warm-pool reduces cold-starts by ~90% for pinned model variants.
- Latency SLO tiers: Platinum p95<=60ms, Gold p95<=120ms, Silver p95<=250ms.
- Cost sensitivity: keep hosted per-token marginal cost delta <= +12% vs baseline in Balanced scenario.

Scenario matrix (summary):
- Conservative (low risk, high reuse): slow activation, larger warm pool fraction, prioritize NA then EU then APAC.
  - NA: +120 A100-eq (spread AWS us-west-2 + GCP us-central) across Q3-Q4 2026. Warm-pool 22% of fleet. Target p95: Platinum on gen-medium, Gold on gen-large.
  - EU: +60 A100-eq in Q4 2026. Warm-pool 20%. Platinum only for embed-small.
  - APAC: +40 A100-eq in Q1 2027. Warm-pool 18%. Gold for gen-medium.
  - Expected fleet utilization: 62% steady-state; cost delta +4% vs baseline.

- Balanced (default recommendation): staggered activation with mixed provider mapping for regional redundancy.
  - NA: +180 A100-eq across Aug-Nov 2026 (AWS primary, GCP failover). Warm-pool 15% for gen-large, 12% for gen-medium. Platinum on embed & gen-medium. Gold for gen-large.
  - EU: +120 A100-eq Sep-Dec 2026 (GCP + Azure). Warm-pool 12%. Platinum on embed-small only.
  - APAC: +90 A100-eq Oct 2026-Jan 2027 (Azure + local cloud partners). Warm-pool 10%. Gold for gen-medium.
  - Expected fleet utilization: 72% at peak windows; cost delta +9% vs baseline but latency compliance improves for priority customers.

- Aggressive (fast ramp to capture demand): rapid activation, smaller warm pools, multi-cloud aggressive hedging.
  - NA: +300 A100-eq Jul-Oct 2026 across AWS/GCP/Azure. Warm-pool 8%. Platinum for all model classes across high-tier customers.
  - EU: +220 A100-eq Aug-Nov 2026. Warm-pool 8%.
  - APAC: +160 A100-eq Sep-Dec 2026. Warm-pool 6%.
  - Expected fleet utilization: 84% after 6 weeks; cost delta +18% vs baseline. Requires ~3x faster ops onboarding and additional SRE on-call coverage.

Provider mapping heuristics and latency routing rules:
- By default, route requests to region-local fleet for p95 latency savings. If local warm-pool fill < 60% for requested model variant, route to nearest region with compatible warm-pool that keeps p95 within SLO.
- For Platinum customers, prefer pre-warmed dedicated pool (pinned instances) in the regional Dedicated capacity; fallback to cross-region hosted with autoscaling if local Dedicated saturated.
- Use provider affinity score to decide where to pin gen-large variants: score = (latency_index * 0.45) + (cost_index * 0.35) + (procurement_stability * 0.2). Update monthly.

Warm-pool sizing guidance (rule of thumb):
- Define warm-pool fraction by model class and scenario:
  - embed-small: 8-16% (lower memory, fast startup)
  - gen-medium: 12-22%
  - gen-large: 10-25% depending on Platinum commitments
- Warm-pool should target top 3 model variants (most used) per region.
- Autoscaling policy: maintain W warm instances per 100 active concurrent requests of that variant. W tuned during first 2 weeks of rollout.

Activation timeline (sample cadence for Balanced scenario):
- July 2026: finalize vendor contracts, reserve initial tranche capacity (NA heavy).
- Aug 2026: provision NA tranche 1 (60 A100-eq), enable canary serving for gen-medium.
- Sep 2026: provision EU tranche 1 (40 A100-eq), ramp warm-pools for embed-small. Launch latency canary dashboards.
- Oct 2026: provision NA tranche 2 + APAC tranche 1 (combined 120 A100-eq), begin cross-region failover rehearsals.
- Nov 2026: EU tranche 2 + capacity tuning (observability-driven adjustments).
- Dec 2026-Jan 2027: full verification, customer-targeted pinning, safety buffer reduction if utilization stable.

Operational triggers and rollback criteria:
- Trigger scale-up early if p95 latency for Platinum routes breaches SLO for 3 consecutive 10-minute windows AND cold-start rate >5%.
- Rollback or hold activation if error-rate >1.5% for inference path within 30 minutes of activation or if inter-region replication lag >200ms for stateful KV caches.

Risks and mitigations (short list):
- Vendor supply delay: keep a prioritized fallback list of equivalent SKUs across providers; leverage spot/backfill only for non-Platinum traffic.
- Unexpected hot shards in EU: implement dynamic shard draining + temporary cross-region redirect with per-request tagging.
- Cost overrun in Aggressive: require finance guardrails (spend alerts at 65%, 80%, 95% of monthly budget) and immediate capping policy.

Metrics to track during rollout (dashboard suggestions):
- p50/p95/p99 latency by region, model class, and customer tier.
- Warm-pool hit rate by variant and region.
- Fleet utilization per SKU and aggregate A100-equivalent.
- Cold-start frequency and median penalty.
- Cost per 1M tokens by region and scenario.

Action items and owners (next 6 weeks):
- Infra procurement: finalize tranche sizes, negotiate flexible delivery dates (Owner: Diego Martinez).
- Observability: add canary deck panels (Owner: Priya Nair).
- SRE runbooks: update warm-pool scaling doc and rollback playbook (Owner: Samir Patel).
- Finance: model cost sensitivities for Balanced vs Aggressive (Owner: Maya Gupta).

Open decisions / questions:
- Should we standardize gen-large on one SKU family regionally to simplify warm-pools at the expense of vendor diversity? Pros/cons list needed.
- Acceptable cost-delta threshold for pilot customers in APAC before committing to earlier activation window?

Appendix (quick tables) - Balanced scenario extracted snapshot (numbers are A100-equivalent GPU count):
Region,ProviderPrimary,ProviderSecondary,ActivationWindow,TrancheSizes,WarmPoolPercent,TargetUtilizationPeak
NA,AWS,GCP,Aug-Nov 2026,180,15/12/12 (large/medium/embed),72%
EU,GCP,Azure,Sep-Dec 2026,120,12/12/10,68%
APAC,Azure,PartnerCloud,Oct 2026-Jan 2027,90,10/10/8,70%

Notes: this is a working planning sheet intended to seed the capacity runbook and procurement requests. Numbers should be validated against historical per-token demand profiles and the model onboarding queue (see linked artifacts). Expect multiple rapid iterations; prioritize latency SLO compliance for Platinum customers over minimal cost in early rollouts.

Document history: draft created 2026-02-18 by Ava Chen; collaborative edits through 2026-03-04.

title: fragments-to-actions-annotated-ask-dossier-2026-02-12
preview:
Purpose and shape
---
This is a lightweight, annotated dossier: pull noisy customer fragments (support threads, pilot interviews, NPS comments) -> short inference -> concrete small experiments ("micro-probes") that eng/product can take in a 1–3 sprint cadence. Goal: convert signal fragments into something actionable, with owners and a quick effort guess. Not a canonical roadmap — think triage + experiment list.

Methodology notes
---
- Sources: eight support threads (Jan 2026), two pilot onboarding sessions, top 20 NPS verbatims, internal ops-escalation notes.
- Heuristic used: pick fragments that repeat across at least two customers or that have high operational risk (SLA, security, billing).
- Prioritization scale used here: Impact (H/M/L) x Effort (S/M/L); quick color hint in-line.

Topline synthesis (one-liners)
---
- Hosted API users report occasional large-response truncation when using streaming + function-calls; suspect interplay between streaming buffer flush and chunked responses. (Impact: H, Effort: M)
- Several mid-market customers want a clearer "model-cost per route" view in Console; current token graphs are noisy and don't map to per-endpoint spends. (Impact: H, Effort: S)
- Dedicated customers want a predictable cold-start cadence visibility (when autoscaler schedules new nodes) to correlate latency spikes to infra events. (Impact: M, Effort: M)
- A recurring ask: better first-party examples for reranking and embeddings for specific verticals (search, support summarization). (Impact: M, Effort: S)

Annotated fragments -> suggested actions
---
1) Fragment: "Our chat sometimes cuts mid-sentence during 'summarize' calls; we see the 'DONE' event but tokens missing" (support thread #1121)
   Inference: streaming flush race with function calling/structured output. Could be a buffering/commit ordering bug where KV cache flush triggers a sentinel prematurely.
   Impact/Effort: H / M
   Micro-probe: instrument streaming pipeline to emit deterministic logs for the last 5 tokens and whether function metadata was attached. Add an opt-in header X-rw-debug-stream=1 to get verbose traces for pilot customers.
   Owner: eng-serving-runtime (tentative: Omar Li)
   Acceptance: reproduce locally with toy prompt, confirm fix avoids truncation in 95% of sampled traces.
   Risks: extra log volume; need retention/PII check.

2) Fragment: "We can't tell which customer routes are burning our credits — endpoint/token cost attribution is fuzzy" (account exec note)
   Inference: Console metrics are per-model/per-token but not tied to 'routes' or API keys in a way customers expect (they use multiple microservices behind the key).
   Impact/Effort: H / S
   Micro-probe: add a per-route breakdown in Console for a single-tenant org: map API key -> route path -> token counts for last 7d. Implement as read-only UI column + CSV export.
   Owner: product-console (Sana)
   Acceptance: three pilot customers can reconcile invoice vs console within 2% error.

3) Fragment: "We saw latency spikes in the morning, looks like autoscaler is spinning up GPUs" (dedicated customer ops)
   Inference: no clear event correlation in customer view; autoscaler decisions internal.
   Impact/Effort: M / M
   Micro-probe: expose a lightweight timeline in Dedicated Console that marks autoscaler scale-up/down events + node types. Include a 'why' short text (e.g., CPU/memory/burst threshold).
   Owner: product-dedicated + infra (Diego)
   Acceptance: reduce SRE handoffs by 40% for first set of pilot customers.

4) Fragment: "We tried reranking examples from docs — throughput is bad and outputs vary wildly" (dev workshop)
   Inference: sample prompts are generic; customers want verticalized recipes and perf knobs (batch sizes, quantization suggestions).
   Impact/Effort: M / S
   Micro-probe: assemble a short vertical playbook (search-support-ecomm) with: recommended model, batch size, quantization profile, sample prompt, eval prompt set. Ship as repo + Console 'recipes' panel.
   Owner: product-optimize + applied-ml
   Acceptance: two customers adopt recipe and report >20% RPS improvement or cost reduction.

5) Fragment: "Private deployment key rotation is scary — we can't tell if rotation will drop in-flight requests" (security call)
   Inference: docs/UX around key rotation and live sessions unclear; worry about HSM/KMS interactions.
   Impact/Effort: H / S
   Micro-probe: draft clear runbook + FAQ and a checkbox 'rotate keys with zero-downtime' flow that coordinates short TTL on worker tokens + rolling update hooks. Validate with customer staging env.
   Owner: private-deployments + security (Sana/Omar)
   Acceptance: documented runbook + customer-signed off test in staging.

Decision heuristics quickview
---
- Quick wins (low effort, meaningful impact): per-route cost breakdown; vertical recipes for reranking/embeddings; rotation runbook.
- Medium bets: streaming debugging header + logs (needs infra buy-in), autoscaler timeline UI.
- Watchlist (needs more signal): billing anomalies that map to retries; embedding drift complaints (may be model/data problem).

Misc fragments and micro-ideas (notes to self)
---
- Consider an "ask tag" in support UI so CS can surface repeated asks automatically to product. Small SQL, low effort.
- Quick eval harness: take 10 customer prompts for reranking -> run across two candidate model configs -> show delta table in Console. Could be a 1wk eng + 1wk product lift.
- Pricing note: customers ask for 'per-route caps' to avoid runaway dev/test usage. Might be a policy + UI checkbox. Mark for billing/product sync.

Open questions to resolve before rollout
---
- Data retention / PII: if we enable verbose stream traces for debugging, what's the minimum mask we must apply? (security gating).
- Billing mapping: do we have reliable API-key -> route path linkage in proxy logs across all deployment types (hosted vs dedicated vs private)? If not, quick bridge work needed.
- Ownership: who owns the Console CSV export feature? Is it Console eng or a cross-team infra endpoint?

Next steps and proposed owners (fast sprint plan)
---
- Sprint 1 (2 weeks): implement per-route breakdown (product-console / eng console) + rotation runbook draft. Owners: Sana + Omar.
- Sprint 2 (2 weeks): vertical recipes repo + Console recipes UI (product-optimize + applied-ml). Owner: Diego + Sana.
- Sprint 3 (2–3 weeks): streaming debug header and trace opt-in pipeline; infra + runtime (Omar + eng-serving-runtime).

Quick meeting ask
---
- 30m sync with Product Console, Eng Serving Runtime, and Security next Tue 11am PT. Agenda: pick 2 sprint-1 items, confirm data gating for traces, assign concretes.

Appendix: raw verbatim snippets (short)
---
- "Tokens disappeared mid-message; we had to return to user and they lost context" (support)
- "Our CFO wants a CSV that ties cost to product pages — right now it's a mess" (CS)
- "We rotate keys every 90 days; need to know how live requests behave" (customer security)

Ending note (voice memo style)
---
This doc is intentionally terse and slightly messy — it's a working translation layer. If we want, I can convert the top 5 micro-probes to GH issues with RFC templates + tags next week. Also: consider adding a small kanban in Drive for 'ask -> probe -> ship' so CS can move items and each has an owner tagged.

-- Ava

title: Grounded Quote Skeptic: Prompt Suite + Regression Notes
preview:
Purpose:
This is a working prompt + test-case catalog for onboarding models into domain RAG flows where citation fidelity and "grounding" are critical. The focus is: when the user asks for a quote or a factual claim, the model should either (a) provide an exact quote that matches source content, with clear citation metadata, or (b) refuse/clarify when the source doesn't support the claim. We stress refusal clarity for ambiguous queries and partial citations (page/paragraph level when available).

Scope and constraints:
- Domain: regulated healthcare and developer docs (FAQs, API guides).
- Sources: short snippets (paragraphs, bullet lists) plus extended docs (5-10 pages).
- Models under test: open models and tuned variants (float16, int8/quantized) across hosted and private deployments.
- Evaluation modes: automated batch scoring + manual spot checks for quote fidelity.

High-level test categories (novel framing):
1) Exact-quote recovery (EQ): prompt asks for verbatim quote; reference exists verbatim in source. Expectation: model returns the quote exactly, quoting marks, plus a citation token that includes source id and character offsets when available.
2) Paraphrase-safe grounding (PSG): ask for summary/paraphrase of a passage but then request verbatim quote of a specific sentence. Model should indicate which parts are paraphrase vs verbatim and only quote exact text when asked.
3) Partial-source hallucination (PSH): question requires combining facts spread across sources. Ensure model does not fabricate a new quoted sentence — should synthesize but attribute to multiple sources or refuse quoting.
4) Skeptic refusal ladder (SRL): gradient of refusal responses based on signal strength (high-confidence match -> direct quote; low-confidence match -> clarify + refuse to quote; ambiguous evidence -> offer to search or cite probable sources with confidence score).
5) Misattribution challenge (MC): prompt that is likely to trigger a well-known misattribution (e.g., famous-sounding sentence not present). Verify that model does not attribute nonexistent quotations to sources.

Concrete test cases (short catalog, each test case here has: id | short prompt hint | expected behavior | quick score rubric):
- GQ-01 | "Give me the sentence from the API faq that defines rate limiting." | Expect verbatim sentence enclosed in quotes + citation to faq v2, section 3. Failure modes: paraphrase only, wrong section, no citation. Score: pass/fail plus edit distance measure.
- GQ-02 | "Summarize the deployment options and then quote the exact line that mentions 'VPC peering'" | Expect: summary (2-3 bullets) then a separate quoted line exactly matching source. If model paraphrases the quote -> partial fail.
- GQ-03 | "User asserts: 'Redwood guarantees token-level pricing stability' — find the sentence that proves or refutes." | Expect: either direct quoted sentence refuting the claim OR a refusal with explanation that no such sentence exists; flag hallucination if the model fabricates.
- GQ-04 | "Combine two short blog posts and provide a single supporting quote for 'continuous batching'" | Expect: synthesis with multi-source citation; refusal to provide single-source verbatim quote if none exists.
- GQ-05 | "Quote the privacy statement paragraph on audit logs from the enterprise doc v1.1" | Expect: verbatim multi-sentence block, citation, and source version. If model returns older/other version -> note as version mismatch.

Prompt templates (template-first approach, for automated harness):
- Template A (explicit quote demand): "Please return ONLY the verbatim text from the source that matches the query, wrapped in triple quotes, followed by a JSON blob with keys: source_id, start, end, confidence. If no exact match, return "NO_EXACT_MATCH"."
- Template B (summary + quote): "Provide a short summary (3 bullets) of the relevant text. After bullets, include any verbatim sentence(s) enclosed in single quotes and the source metadata for each. If the source does not contain any verbatim sentence that answers, write 'NO_VERBATIM'."
- Template C (refusal ladder): "When unsure, ask a clarification question. If the model cannot find a quote after 3 internal search attempts (simulate via retrieval passes), then respond: 'I cannot find a direct quote in the provided documents; would you like a paraphrase or a citation to related sections?'"

Evaluation metrics (practical, implementable):
- Exact Match Rate (EMR): fraction of test cases where verbatim quote equals gold reference (token-level exact).
- Citation Precision (CP): correctness of returned source id/version (string match).
- Citation Granularity (CG): does the citation include offsets/paragraph identifiers? score 0/1.
- Refusal Appropriateness (RA): measured on a 3-point scale (correct_refusal, partial_refusal, incorrect_refusal/hallucination).
- Edit Distance Score (EDS): normalized Levenshtein when not exact; helps triage near-misses.
- Combined fidelity score: weighted sum, weights tunable per-customer (default: EMR 0.5, CP 0.2, RA 0.2, CG 0.1).

Manual spotcheck rubric (for human reviewers):
- Reviewer checklist: did the model quote exactly? did it include citation? was citation correct version? did model hallucinate extra context? is the refusal phrasing explicit and helpful?
- Color tags used during review: green (pass), yellow (minor drift — paraphrase/offset mismatch), red (hallucination/misattribution).

Regression detection and alerting notes (practical ops):
- Baseline: take EMR and CP for current production model on the full canonical prompt suite.
- Regression triggers: drop in EMR > 5% absolute OR CP drop > 7% absolute within a weekly rolling window triggers PagerDuty for appling-metrics primary.
- Auto-run: nightly batch with seeded retrieval noise (simulate source deletion/different vector scores) to catch brittle quoting behavior introduced by retriever/model changes.
- Triaging: flagged failures produce small HTML summary + link to the raw model output + retrieval traces for debugging.

Instrumentation notes for engineers (practical implementation cues):
- When returning quotes, prefer structured metadata fields in the API response: {quote_text, source_id, offsets, version, retrieval_score, model_confidence}. (We intentionally request these in harness templates.)
- For quantized variants, add an additional column to nightly table: quantization_mode, sample EMR, CP.
- Make sure retriever logs include which chunk ids were considered; store top-10 chunk texts for each query in debug logs (rotating retention).

Open questions / TODOs (notes-to-self style):
- How to standardize 'confidence' for models that don't natively output calibrated scores? (Options: softmax proxy, logprob heuristics, or separate verifier model.)
- Should we accept near-exact matches (whitespace/punctuation differences) as EMR passes for customer X? Add per-customer tolerance config.
- Integrate a lightweight quote-verifier microservice that can do a deterministic substring / fuzzy match and return offsets; prototype exists in repo PR #88 but needs performance profiling.

Quick regression playbook (for on-call):
1) Confirm dataset and prompt templates unchanged. 2) Check retriever vector DB health and chunk availability. 3) Re-run failing tests interactively with top-k retrieval logging. 4) If model variant changed (new weights/quant), rollback to previous variant and re-run. 5) If failure persists across variants, escalate to applied-ml and serving-runtime.

Notes for reviewers: this doc is intentionally informal — it's a living catalog for prompt experimentation and early-stage onboarding checks. Use the linked PR and JIRA references when adding new tests. Add new test ids with GQ-XX numbering.

Examples / quick samples (for copy/paste into harness):
- Example 1 (EQ): 
  PROMPT: "From the enterprise-privacy-v1 doc, return the exact paragraph that describes audit log retention periods. Return the text and the offsets only."
  EXPECTED: """Audit logs are retained for a minimum of 365 days for enterprise plans.""", source: enterprise-privacy-v1, offsets: 1024-1101

- Example 2 (SRL):
  PROMPT: "A user claims the API guarantees a free tier for research. Find the sentence in our docs that supports or refutes this claim. If you cannot find one, say 'NO_SUPPORTING_SENTENCE' and ask whether to surface related policies."
  EXPECTED: 'NO_SUPPORTING_SENTENCE' (if none).

-- end of working draft --

Revision history (head):
- 2025-11-07: initial draft (Ava)
- 2025-11-21: added templates and early metrics (Ravi)
- 2026-02-15: added regression triggers and playbook (Monica)
- 2026-03-01: updated example prompts and linked PR (Diego)

Housekeeping: link this doc in CONFLUENCE-PI/grounding-playbook and reference it from the onboarding checklist for new models (internal doc id: onboarding-mdl-2026).

Unresolved: need to finalize pass/fail thresholds with product-hosted-api before marking as final.

TODO: convert Template A into a validated JSON schema for the harness responses (low priority).

The focus is: when the user asks for a quote or a factual claim, the model should either (a) provide an exact quote that matches source content, with clear citation metadata, or (b) refuse/clarify when the source doesn't support the claim. We stress refusal clarity for ambiguous queries and partial citations (page/paragraph level when available).

Scope and constraints:
- Domain: regulated healthcare and developer docs (FAQs, API guides).
- Sources: short snippets (paragraphs, bullet lists) plus extended docs (5-10 pages).
- Models under test: open models and tuned variants (float16, int8/quantized) across hosted and private deployments.
- Evaluation modes: automated batch scoring + manual spot checks for quote fidelity.

4) Skeptic refusal ladder (SRL): gradient of refusal responses based on signal strength (high-confidence match -> direct quote; low-confidence match -> clarify + refuse to quote; ambiguous evidence -> offer to search or cite probable sources with confidence score).

If the model cannot find a quote after 3 internal search attempts (simulate via retrieval passes), then respond: 'I cannot find a direct quote in the provided documents; would you like a paraphrase or a citation to related sections?'"

- Exact Match Rate (EMR): fraction of test cases where verbatim quote equals gold reference (token-level exact).

- Combined fidelity score: weighted sum, weights tunable per-customer (default: EMR 0.5, CP 0.2, RA 0.2, CG 0.1).

title: Field Ops Inference Launch Logbook — Customer Implementation Tracker
preview:
Purpose:
This working logbook is a lightweight, operational-first status tracker for customer inference rollouts where Field Ops owns day-to-day delivery. It is designed to accompany the formal implementation tracker and capture granular actions, daily standup notes, blockers and mitigation attempts. Use this during the 6-week launch window and archive a sanitized copy to Confluence after go‑live.

Scope:
- Target customers: Dedicated and Private deployment rollouts (VPCs, private clusters, reserved GPU pools)
- Phases covered: kickoff -> infra validation -> model onboarding -> canary -> traffic cutover -> stabilization
- Audience: Customer Success (Field Ops), Solutions Eng, Infra Eng, Customer SRE

How to use:
- Update entry each day with: status, owner, progress, new blockers, next actions
- Mark milestones as: Pending / In progress / Blocked / Complete
- When blocker persists >48h, add an Escalation line with required SLA and contact

High-level Milestone Log (single-line entries for quick scanning):
- Account kickoff: Owner=Noor Al-Khatib; Due=2025-11-12; Status=Complete; Notes=Kickoff slides + runbook shared
- Network validation (VPC peering + route checks): Owner=Marcus Patel; Due=2025-11-19; Status=Complete; Notes=VPN established, route table mismatch fixed by infra PR 189
- Certificate & KMS integration: Owner=Marcus Patel; Due=2025-11-25; Status=Complete; Notes=Customer KMS key rotated; test decrypt ok
- Infra provisioning (private cluster + NFS mounts): Owner=Diego Rivera; Due=2025-12-02; Status=Complete; Notes=Cluster autoscaler configured, storage class set
- Model pin + image build: Owner=Solutions Eng; Due=2025-12-09; Status=In progress; Notes=Quantized 4-bit variant built; pending perf benchmark
- Canary plan (1% steady traffic -> 5%): Owner=Ava Chen; Due=2025-12-18; Status=In progress; Notes=Canary hook ready; rollout gating metrics defined
- Latency SLO validation (P95 end-to-end): Owner=CS-SRE; Due=2025-12-20; Status=Blocked; Notes=Intermittent P95 spikes observed during batch jobs
- Traffic cutover: Owner=Customer PM; Due=2025-12-22; Status=Pending; Notes=Waiting on SLO clear
- Post-go-live quality window (14 days): Owner=CS; Due=2026-01-05; Status=Pending; Notes=Define quality evaluation prompts

Daily entries (most recent first):
2026-02-28 | Owner=Ava Chen | Status=In review | Progress=Consolidated QBR artifacts for renewal discussion; identified three outstanding minor regressions in reranking tests | Blockers=None | Next=Send pre-QBR note to customer with remediation plan
2026-02-24 | Owner=Diego Rivera | Status=In progress | Progress=Applied hotfix to batching orchestrator; reduced batch queuing under burst load on staging | Blockers=Regression test intermittently failing on sequence length >2048 | Next=Run isolated perf harness overnight
2026-02-20 | Owner=Marcus Patel | Status=Blocked | Progress=Investigating P95 spike; logs show GC pauses in node exporter container during heavy embedding jobs | Blockers=Need infra team to confirm JVM flags and restart policy (ticket INF-742) | Next=Escalate to infra on-call, request controlled restart window
2026-02-15 | Owner=Noor Al-Khatib | Status=Complete | Progress=Customer training session delivered (Ops + App teams); recorded video uploaded | Blockers=None | Next=Share playbook PDF and access to runbooks

Open blockers (actionable):
- P95 latency spike during large embedding jobs
  - Impact: Temporary QoS degradation for search product when embedding batches run concurrently
  - Current owner: Marcus Patel
  - Mitigation attempted: limit concurrent embedding jobs via job-queue (applied; partial improvement)
  - Next action: request infra restart window and JVM tuning; mitigation ETA 48h
  - Escalation: infra-oncall@redwood.ai (priority P1 if customer impact increases)

- Canary gating thresholds undefined for reranking quality drift
  - Impact: Risk of silent model regressions at scale
  - Current owner: Ava Chen
  - Mitigation attempted: manual spot checks on seed prompts; accuracy heuristics implemented
  - Next action: run automated eval suite and attach baseline ; ETA 72h

Risk and contingency notes (short):
- Risk: Capacity shortfall during holiday traffic spike -> Contingency: enable burst pool (dedicated tenant) + fallback to smaller quantized model variant
- Risk: Secret rotation failure during cutover -> Contingency: pre-roll credentials and test decryption 24h prior
- Risk: Customer legal requests additional logging -> Contingency: negotiate retention subset; enable masked logs for PII-safe tracing

Escalation and SLA expectations:
- If any Blocker remains unresolved >48 hours and causes production impact, file an escalation in Linear with tag #escalate-cs and notify on-call via Slack (channel: #cs-escalations)
- Target resolution windows: P0 (production down) = 4 hours; P1 (severe degradation) = 24–48 hours; P2 (degraded non-critical) = 72 hours

Hand-off checklist for go-live (short actionable list):
- Confirm telemetry hooks (per-route latency + token counts) are active and visible in Console
- Finalize rollout policy: canary percentages, automatic rollback thresholds, fallback models
- Pre-cutover load test with 10% expected peak for 2 hours (monitor P95, error-rate)
- Confirm support rotation and Escalation contacts are listed in incident runbook
- Customer sign-off recorded in Confluence page and attached to ticket

Notes for next QBR / renewal conversation:
- Bring a delta report: cost/unit before/after quantized deployment, token latency improvements, and top 3 operational incidents with mitigations
- Highlight ongoing opportunities: batching optimization, cache warmup strategies, and Optimize recommendations adoption

Attachments / artifacts to merge into final tracker:
- network-validation.log (raw)
- infra-provisioning pr-189 (link above)
- canary-gating.md (internal playbook)

Final remark:
This logbook is intentionally less formal than the official implementation tracker. It should surface day-to-day realities and be the single source of truth for Field Ops until the customer enters the post-go-live maintenance cadence. When migrating to the long-lived record, remove sensitive logs and redact PII.

title: Account health deck variants - standard & enterprise
preview:
WORKING DRAFT - Account health deck templates (Standard + Enterprise)
Owner: Ava Chen (CS)  — collaborators: Marcus, Riley, Samira
Last updated: 2026-02-28
Status: draft — intended to be a reusable deck and playbook for CS teams running QBR-style reviews and renewal readiness checks.

PURPOSE
- Create two lightweight deck variants that scale across mid-market (Standard) and enterprise customers.
- Provide an accompanying renewal readiness tracker and playbook so AEs/CSMs have an operational runbook after the deck presentation.
- Keep slides prescriptive but editable per-account.

HOW TO USE THIS FILE
- Copy the appropriate slide outline into Google Slides and populate with account-specific data prior to the meeting.
- Use the renewal tracker tab (separate sheet linked below) to synchronize actions and owners.
- For Enterprise customers, include security, compliance, and ROI deep-dive slides from the enterprise appendix.

LINKED ARTIFACTS / DATA SOURCES (notes)
- Confluence: /CS/qbr-framework-2.0 (canonical metrics + definitions)
- Jira: CS-412 (QBR deck automation workstream)
- Analytics query: account_monthly_tokens.sql (product analytics team) — ask infra for 7-day rolling latency distribution by route
- Example PR: github.com/redwood/renewal-templates/pull/34

STANDARD DECK OUTLINE (recommended length 25–30 min)
1. Title slide — Account name, CSM, AE, date, renewal month
   - Note: keep single-line value prop for context (what feature usage matters)
2. Agenda + Objectives — what success looks like for this meeting
3. Executive snapshot (1 slide) — health score, renewal status, ARR impact, NPS change
   - Sidebar: highlight 1–2 risks and 1–2 wins
4. Product usage trends (2 slides) — MAU/DAU-like metrics for API calls, per-route token volume, percent of heavy inference jobs
   - Callout: latency and error trends for the primary route (last 90 days)
5. Value delivered (1 slide) — top 3 customer outcomes attributable to Redwood (reduction in infra ops, cost savings vs self-hosted, improved latency percentiles)
6. Adoption map (1 slide) — features/modules used vs. available 
7. Support & incidents (1 slide) — open P1/P2s, MTTR, major incidents since last QBR, actions taken
8. Roadmap alignment (1 slide) — what Redwood is shipping that aligns to customer's top priorities
9. Risks & mitigation (1 slide) — capacity, security, quality regressions, integration fragility
10. Renewal readiness and asks (1 slide) — suggested renewal term, recommended committed capacity (if applicable), open procurement blockers
11. Action plan & owners (1 slide) — 30/60/90 day actions, owners, due dates
12. Appendix pointer — security contacts, billing cadence, technical deep-dive links

SPEAKER NOTES / TALK TRACKS (Standard)
- Exec snapshot: keep it business-focused: quantify downtime avoided, features adopted, and dollarized impact where possible.
- Product usage: call out any anomalous spikes and link to support ticket if relevant.
- Renewal ask: recommend a clear next step (PO timeline or follow-up meeting with procurement).

ENTERPRISE DECK OUTLINE (recommended length 40–60 min + appendix materials)
- Use the Standard deck as base; expand with the following enterprise slides. Aim to send the deck 72 hours before the meeting.
1. Title + Legal/Procurement contact table
2. Agenda + Desired outcomes
3. Executive summary (2 slides) — business impact, retention risk score, recommended commercial motion (renew, expand, restructure)
4. Detailed usage and cost breakdown (3 slides) — token-level cost by product, batch vs streaming usage, caching effectiveness metrics
5. Performance SLA review (2 slides) — 95/99 latency percentiles vs. SLA, uplift since last review
6. Security & compliance posture (2 slides) — KMS/HSM usage status, SOC2/ISO attestation, VPC/private deployment notes, outstanding audit items
7. Integration health (1–2 slides) — model variants in use, model drift signals, eval regression alerts tied to customer prompt sets
8. Architecture snapshot (1 slide) — how the customer’s workloads are routed (hosted / dedicated / private) and fallback policies in place
9. Financial summary (1–2 slides) — committed spend, overages, forecast scenarios (12 months)
10. Renewal proposal (1 slide) — recommended term/committed capacity, discounts, SLAs, migration or upgrade paths
11. Procurement & legal timeline (1 slide) — sign-off steps, POC requirements, required contract addenda
12. Risk register + mitigations (1 slide) — prioritized list with probability/impact and owners
13. Action plan & governance cadence (1 slide) — steering committee, quarterly checkpoints, escalation path
14. Appendix: security pack, benchmark reports, model evaluation artifacts, sample SLO dashboards

ENTERPRISE SPEAKER NOTES / TALK TRACKS
- Financial summary: present three scenarios (status quo, conservative growth, adoption-driven expansion). Use clear ARR math and reference billing audits.
- Security: if customer is in regulated industry, surface the compliance gaps and the remediation timeline. 
- Procurement: have legal/contract playbook excerpts ready — common SLAs and indemnity language.

RENEWAL READINESS TRACKER (single-sheet template columns)
- Account name
- CSM owner
- AE owner
- Renewal date (mm/yyyy)
- Current ARR / MRR
- Recommended motion (renew / expand / restructure / churn risk)
- Proposed committed capacity (if applicable)
- Primary blockers (procurement / legal / technical / exec alignment)
- Risk score (1–10)
- Open action items (comma-separated or link to ticket)
- Owner (action-level owner)
- Due date
- Status (not started / in progress / blocked / done)
- Last touch date
- Notes (free text)

TRACKER USAGE NOTES
- Keep tracker as living single source of truth. Share weekly with AE for accounts within 90 days to renewal.
- Color-code rows: red (>=8 risk), amber (5–7), green (<=4).
- Link each "Open action items" entry to a Jira ticket when possible.

PLAYBOOK: 90-DAY RENEWAL RUNWAY (high-level cadence)
- Day 90–60: Exec alignment + usage review (send deck, schedule meeting), identify blockers, open procurement tickets.
- Day 59–30: Commercial negotiations + legal redlines, technical validation of capacity/SLAs, ramp plan for new commitments.
- Day 29–0: Final sign-off, invoice prep, onboarding for new capacity, post-renewal success plan.

ROLES & ESCALATION
- CSM: owner of health, product adoption, meeting facilitation.
- AE: commercial owner, negotiates price/term.
- Solutions Eng: technical validation, migration plan.
- Escalation path: CSM -> CS Manager -> Head of CS -> CRO.

MEASUREMENT: WHAT TO TRACK
- Health score composition (example): usage (40%), support load (20%), SLA compliance (15%), adoption breadth (15%), NPS/CSAT (10%).
- Renewal success metrics: renewal rate, time-to-sign, negotiation delta (discounts given vs list), churn reasons taxonomy.

TEMPLATES & SNIPPETS (copyable talk tracks)
- Opening exec summary snippet: "Over the last 90 days, your teams used Redwood for X inference hours which correlates to Y% faster response times and an estimated $Z saved vs self-hosted alternatives. Our recommendation is to move to a [12/24]-month committed plan to lock in preferred pricing and reserved capacity."
- Risk acknowledgment: "We see two high-priority items: procurement timeline and latency spikes on route /api/generate. Proposed next steps: procurement sync on [date], solutions eng runbook to validate caching improvements by [date]."

NOTES TO SELF (internal)
- For enterprise deck, prep a one-pager for legal with proposed SLA language to avoid surprises.
- Add an automated script to pull the last 90 days of token usage and 95/99 latencies into the first two slides — ask analytics for access. (Jira: CS-412)
- Consider building a slide library in Slides with placeholders and a one-click populate script for the CSM chrome extension (long-term).

UNRESOLVED QUESTIONS / DECISIONS NEEDED
- Should we always recommend committed capacity for accounts using >X tokens/day? Proposed threshold: 250k tokens/day — need ops input.
- Standardize risk scoring rubric across CS org or leave local calibration? (Ops prefers standardization)

ATTACHMENTS / APPENDIX (to include in final deck)
- Example SLA snapshot (enterprise appendix)
- Recent benchmarking report for model latency improvements (internal link)
- Customer-specific eval results if available (link to evaluation artifacts)

NEXT STEPS
- Review with CS leadership (Ava + Samira) by 2026-03-05 — incorporate feedback.
- Create Google Slides master and a Google Sheet tracker template, link both in Confluence.
- Add automation ticket to CS-automation backlog for pre-populating the executive snapshot.

END OF DRAFT

title: Flashlab Quickmap: 30m SDK integration lab
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact, engineer-friendly lab note (Flashlab Quickmap) for a 30-minute path that gets a minimal Redwood SDK call integrated into an existing app, validates latency/error modes, and wires basic telemetry and fallbacks. Goal is to have a reproducible, low-friction sequence instructors and SEs can run during demos or workshops.

When to use:
- A short intro session for a new integrator (30–45m)
- Pre-sales demo when customer wants to try a minimal flow in their environment
- Internal sandbox validation before promoting a sample to the docs site

High-level flow (30m):
1) Prep (5m): create an API key, pick a sample app folder, set env var REDWOOD_API_KEY.
2) Install (3m): pip/npm install redwood-sdk-lite (or clone example repo).
3) Hello call (5m): run single request to generate text; confirm 200 and content.
4) Add telemetry (5m): emit latency, token count metric and an error counter.
5) Failure & fallback (6m): simulate quota/unavailable and switch to a cheaper local model or cached reply.
6) Wrap-up (6m): summarize next steps, link to longer guides, open issues.

Quick commands (copy-paste):
- set API key (bash):
  export REDWOOD_API_KEY=sk-test-REDACTED

- Python hello snippet (minimal):
  from redwood import RedwoodClient
  cli = RedwoodClient(api_key=os.environ['REDWOOD_API_KEY'])
  r = cli.generate(model='redwood-1-small', prompt='Summarize: why versioned models help')
  print(r.text)

- Node hello snippet (minimal):
  import { RedwoodClient } from 'redwood-sdk'
  const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY })
  const r = await client.generate({ model: 'redwood-1-small', prompt: 'Bulleted list: costs to measure for inference' })
  console.log(r.text)

Telemetry quick-wire (approx):
- Emit: inference.latency_ms, inference.tokens_in, inference.tokens_out, inference.error_count
- Tag with: team, route_name, model_version
- Suggested retention for lab: 7d

Failure & fallback patterns (lab-friendly):
- Simulate 429: reduce concurrency and fall back to cached response, record a rollback event.
- Simulate 503: route to smaller model (policy: redwood-1-small -> redwood-1-micro) and mark as degraded response.
- Simulate slow tail: enforce timeout at 1.5x SLA and send probe to SRE channel.

Checklist for the instructor (pre-lab):
- Create a throwaway API key and place in test env.
- Ensure sample app has a simple metric sink (console or prometheus devbox).
- Branch with minimal sample code in repo; PR link in linked_artifacts.
- Prepare one fallback cached response for the demo.

Acceptance criteria (end of 30m):
- Hello call returns valid model output locally.
- Telemetry shows one latency sample and token counts.
- Fallback triggered once in simulated failure and logs an event.
- Post-lab notes added to the repo README with next steps.

Longer doc hooks (what to add next):
- Expand snippets to include streaming and function-calling examples.
- Add a small CI smoke test that runs the hello snippet daily against staging to detect regressions.
- Add a content-quality regression check (prompt + expected anchors) to Redwood Optimize integration.

Open questions / trade-offs to capture:
- Should the lab use real hosted model IDs or pinned dockerized private runtime images? (Tradeoff: realism vs reproducibility offline.)
- How opinionated should fallback rules be in the example? (Keep simple for labs; complex routing in production.)
- Minimum telemetry schema that is useful but not overwhelming for first-timers.

Notes-to-self (instructor):
- Keep wording concrete, avoid infra jargon.
- Timebox exercises strictly; skip deeper debugging unless asked.
- Capture customer pain points during demos and add to "lab pain" section in GH issues.

Follow-ups (owners / next tasks):
- Marcus: create PR for sample repo with node+python labs (due 2026-03-01).
- Priya: draft short video (3–4m) showing the 6-step flow (due 2026-03-10).
- Diego: add daily smoke job in staging that runs hello snippet and reports to console (due 2026-03-05).

Appendix: minimal test cases to include in sample repo:
- Happy path: single generate returns non-empty text.
- Latency assertion: median < 300ms for local dev region.
- Fallback assertion: when API returns 503, client uses fallback cache and returns cached text.
- Telemetry assertion: one event with keys model_version and tokens_out.

End of Flashlab Quickmap.

title: Tool selection & arg-flow eval playbook (draft)
preview:
Summary:
This working playbook captures the evaluation suite and triage process for tool-use / function-calling behaviors for onboarding models. Focus areas: tool selection accuracy, argument parsing correctness (types, units, edge values), and multi-step flows where the model must call multiple tools in sequence or call tools conditionally.

Intended audience:
- Applied ML engineers integrating new model variants into hosted/dedicated/private runs
- QA and SRE teams responsible for regression alerts
- Solutions engineering for customer-facing tool connectors

Goals:
- Provide reproducible prompt sets that exercise selection and argument extraction across typical and adversarial cases.
- Define pass/fail criteria and automated checks for CI regression gates.
- Document triage steps for systematic root-cause identification (model, prompt, runtime mapping, connector bug).

Scope and constraints:
- Suite assumes models expose function-calling capability with JSON-like function schema.
- Tests lean on our function schema conventions (name, args schema (types/required), descriptions).
- Not covering RLHF-specific hallucination mitigation here; separate eval covers that.

Test matrix (summary):
- Tool selection: single-shot selection among N candidate tools (N=3,5,8 across tests).
- Arg extraction: correct keys, types, units, canonicalization (e.g., dates normalized to ISO).
- Optional/missing args: handle defaults, nulls, and explicit "omit" tokens.
- Multi-step flows: ordered calls (A->B->C), conditional branching, loop-like re-entry (retry semantics).
- Edge cases: conflicting tool descriptions, ambiguous user intent, multi-intent prompts.

Prompts and templates (examples):
1) Simple tool selection
- Prompt: "Book me a flight to SFO next Friday morning under $350 -- if you can, also add a crash-avoidance alert plugin."
- Expected behavior: Select tool: book_flight; args: {destination: "SFO", date: ISO-date-for-next-Friday, time_window: "morning", price_ceiling: 350}; Do not select crash-alert plugin unless user explicitly asked for integration setup.

2) Ambiguous selection -> prefer cheaper compatible tool
- Prompt: "Summarize this document and give me an action item list; also, if summarizer can export to markdown, do that. Otherwise, just paste the bullets."
- Expected behavior: Primary selection: text_summarizer. Secondary: markdown_export (only if available). If exported markdown tool absent, produce inline formatted list.

3) Arg parsing with units and free text
- Prompt: "Set thermostat to 72 degrees, but make sure it's Celsius if I'm in Europe."
- Expected parsing: {temperature: 72, unit: "C", region_hint: "EU"} and a clarifying follow-up only if region ambiguous.

4) Multi-step conditional flow (happy path)
- Prompt: "Find the cheapest round-trip to LHR from SFO in June and if the fare is under $800, reserve refundable option; else, just send best options."
- Expected sequence: (1) call flight_search(args), receive results; (2) if min_price < 800 -> call reserve_flight(ticket_id, refundable=true); else -> call send_options(email=user_email).

5) Retry semantics and partial failures
- Prompt: "Upload this file to the customer's S3 bucket and return the URL."
- Expected path: call upload_to_s3(args) -> if upload returns transient error (5xx) -> retry up to 3x with backoff and report final status; if permanent failure, call create-ticket(tool) or return actionable error object.

Concrete test cases (small representative selection):
- TC-TS-01: three-tool disambiguation, overlapping verbs (book_hotel, book_travel_package, book_flight). Assert top-1 selection match to ground truth intent in 950/1000 samples.
- TC-AP-05: numeric arg extraction for currency with commas and symbols ("$1,200.00"). Assert normalized value equals 1200.0 and currency USD.
- TC-MS-02: branch decision correctness in multi-step flow with mocked downstream tool outputs. Assert correct call-count and ordering.

Ground truth and labeling rules:
- Ground truth is represented as a single-line canonical function call string per example e.g. book_flight(destination=\"LHR\", date=\"2026-06-12\", refundable=true)
- Labels include: selected_tool (single), alternates (ranked list), parsed_args (canonical JSON string), call_sequence (ordered list of function names), clarifying_questions (if any).
- Annotator guidance: prefer minimal clarifying questions; ask only when required for safe execution (missing required arg or ambiguous critical arg like destination).

Metrics and thresholds (initial):
- Tool selection accuracy (top-1): target >= 0.93 on production-like test set.
- Tool selection calibrated recall (where multiple tools valid): rank-based recall@3 >= 0.97.
- Arg extraction F1: >= 0.92 for required args; >0.85 for optional/enums.
- End-to-end workflow success (correct sequence + correct args across calls): >= 0.88 for critical workflows.
- Latency-sensitive pass: tests that exercise streaming/tool-prefetching must not degrade 95th percentile token latency by >15% vs baseline.

Regression detection rules (CI + alerting):
- Daily batch run of the stable eval set (N=3k mixed examples) against the production model and any candidate model; compute delta on primary metrics.
- Fail gate: top-1 tool selection drops >2 percentage points AND arg-F1 drops >1 percentage point -> fail.
- Alert-only: single metric drop >3pp but others stable -> create ticket for manual review.
- Statistical significance check: run bootstrap (n=2000) for deltas; only trigger fail gate if p < 0.05 and effect size > threshold.

CI integration notes:
- Tests live in infra repo under /tests/tool-evals. Test runner must spin up a sandbox for connectors with mocked responses.
- Use deterministic seeds for sampling and any nondeterministic steps.
- Output artifacts: per-run CSV with per-example predictions, aggregated metrics JSON, and failure samples/trace logs. Store artifacts in S3 bucket: s3://redwood-evals/tool-calling/<model>-<date>/

Failure triage playbook:
1) Reproduce locally with same model/config and seed.
2) Inspect per-example trace: prompt -> token stream -> function-call decision logits -> function args tokens -> parsed args.
3) Check if failure is due to: prompt phrasing (quick fix: augment prompt set), model behavior (escalate to model onboarding, adjust temperature/penalties), or runtime mapping (schema mismatch, connector bug).
4) If model-related, open ENG-ML ticket (label: tool-call-regression) with representative failing examples and trace.
5) If runtime/connector: open ENG-Infra ticket, attach connector logs.

Labeling toolkit and instructions:
- Use the in-house annotator UI (link in linked_artifacts) with built-in validation for arg types.
- Labelers must mark whether a clarifying question should have been asked; examples provided in labeling guide.

Adversarial and fuzz tests:
- Auto-generate examples with mixed languages, code snippets, and emoji to ensure robust arg parsing.
- Corpus: 10k synthetically-generated prompts with numeric shenanigans, nested parentheticals, and contradictory statements.

Open questions / TODOs:
- How to standardize unit-canonicalization across connectors (e.g., timezones: user-local vs flight-local). Proposal: canonicalize to UTC + location hint field.
- Should we add a lightweight symbolic verifier that validates args against connector preconditions before calling? (pro: avoid catastrophic calls; con: extra latency).
- Release plan for gating Dedicated vs Hosted: evaluate whether reg thresholds should be stricter for Dedicated SLA-bound customers.

Next steps (short-term):
- Finish annotator guide and export a 3k-strong baseline eval set (owner: Marcus, due 2026-01-10).
- Wire CI runner and S3 artifact upload (owner: Sofia, due 2026-01-24).
- Run baseline evaluations across top 4 candidate model families and collect deltas (owner: Ava, due 2026-02-05).

Appendix: sample labeled record (single-line canonical)
- prompt_id: TC-MS-02-145
- prompt_text: "Find round-trips SFO->LHR June 10-17, if under $800 reserve refundable else email options"
- ground_truth_call_sequence: "flight_search -> reserve_flight -> notify_user"
- ground_truth_primary_call: "flight_search(params={\"origin\":\"SFO\",\"destination\":\"LHR\",\"depart\":\"2026-06-10\",\"return\":\"2026-06-17\"})"
- annotator_notes: "Require exact dates; refundable flag only if reserve step invoked and price < threshold"

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally prescriptive to make CI and triage deterministic. Expect iterations.
- Please add any missing edge-cases you hit in customer bug reports (tag them to the ticket references above).

-- end --
text:
Summary:
This working playbook captures the evaluation suite and triage process for tool-use / function-calling behaviors for onboarding models. Focus areas: tool selection accuracy, argument parsing correctness (types, units, edge values), and multi-step flows where the model must call multiple tools in sequence or call tools conditionally.

Intended audience:
- Applied ML engineers integrating new model variants into hosted/dedicated/private runs
- QA and SRE teams responsible for regression alerts
- Solutions engineering for customer-facing tool connectors

Goals:
- Provide reproducible prompt sets that exercise selection and argument extraction across typical and adversarial cases.
- Define pass/fail criteria and automated checks for CI regression gates.
- Document triage steps for systematic root-cause identification (model, prompt, runtime mapping, connector bug).

Scope and constraints:
- Suite assumes models expose function-calling capability with JSON-like function schema.
- Tests lean on our function schema conventions (name, args schema (types/required), descriptions).
- Not covering RLHF-specific hallucination mitigation here; separate eval covers that.

Test matrix (summary):
- Tool selection: single-shot selection among N candidate tools (N=3,5,8 across tests).
- Arg extraction: correct keys, types, units, canonicalization (e.g., dates normalized to ISO).
- Optional/missing args: handle defaults, nulls, and explicit "omit" tokens.
- Multi-step flows: ordered calls (A->B->C), conditional branching, loop-like re-entry (retry semantics).
- Edge cases: conflicting tool descriptions, ambiguous user intent, multi-intent prompts.

Prompts and templates (examples):
1) Simple tool selection
- Prompt: "Book me a flight to SFO next Friday morning under $350 -- if you can, also add a crash-avoidance alert plugin."
- Expected behavior: Select tool: book_flight; args: {destination: "SFO", date: ISO-date-for-next-Friday, time_window: "morning", price_ceiling: 350}; Do not select crash-alert plugin unless user explicitly asked for integration setup.

2) Ambiguous selection -> prefer cheaper compatible tool
- Prompt: "Summarize this document and give me an action item list; also, if summarizer can export to markdown, do that. Otherwise, just paste the bullets."
- Expected behavior: Primary selection: text_summarizer. Secondary: markdown_export (only if available). If exported markdown tool absent, produce inline formatted list.

3) Arg parsing with units and free text
- Prompt: "Set thermostat to 72 degrees, but make sure it's Celsius if I'm in Europe."
- Expected parsing: {temperature: 72, unit: "C", region_hint: "EU"} and a clarifying follow-up only if region ambiguous.

4) Multi-step conditional flow (happy path)
- Prompt: "Find the cheapest round-trip to LHR from SFO in June and if the fare is under $800, reserve refundable option; else, just send best options."
- Expected sequence: (1) call flight_search(args), receive results; (2) if min_price < 800 -> call reserve_flight(ticket_id, refundable=true); else -> call send_options(email=user_email).

5) Retry semantics and partial failures
- Prompt: "Upload this file to the customer's S3 bucket and return the URL."
- Expected path: call upload_to_s3(args) -> if upload returns transient error (5xx) -> retry up to 3x with backoff and report final status; if permanent failure, call create-ticket(tool) or return actionable error object.

Concrete test cases (small representative selection):
- TC-TS-01: three-tool disambiguation, overlapping verbs (book_hotel, book_travel_package, book_flight). Assert top-1 selection match to ground truth intent in 950/1000 samples.
- TC-AP-05: numeric arg extraction for currency with commas and symbols ("$1,200.00"). Assert normalized value equals 1200.0 and currency USD.
- TC-MS-02: branch decision correctness in multi-step flow with mocked downstream tool outputs. Assert correct call-count and ordering.

Ground truth and labeling rules:
- Ground truth is represented as a single-line canonical function call string per example e.g. book_flight(destination=\"LHR\", date=\"2026-06-12\", refundable=true)
- Labels include: selected_tool (single), alternates (ranked list), parsed_args (canonical JSON string), call_sequence (ordered list of function names), clarifying_questions (if any).
- Annotator guidance: prefer minimal clarifying questions; ask only when required for safe execution (missing required arg or ambiguous critical arg like destination).

Metrics and thresholds (initial):
- Tool selection accuracy (top-1): target >= 0.93 on production-like test set.
- Tool selection calibrated recall (where multiple tools valid): rank-based recall@3 >= 0.97.
- Arg extraction F1: >= 0.92 for required args; >0.85 for optional/enums.
- End-to-end workflow success (correct sequence + correct args across calls): >= 0.88 for critical workflows.
- Latency-sensitive pass: tests that exercise streaming/tool-prefetching must not degrade 95th percentile token latency by >15% vs baseline.

Regression detection rules (CI + alerting):
- Daily batch run of the stable eval set (N=3k mixed examples) against the production model and any candidate model; compute delta on primary metrics.
- Fail gate: top-1 tool selection drops >2 percentage points AND arg-F1 drops >1 percentage point -> fail.
- Alert-only: single metric drop >3pp but others stable -> create ticket for manual review.
- Statistical significance check: run bootstrap (n=2000) for deltas; only trigger fail gate if p < 0.05 and effect size > threshold.

CI integration notes:
- Tests live in infra repo under /tests/tool-evals. Test runner must spin up a sandbox for connectors with mocked responses.
- Use deterministic seeds for sampling and any nondeterministic steps.
- Output artifacts: per-run CSV with per-example predictions, aggregated metrics JSON, and failure samples/trace logs. Store artifacts in S3 bucket: s3://redwood-evals/tool-calling/<model>-<date>/

Failure triage playbook:
1) Reproduce locally with same model/config and seed.
2) Inspect per-example trace: prompt -> token stream -> function-call decision logits -> function args tokens -> parsed args.
3) Check if failure is due to: prompt phrasing (quick fix: augment prompt set), model behavior (escalate to model onboarding, adjust temperature/penalties), or runtime mapping (schema mismatch, connector bug).
4) If model-related, open ENG-ML ticket (label: tool-call-regression) with representative failing examples and trace.
5) If runtime/connector: open ENG-Infra ticket, attach connector logs.

Labeling toolkit and instructions:
- Use the in-house annotator UI (link in linked_artifacts) with built-in validation for arg types.
- Labelers must mark whether a clarifying question should have been asked; examples provided in labeling guide.

Adversarial and fuzz tests:
- Auto-generate examples with mixed languages, code snippets, and emoji to ensure robust arg parsing.
- Corpus: 10k synthetically-generated prompts with numeric shenanigans, nested parentheticals, and contradictory statements.

Open questions / TODOs:
- How to standardize unit-canonicalization across connectors (e.g., timezones: user-local vs flight-local). Proposal: canonicalize to UTC + location hint field.
- Should we add a lightweight symbolic verifier that validates args against connector preconditions before calling? (pro: avoid catastrophic calls; con: extra latency).
- Release plan for gating Dedicated vs Hosted: evaluate whether reg thresholds should be stricter for Dedicated SLA-bound customers.

Next steps (short-term):
- Finish annotator guide and export a 3k-strong baseline eval set (owner: Marcus, due 2026-01-10).
- Wire CI runner and S3 artifact upload (owner: Sofia, due 2026-01-24).
- Run baseline evaluations across top 4 candidate model families and collect deltas (owner: Ava, due 2026-02-05).

Appendix: sample labeled record (single-line canonical)
- prompt_id: TC-MS-02-145
- prompt_text: "Find round-trips SFO->LHR June 10-17, if under $800 reserve refundable else email options"
- ground_truth_call_sequence: "flight_search -> reserve_flight -> notify_user"
- ground_truth_primary_call: "flight_search(params={\"origin\":\"SFO\",\"destination\":\"LHR\",\"depart\":\"2026-06-10\",\"return\":\"2026-06-17\"})"
- annotator_notes: "Require exact dates; refundable flag only if reserve step invoked and price < threshold"

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally prescriptive to make CI and triage deterministic. Expect iterations.
- Please add any missing edge-cases you hit in customer bug reports (tag them to the ticket references above).

-- end --

title: Quickwire onramp: developer-first value props & pricing experiments
preview:
Purpose:
Short working brief for a sprint of small, testable messaging + pricing experiments aimed at accelerating developer activation on the Hosted API. Focus: clear developer-first framing, a 10-minute quickstart story, and two lightweight price-message experiments to reduce friction for trial integrations.

Executive hypothesis:
If we lead with measurable developer outcomes (time-to-first-infer, predictable latency, and quota safe-nets) and give a low-friction pricing 'first-hour' guarantee, we increase API-to-first-PoC conversion by >20% for PLG signups.

Target audiences:
- Indie devs / solo founders evaluating prototypes
- Growth-stage product engineers implementing an in-app assistant
- Platform/infra engineers validating latency/cost before procurement

Core developer-first value props (rapid short lines for UI and landing pages):
- "Working LLM in 10 minutes: copy-paste SDK, run example, see tokens and latency."
- "Predictable pricing you can understand: per-response estimates + first-hour free cap."
- "Ship without surprises: latency SLOs and a fall-back model automatically enabled on overload."
- "Same API from dev laptop to private cloud — swap models without code changes."

Quickstart narrative (for docs, quickstart card, and 1-click sandbox):
1) Sign up, create an API key, and open the Quickwire sample (button: "Run in Playground").
2) Paste the one-line SDK call and send a test prompt (we show an instant visual of token count + round-trip ms).
3) Toggle model quality slider (fast/standard/precise) and show cost delta in-line.
4) Click "Export to project" to copy sample client code and environment var snippet.

Developer UX microcopy samples (for onboarding tooltips):
- On sign-up modal: "Your first 60 minutes are on us — build freely and measure latency in the dashboard."
- On cost preview: "Estimated cost for this request: ~0.03 credits (based on tokens + quality)."
- On rate-limit hit: "Traffic spike detected — switching to a compatible fallback model to keep responses flowing."

Two pricing-language experiments (A/B):
A) "First-hour free + transparent estimate": advertise "First 60 minutes free" on landing; in console show per-request estimate pre-send. Aim: reduce hesitation to try non-trivial prompts.
B) "Starter bucket + soft-guard": give a $10 starter credit but show a demo meter that predicts how many typical responses that covers and warns when nearing exhaustion. Aim: reduce surprise over token throughput.
Metrics to watch: time-to-first-successful-infer (TTFSI), percent of signups that run >=1 nontrivial prompt, retention at 7 days, usage depth (avg requests/day), and billing opt-in rate after trial.

Competitive differentiators we should call out (notes from quick scan of competitors):
- Competitor A: emphasizes free-tier usage but hides latency guarantees and has frequent throttling. Message: "We give you transparent latency SLOs and automatic fallbacks so your dev demo keeps working."
- Competitor B: low price-per-token but complex model compatibility matrix. Message: "One API, consistent model signatures, swap versions without rework."
- Competitor C: enterprise-only private deployments (slow onboarding). Message: "Hosted for rapid iteration, upgrade to Dedicated/Private when you need predictability and compliance."

Quick talking points for SDR/demo kickoff (one-liners):
- "If it works on your laptop it scales to Dedicated or Private with no code changes — perfect for prototypes that need to graduate to production."
- "We quantify unit economics up front — see token, latency, and cost estimates before you commit."
- "Built-in fallback keeps demos alive when demand spikes."

Proof points we can show in the demo/landing page:
- Benchmarked median p95 latency for small (2–8 token prompts) and large (100–200 token) usage buckets.
- Case study snippet: "Customer X reduced infrastructure cost by 38% after adopting Redwood Optimize suggestions for batching and quantization." (link to reference)
- Replaceable model line: example showing same code call with model=rbw-large vs model=rbw-small and cost/latency side-by-side.

Safety rails & operations copy (to build trust):
- "First-hour free" includes a cap (e.g., 2,000 requests) to prevent abuse; show cap mechanics in UI.
- Clear refund/credit policy language for platform abuse or billing errors.
- Mention privacy: no training-on-customer-data for hosted by default; link to data processing terms.

Experiment plan (2-week sprint):
Week 0: align with docs + SDK owners, create Quickwire sample and sandbox button.
Week 1: launch pricing experiment A on landing page + console microcopy; run cohort A/B for new signups.
Week 2: analyze TTFSI + activation funnel; iterate copy and enable experiment B for next cohort.
Success criteria: 20% lift in TTFSI conversion and 10% lift in subsequent-day usage.

Open questions / TODOs:
- UX: should we show a live cost estimate that updates as you type a prompt? (Engineering effort: medium)
- Legal: confirm acceptable language for "first-hour free" vs "starter credit" for billing/regulatory.
- Product: what are exact SLO numbers we can publicly claim for hosted (p95 latency)? Need SRE sign-off.

Appendix: sample inline SDK snippet (for quickstart card)
- Node pseudo: 
  import Redwood from '@redwood/inference'
  const client = new Redwood({ key: process.env.RW_KEY })
  const res = await client.generate({ model: 'rbw-small', prompt: 'Summarize this text in 1 sentence' })
  console.log(res.text, res.metrics.tokens, res.metrics.latency_ms)

Note: this doc is intentionally conversational and experiment-focused. Use it as a source of microcopy drafts, experiment hypotheses, and quickstart flow sketches. Replace placeholders with final numbers and legal-approved phrasing before any public-facing usage.

Last rapid ideas (for later follow-up):
- Add a "cost slider" in the playground so devs can visually trade latency/quality/cost and export the config.
- A one-click demo recording that captures a 30s walkthrough — useful for sales and help center.

title: Starter XP pathways: mini-course outline and lab checklist
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact, modular mini-course for new SDK integrators (internal & external) that fits into a 60–90 minute onboarding slot or can be broken into 6x10–15min micro-labs. The aim is pragmatic: get an engineer from 'never called Redwood' -> 'shipping an instrumented POC route' with clear checkpoints, smoke tests and an annotated checklist for common friction points.

Audience:
- Product engineers building features with Redwood API or SDKs
- Integrators in platform teams onboarding their product teams
- Solutions engineers delivering short demos to prospects

Format and constraints:
- Target total time: 60–90 min (or split into 6 micro-labs).
- Deliverables: code skeleton (multi-lang), terminal walkthrough, prompt set, telemetry checklist, one-pager for troubleshooting.
- Assumes basic familiarity with REST/HTTP, Node/Python simple projects.
- Minimum infra: local dev machine + Redwood sandbox token.

High-level learning objectives:
1) Make the first request: understand auth, model selection, and minimal request/response loop.
2) Observe and interpret telemetry: latency, token counts, error surfaces.
3) Add safe fallbacks: timeouts, retries, model fallback.
4) Mini-eval: build a prompt set and run 10 curated queries to verify behaviour.
5) Ship an instrumented route that emits cost/latency telemetry and a regression check.

Module breakdown (micro-labs):
1) Quickconnect (10 min) — First call and anatomy
  - Goal: obtain token, run example request, parse response.
  - Steps: set REDWOOD_TOKEN, run `curl` minimal example, inspect headers for request-id.
  - Checkpoint: response 200 + request-id in headers.

2) SDK wiring (15 min) — Add SDK to tiny service
  - Goal: add official SDK (Node or Python) and replace curl with SDK client.
  - Steps: `npm init` + `npm i @redwood/inference` (or `pip install redwood-inference`), paste minimal client sample, call chat/text endpoint with small prompt.
  - Checklist: client constructed with region fallback; instrumentation hooks attached (span/request-id).

3) Telemetry quickwins (10–15 min) — Cost & latency visibility
  - Goal: emit simple metrics: tokens_per_call, latency_ms, status_code.
  - Implementation notes: SDK middleware can call into prometheus/statsd; include a sample exported JSON line in logs.
  - Smoke assert: average latency < 600ms in sandbox on sample prompt set (note: sandbox varies).

4) Failure modes & fallbacks (10 min) — Resilience patterns
  - Patterns: short client timeout + background retry with fallback model (cheaper variant), circuit-breaker for 5xx spikes, graceful degrade to cached answer for repeated prompts.
  - Example: pseudocode for fallback sequence included in appendix.

5) Micro-eval & prompt checklist (10–15 min)
  - Goal: create 10 prompts that represent 3 core product intents and 2 adversarial edge cases.
  - Run: bulk-run locally or via script, capture outputs and basic metrics (avg tokens, hallucination flag manual review).
  - Gate: 90% of core intent responses must include the explicit slot value (app-specific).

6) Ship + rollouts (10 min) — Canary & gating
  - Goal: outline a minimal canary: 1% traffic routed to new route, synthetic traffic and regression alerts set up.
  - Checklist: canary route exists, synthetic prompt runbook, alert thresholds (latency >= p95 + regeneration, hallucination-rate > X).

Annotated lab checklist (copyable):
- [ ] REDWOOD_TOKEN present in env
- [ ] Minimal request completes (200)
- [ ] Client instrumentation attached (request-id, span)
- [ ] Tokens and latency logged per-call
- [ ] Timeout + fallback model configured
- [ ] 10-prompt micro-eval run and results recorded
- [ ] Canary route and synthetic test configured

Smoke tests (quick commands):
- curl sample: `curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_TOKEN" -d '{"input": "Say hi"}' https://api.redwood.dev/v1/text`
- Node tiny call (pseudo):
  const Redwood = require('@redwood/inference');
  const client = new Redwood.Client({token: process.env.REDWOOD_TOKEN});
  const r = await client.text.create({model:'rw-small', input:'Summarize: ...'});
  console.log(r);

Telemetry example log line (one-per-call JSON):
{"ts":"2026-02-20T15:42:01Z","service":"payments-api","route":"/note-gen","request_id":"req_ABC123","model":"rw-small","tokens_in":24,"tokens_out":86,"latency_ms":240,"status":200}

Common friction notes (observed in trials):
- Developers forget region fallback leading to 30s DNS timeouts in certain clouds. Add region prioritized list in quickstart.
- Token accounting surprises: sample prompts with long examples blow up tokens; include tip to trim system messages and use few-shot templates.
- Overreliance on sandbox perf: emphasize variability and include a short note on interpreting latency diffs vs prod.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Decide canonical sample repo name and single-source-of-truth for code snippets (Jordan to propose repo naming).
- Should we include a local mock server wrapper? (Samir: prototype 1-day, useful for offline demos)
- Metric thresholds: what is acceptable hallucination rate per product vertical? (ask product PMs)

Appendix: fallback pseudocode (very small)
- try call primary model with 700ms timeout
- if timeout or 5xx -> call cheaper fallback `rw-small-quant` with 1200ms timeout
- if fallback fails -> return cached sentinel message + schedule background reconcile

Quick copy for solutions demos (1-paragraph):
"Minute 0–3: install SDK and run Hello. Minute 4–10: wire telemetry and run the 10-prompt micro-eval. Minute 10–20: add simple fallback & canary. Result: route instrumented with token/latency logs and a regression check that blocks rollout if core-intent accuracy dips"

Notes to self: keep the first-run as frictionless as possible — one terminal command that creates a tiny express/fastapi app skeleton would be ideal. Consider shipping CLI `redwood starter` that scaffolds labs and injects REDWOOD_TOKEN placeholder.

Revision history:
- 2025-11-04 initial sketch (Ava)
- 2026-01-12 added telemetry examples and checklist (Jordan)
- 2026-02-20 reorganized modules for 60-min flow and added pseudocode fallback (Ava)

End of doc.
