4) Failure modes & fallbacks (10 min) — Resilience patterns
  - Patterns: short client timeout + background retry with fallback model (cheaper variant), circuit-breaker for 5xx spikes, graceful degrade to cached answer for repeated prompts.
  - Example: pseudocode for fallback sequence included in appendix.

5) Micro-eval & prompt checklist (10–15 min)
  - Goal: create 10 prompts that represent 3 core product intents and 2 adversarial edge cases.
  - Run: bulk-run locally or via script, capture outputs and basic metrics (avg tokens, hallucination flag manual review).
  - Gate: 90% of core intent responses must include the explicit slot value (app-specific).

6) Ship + rollouts (10 min) — Canary & gating
  - Goal: outline a minimal canary: 1% traffic routed to new route, synthetic traffic and regression alerts set up.
  - Checklist: canary route exists, synthetic prompt runbook, alert thresholds (latency >= p95 + regeneration, hallucination-rate > X).

Annotated lab checklist (copyable):
- [ ] REDWOOD_TOKEN present in env
- [ ] Minimal request completes (200)
- [ ] Client instrumentation attached (request-id, span)
- [ ] Tokens and latency logged per-call
- [ ] Timeout + fallback model configured
- [ ] 10-prompt micro-eval run and results recorded
- [ ] Canary route and synthetic test configured

Smoke tests (quick commands):
- curl sample: `curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_TOKEN" -d '{"input": "Say hi"}' https://api.redwood.dev/v1/text`
- Node tiny call (pseudo):
  const Redwood = require('@redwood/inference');
  const client = new Redwood.Client({token: process.env.REDWOOD_TOKEN});
  const r = await client.text.create({model:'rw-small', input:'Summarize: ...'});
  console.log(r);

Telemetry example log line (one-per-call JSON):
{"ts":"2026-02-20T15:42:01Z","service":"payments-api","route":"/note-gen","request_id":"req_ABC123","model":"rw-small","tokens_in":24,"tokens_out":86,"latency_ms":240,"status":200}

Common friction notes (observed in trials):
- Developers forget region fallback leading to 30s DNS timeouts in certain clouds. Add region prioritized list in quickstart.
- Token accounting surprises: sample prompts with long examples blow up tokens; include tip to trim system messages and use few-shot templates.
- Overreliance on sandbox perf: emphasize variability and include a short note on interpreting latency diffs vs prod.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Decide canonical sample repo name and single-source-of-truth for code snippets (Jordan to propose repo naming).
- Should we include a local mock server wrapper? (Samir: prototype 1-day, useful for offline demos)
- Metric thresholds: what is acceptable hallucination rate per product vertical? (ask product PMs)

Appendix: fallback pseudocode (very small)
- try call primary model with 700ms timeout
- if timeout or 5xx -> call cheaper fallback `rw-small-quant` with 1200ms timeout
- if fallback fails -> return cached sentinel message + schedule background reconcile

Quick copy for solutions demos (1-paragraph):
"Minute 0–3: install SDK and run Hello. Minute 4–10: wire telemetry and run the 10-prompt micro-eval. Minute 10–20: add simple fallback & canary. Result: route instrumented with token/latency logs and a regression check that blocks rollout if core-intent accuracy dips"

Notes to self: keep the first-run as frictionless as possible — one terminal command that creates a tiny express/fastapi app skeleton would be ideal. Consider shipping CLI `redwood starter` that scaffolds labs and injects REDWOOD_TOKEN placeholder.

Revision history:
- 2025-11-04 initial sketch (Ava)
- 2026-01-12 added telemetry examples and checklist (Jordan)
- 2026-02-20 reorganized modules for 60-min flow and added pseudocode fallback (Ava)

End of doc.

title: Third-Party Audit Scope & Evidence Checklist — Template
preview:
Purpose:
This template standardizes requests to third-party vendors when Redwood Inference needs to perform security, privacy, or compliance assessments. It is intended to accelerate evidence collection, reduce back-and-forth, and ensure requests are scoped to the controls we rely on. Use this as the starting point for vendor security questionnaires, audit windows, and live/desk reviews.

Scope (pick applicable):
- Logical access controls (identities, RBAC, least privilege)
- Data separation, segmentation, and tenant isolation
- Encryption in transit and at rest (algorithms, key management, KMS/HSM details)
- Logging, monitoring, and retention (SIEM integration, log retention periods, access to logs)
- Change management and patching (release process, emergency patch handling)
- Vulnerability management and penetration test results
- Backup, recovery, and disaster recovery exercises
- Subprocessors and supply chain controls
- Physical security (if relevant for on-prem or co-located environments)
- Data locality and cross-border transfer mechanisms

Audit types (select):
- Desk review: submit documents, policies, configurations, and screenshots. No live access required.
- Remote interactive review: time-boxed video/webex session with screen sharing and the vendor SME for walkthroughs.
- Onsite audit: physical visitation and evidence inspection (rare; requires legal and SRE coordination).

Evidence request matrix (map each item to control domain and acceptable formats):
1) Access control
   - List of privileged accounts and last activity export (CSV).
   - IAM role definitions and policy attachments (JSON or screenshots).
   - MFA enforcement proof (policy export or screenshot of enforcement rule).
2) Encryption & key management
   - Encryption-at-rest configuration (blob-store/object-store settings or annotated architecture diagram).
   - KMS key lifecycle policy and rotation schedule (PDF or export).
   - Example of envelope encryption flow (diagram + short explanation).
3) Logging & monitoring
   - Sample log extract showing authentication events (sanitized) and fields mapping (CSV).
   - SIEM alerting rules relevant to suspicious access (text or screenshot).
   - Log retention policy document (PDF).
4) Vulnerability management
   - Recent vulnerability scan reports (redacted unless NDA allows) with dates and mitigation status.
   - Penetration test executive summary and remediation timeline.
5) Change management
   - Change ticket template and one completed change ticket showing approvals.
   - Deployment runbook and rollback procedure (text/PDF).
6) Backup & recovery
   - Backup schedule and recent restore test report (PDF/CSV).
   - RTO/RPO definitions and evidence of last DR exercise.
7) Subprocessors
   - Subprocessor register (name, scope, country, data types processed).

Evidence format guidance and redaction rules:
- Prefer native exports (CSV/JSON/PDF) over screenshots where possible.
- Redact customer-identifiable data unless specifically authorized via NDA. Mark redactions and provide a brief reason for each redaction.
- Timestamped artifacts are valued; include generated_at/collected_at where available.

Minimum response timeline and SLAs:
- Initial acknowledgement: within 3 business days of receipt.
- Desk review evidence delivery: 10 business days (can be extended by mutual agreement).
- Scheduling interactive sessions: vendor provides 3 available 1-hour windows within 7 business days.
- Critical follow-up clarifications: vendor to respond within 5 business days.

Confidentiality and handling:
- All vendor materials provided to Redwood Inference will be stored in the secure evidence repository (access-limited to security review group).
- Materials must not be republished; distribution requires approval.
- If vendor attaches their own sensitivity markings (e.g., CUI), Redwood will treat accordingly and coordinate handling.

Escalation path:
1) Primary reviewer (security) — Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai)
2) Secondary reviewer (engineering liaison) — Marco Ruiz (marco.ruiz@redwood.ai)
3) Legal & procurement escalation — Priya Nair (priya.nair@redwood.ai)

Acceptance criteria and sign-off:
- For each control area, the security reviewer marks: Acceptable / Acceptable with remediation plan / Unacceptable.
- If "Acceptable with remediation plan" is chosen, vendor must supply a measurable remediation timeline and a point of contact.
- Final sign-off requires security reviewer + engineering liaison.

Sample vendor request checklist (to copy into vendor email):
- Purpose and scope (one paragraph)
- Requested audit type (desk/remote/onsite) and preferred dates
- Primary vendor contact for evidence delivery (name, email, phone)
- Attach canonical evidence list (use matrix above, tick applicable rows)
- Links to Redwood supporting docs and NDA (if required)
- Instructions for secure upload (SFTP link or Redwood evidence portal link)

Template versioning & notes:
- v0.1 (draft): initial checklist and matrix — legal and procurement review required.
- Use internal Jira ticket prefix SEC-AUDIT when tracking requests and evidence.

Open questions & negotiation tips (notes to reviewer):
- Push for native exports rather than screenshots; explain operational cost of reformatting.
- If vendor refuses to share raw logs, request aggregated metrics and alerting rules as an interim.
- For sensitive customer data, propose a redaction review call where a limited set of unredacted samples can be viewed but not copied.

Appendix: Example short-form email to vendor:
"Hi <VendorName> — Redwood Security requests a desk review covering encryption-at-rest, IAM, logging, and vulnerability management. Please see the attached evidence checklist and let us know if the timeline is feasible. We can accept CSV/JSON exports, PDFs, or links to your compliance portal. A secure upload link is attached. Please acknowledge within 3 business days. Thanks — Ava Chen, Redwood Security."

Document history:
- Drafted by: Ava Chen (Security)
- Reviewed: Marco Ruiz (Engineering liaison), Priya Nair (Legal)
- Intended audience: Security reviewers, procurement, vendor managers

title: Executive Sponsor Briefing + Customer Quarterly Recap Template
preview:
Purpose and use notes:
This file is a compact, two-part working template to be used by CSMs for: (A) one-page Executive Sponsor Briefings delivered ahead of QBRs and renewal conversations, and (B) the customer-facing quarterly recap email that follows a QBR.

Quick guidance (to CSMs):
- Keep the executive briefing to one page, single-sided. Prioritize top-line business impact and a single ask.
- Use the customer-facing recap email to summarize outcomes, decisions, and agreed next steps within 72 hours of the meeting.
- Replace all {{PLACEHOLDERS}} before sending. Use metric sources (Console > Metrics > Customer) and attach the cost/latency export for dedicated customers.
- When in doubt, escalate to the AE for renewal-related asks or pricing changes.

Part A — Executive Sponsor Briefing (one pager)
Filename suggestion: exec-sponsor-briefing-{{CUSTOMER_NAME}}-Q{{QUARTER}}-{{YEAR}}.pdf
Audience: Customer executive sponsor (VP-level+), internal AE and CSM.
Format: PDF or doc, one page.

Header:
- Title: Executive Briefing — {{CUSTOMER_NAME}} (Q{{QUARTER}} {{YEAR}})
- Date: {{MEETING_DATE}}
- Prepared by: {{CSM_NAME}} / {{AE_NAME}}

Topline (3 bullets, max):
- 1 sentence impact: "Redwood inference driving X reduction in latency / Y% cost per token improvement; expected $Z saved over next 12 months."
- 1 sentence adoption: Key products in use (Hosted API / Dedicated / Private) + most-used models.
- 1 sentence risk/ask: e.g., renewal conversation, capacity spike risk, or exec-level decision needed.

Health snapshot (single row metrics, use short labels):
- Traffic: requests/day -> {{REQUESTS_PER_DAY}}
- Tokens: generated tokens/month -> {{TOKENS_MONTH}}
- Latency P50/P95: {{LAT_P50}} / {{LAT_P95}} ms
- Error rate: {{ERROR_RATE}}%
- Cost trend (MTD vs prior month): {{COST_TREND}}
- SLAs: current uptime (30d) -> {{UPTIME_30D}}%

Business outcomes / wins (3 bullets):
- Product/feature + outcome with measurable metric (e.g., "Search relevance rerank: +8% CTR; reduced human review by 60%")
- Operational: "Average inference spend per thousand requests down X% after quantization/ batching changes"
- Risk mitigation: "Fallback policy implemented for region eu-west-1; no customer-facing incidents in 60d"

Open items and asks (explicit):
- Renewal posture and timeline: "Contract renewal target: {{RENEWAL_DATE}}; current status: {{RENEWAL_STATUS}}"
- Exec ask: one sentence for exec sponsor (e.g., authorize Dedicated capacity increase, approve pilot budget, introduce to compliance team)
- Project/roadmap ask: dedicated feature or professional services request and an owner

Risks & mitigation (one-line each):
- Risk: capacity or model regression. Mitigation: canary rollout, auto-fallback to model X.
- Risk: data residency/compliance for new use case. Mitigation: private deployment / managed KMS.

Next steps (with owners and dates):
- Action 1: owner — due date
- Action 2: owner — due date

Notes for CSMs (internal):
- Attach: Console snapshot (tokens & latency), last 90d incident timeline, and cost breakdown exported from Billing tab.
- Use neutral, executive language. Avoid in-depth technical detail; include a 1-pager appendix for SRE/infra if needed.
- If renewal is within 90d: include ARR impact estimate and a simple ROI slide (1–2 bullets).

Part B — Customer-facing Quarterly Recap Email
Filename suggestion: qtr-recap-{{CUSTOMER_NAME}}-Q{{QUARTER}}-{{YEAR}}.docx
Audience: primary technical lead, exec sponsor, procurement (cc: AE, CSM)
Tone: concise, friendly, action-oriented

Email subject line options (pick one):
- "{{CUSTOMER_NAME}} — Q{{QUARTER}} Recap & Next Steps"
- "Redwood | Q{{QUARTER}} Business Review — Summary & Actions"

Email body template (replace brackets):
Hi {{FIRST_NAME}} and team,

Thanks again for your time on the Q{{QUARTER}} review. Below is a short recap and the agreed actions so we can keep momentum. Please let me know if anything is missing.

Quick highlights:
- Adoption & performance: "Hosts: {{DEPLOYMENT_MODE}} (Hosted/Dedicated/Private); Top model: {{MODEL_NAME}}; Tokens last 30d: {{TOKENS_30D}}."
- Business result: short measurable outcome (e.g., "Reduced median inference latency by {{LAT_REDUCTION}}ms; improved feature X conversion by {{PERCENT}}")
- Health: uptime {{UPTIME_30D}}%; P95 latency {{LAT_P95}}ms

Decisions made in meeting:
- Decision 1: short summary
- Decision 2: short summary

Agreed next steps (owner — due date):
- {{ACTION_1}} — {{OWNER_1}} — {{DUE_1}}
- {{ACTION_2}} — {{OWNER_2}} — {{DUE_2}}

Renewal and capacity note (if applicable):
- Renewal window: {{RENEWAL_WINDOW}}
- Suggested ask: "Recommend reserving Dedicated capacity tier to avoid regional preemption during peak events — estimated incremental cost: ${{EST_COST}}/month"

Attachments (include links):
- QBR one-pager (this doc)
- Performance & cost export (console link or CSV)
- Incident timeline (if any)

Closing:
Thanks — we appreciate the partnership. If helpful, we can schedule a brief follow-up with engineering to review the planned rollout for {{FEATURE}}.

Best,
{{CSM_NAME}}
Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference
{{CSM_EMAIL}}

CSM checklist before sending the recap email:
- [ ] Verify metrics from Console (tokens, latency, errors)
- [ ] Attach CSV export & include console links
- [ ] Confirm renewal dates with AE and legal ops
- [ ] Include AE on renewal asks or pricing approvals
- [ ] Run quick grammar pass / personalize subject line

Appendix (optional items to include when relevant):
- Detailed cost breakdown (per-model, per-region)
- SLO/uptime graphs (30/90d)
- Recent incident summaries and RCA status
- Short technical note on quantization/batching changes and any expected model-quality tradeoffs

Examples (short snippets you can paste into the briefing):
- "Topline: 'Since enabling automatic KV caching in Nov, your per-request token cost has dropped 22% and P95 latency improved from 360ms to 220ms.'"
- "Ask: 'Authorize 2x Dedicated workers for region us-west to support the holiday traffic spike; expected cost delta: $6k/month.'"

Versioning & maintenance notes (for internal ops):
- This template lives in Drive and GitHub (see linked_artifacts). If we make changes that affect AE/CS workflows (renewal wording or pricing asks), notify AE leads and update Confluence page CS/QBR-templates.

Open questions & TODOs for template owners:
- Add a small ROI calculator snippet that computes annualized savings from batching changes (CS dev task).
- Consider a one-click export from Console that produces the Exec Briefing PDF with populated metrics.

End of template.

Draft history: initial author: Ava Chen (11/08/2025). Collaborators: Liam (notes on renewals), Sofia (email wording), Ethan (metrics layout). Last edit: 02/20/2026 — tightened Topline bullets and added CSM checklist.

title: SDK concise pathways for non-engineer onboarding
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This is a compact, deliberately low-friction playbook for getting product managers, designers, and customer-success reps from zero to a meaningful SDK-first experiment within 30–60 minutes. Target assumes minimal terminal experience and no infra access. Focus: clarity, short happy-path, dogfooding loop, and telemetry hooks to measure success.

Context / why this exists:
- We see non-engineering stakeholders trying SDKs and getting blocked by environment setup, secrets, or heavy examples.
- Goal: reduce cognitive load, surface value quickly, and capture metrics that tell us if the flow worked (and where).

High-level flow (30–60 min):
1) 0–5m: sign up + get a test key (sandbox) — copy/paste only, no CLI install required.
2) 5–20m: run a single small example in the browser-based notebook/gist (JS or Python).
3) 20–35m: swap in a real prompt or dataset from their product area and run 1–3 calls.
4) 35–50m: capture outputs, annotate quality, file a simple issue or feedback item.
5) 50–60m: enable telemetry opt-in to send a small usage sample (anonymized) to DevExp for follow-up.

Design constraints (must):
- No package installs required for first run. Use CDN + snippet or an embedded playground iframe.
- Single-file examples that are copy/paste runnable.
- Minimal jargon; calls-to-action as actionable sentences.
- One clear success criterion: "I generated an output that I can comment on."

Playbook pages to create (minimum viable set):
- Quick landing page: "Try the SDK in 10 minutes (no install)" — code snippets in JS + Python via CDN.
- Templated example notebook: "Customer email summarizer" with 2 prompts and expected output samples.
- Troubleshooting mini-FAQ: network errors, quota errors, common copy/paste mistakes.
- Dogfooding form: short feedback + checkbox to opt in to send telemetry.

In-browser example (JS snippet for landing page) — text to use verbatim:
// copy/paste in browser console or codepen
(async () => {
  const res = await fetch('https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'Authorization': 'Bearer TEST-KEY-XXXX' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ model: 'redwood-lite-1', input: 'Summarize: The meeting focused on Q4 hiring and a new roadmap for mobile.' })
  });
  const json = await res.json();
  console.log('Generated:', json.output);
})();

Telemetry and success events (what we will measure):
- devx.sdk_onboard_started (user_id: anon_id)
- devx.sdk_example_ran (example_id, runtime: "browser", duration_ms)
- devx.sdk_feedback_submitted (quality_score: 1-5, tags)
- devx.sdk_dogfood_opt_in (boolean)
- devx.sdk_error_rate (error_code, example_id)

Privacy note: telemetry must be opt-in. If opt-in is true, we only collect the request metadata, timing, and a small anonymized snippet of the prompt (first 128 chars) with PII scrubbed. Implementation TODO: add server-side scrubber component (see linked PR).

Checklist for content authoring (for each example):
- [ ] Title and one-sentence outcome
- [ ] 2 short prompts (happy path and edge case)
- [ ] Example input + expected output sample
- [ ] Step-by-step copy/paste instructions (browser-first)
- [ ] Troubleshooting section (3 bullet quick fixes)
- [ ] Telemetry opt-in checkbox + brief privacy copy
- [ ] Feedback link that auto-populates context (example_id, browser UA)

Dogfooding plan (week 0–4):
Week 0: soft launch to PM/design cohort (20 people). Capture issues in JIRA and get 1–1 feedback.
Week 1: iterate on wording and one-click snippets based on failures.
Week 2: expand to CS and Solutions (50 people). Add two industry-specific examples.
Week 3–4: open internal wide beta and instrument funnel metrics. Goal: 25% completion rate (example run + feedback) from cohort.

Open questions / discussion items (triage in next sync):
- What is the process for issuing short-lived TEST keys? Who approves the quota? (Asked to infra team.)
- Do we include Node/npm instructions as a second step or keep browser-first only?
- How do we handle embargoed models or enterprise-only features in the public quickstart?
- Should the feedback form auto-create a GH issue or a JIRA ticket? (Pref: JIRA for product teams.)

Quick troubleshooting notes for common blockers:
- Bad API key / 401: remind to use the TEST-KEY prefix and no extra spaces when pasting.
- CORS in embedded playground: use our proxy endpoint /playground-proxy while we finalize CSP.
- Slow response / timeouts: switch model param to 'redwood-lite-1' or reduce max_tokens to 128.

Owner actions (Ava):
- [ ] Draft landing page copy (first pass due 2025-11-10)
- [ ] Coordinate test-key creation flow with infra (P0)
- [ ] Create templated notebook for PMs (Python + JS) and add to repo examples

Next steps for collaborators:
- Diego: validate telemetry event schema and stub endpoint (target 2025-11-17)
- Priya: write non-engineer copy and run first cohort (target 2025-12-01)
- Ellen: create feedback form and wire to JIRA (target 2025-11-24)

Appendix (sample feedback prompt to include in form):
- What did you try? (short)
- Was the output useful? (1-5)
- If not useful, what was wrong? (short)
- Would you like a follow-up from DevExp? (email optional)

Rough timeboxing suggestions for a 45-minute workshop:
- 0–5m: 1-slide context + ask (why we’re trying SDKs)
- 5–20m: run example and iterate on prompt
- 20–35m: internal prompt swap (bring a real short doc)
- 35–45m: feedback + submit form

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep examples short — PMs get bored with long prompts.
- Use real product examples to motivate adoption (ticket subject lines, short user stories).
- Consider 'cheat sheets' with 3 one-liners for prompting.

-- end of draft --

title: Context window resilience matrix and prompt harness
preview:
Purpose:
This is a working report for the long-context / retrieval stress suite used during model onboarding and regression checks. Focus: context-window resilience (8k, 32k, 128k variants), prompt-placement sensitivity, truncation behavior, and attention-collapse ("attention sink") modes that cause context corruption or hallucination. This doc captures the harness, prompt families, experiment matrix, quick findings, and next actions.

Quick summary (TL;DR):
- Most regressions occur when retrievals are appended after a long conversational history or when the retrieval anchor is near the buffer boundary (last ~4k tokens).
- rw-infer-alpha-32k-v2 shows stable citation anchoring up to ~28k tokens but exhibits increased hallucination when the retrieved chunk overlaps a sentence boundary across the truncation point.
- rw-infer-alpha-128k-quant (int8) has lower memory bandwidth but shows an attention-collapse index (ACI) spike for sequences with >96k context where many short repeated tokens are present (e.g., long enumerations).
- The prompt harness found two common failure modes: (1) retrieval-prefix-drop (retrieved text silently truncated mid-sentence), (2) citation drift (model substitutes a different retrieved doc title when context is long).

Experiment harness and reproducibility notes:
- Harness: prompt_harness_v3 (python) — generates three families of prompts and wraps retrievals in 3 placement modes: prefix, mid-history-insert, and trailing-append.
- Models tested: rw-infer-alpha-8k-v1, rw-infer-alpha-32k-v2, rw-infer-alpha-128k-quant.
- Baseline commit used for regression comparisons: serve@6f3c2d1 (2025-10-28). Current run: serve@7a9b44e (2026-02-18).
- Datasets: legal-chapters-collection (synthetic long legal chapters stitched), forum-thread-sim (simulated chat threads), pubmed-article-stitch (retrieved abstracts + body).
- Prompt sets: doc_salience_probe_v1, retrieval-anchor-perturb_v2, chapter-boundary-stitch_v1. Prompts are intentionally short instructions + retrieval block + question to avoid biasing the model.

Experiment matrix (selected cells):
- Variables: model_variant, context_length_target (8k/32k/128k), retrieval_size (2k/8k/16k), retrieval_placement (prefix/mid/append), quant_mode (fp16/int8), batch_size.
- Metrics collected per run: citation_exact_match_rate, citation_anchor_loss_rate, hallucination_rate (manual eval on 200 samples), token_truncation_count, ACI (attention-collapse-index, internal metric), p95_latency_ms.

Representative results (aggregated):
1) rw-infer-alpha-8k-v1 (context target 8k, retrieval 2k, append):
   - citation_exact_match_rate: 0.92
   - citation_anchor_loss_rate: 0.03
   - hallucination_rate: 0.06
   - token_truncation_count (avg): 12 tokens per sample (mostly punctuation cut)
   - ACI: 0.12
   - p95_latency_ms: 240ms
   Notes: expected baseline performance; small risk when retrieval placed near end of buffer with multiple punctuation-heavy retrievals.

2) rw-infer-alpha-32k-v2 (context target 32k, retrieval 8k, mid-history-insert):
   - citation_exact_match_rate: 0.85
   - citation_anchor_loss_rate: 0.07 (increases to 0.13 when retrieval crosses sentence boundary at ~28k tokens)
   - hallucination_rate: 0.12 (sampled, mostly title swaps)
   - token_truncation_count (avg): 48 tokens per sample when truncation occurred
   - ACI: 0.25 (spikes tied to repeated section headers)
   - p95_latency_ms: 720ms
   Notes: performance good when retrieval is inserted earlier; sensitivity increases when retrieval anchor is close to effective context window end.

3) rw-infer-alpha-128k-quant (context target 128k, retrieval 16k, append, int8):
   - citation_exact_match_rate: 0.78
   - citation_anchor_loss_rate: 0.18
   - hallucination_rate: 0.22 (noticeable hallucination for multi-doc stitching)
   - token_truncation_count (avg): 110 tokens per sample (trimmed from front in several runs due to upstream chunking bug)
   - ACI: 0.57 (high for lists/repeated tokens)
   - p95_latency_ms: 2.8s
   Notes: quantized path increases risk of attention instability for pathological inputs; follow-up recommended.

Sample prompt families (examples):
- doc_salience_probe_v1 (short):
  "You are an assistant. Below is a retrieved excerpt and a user question. Use only the retrieved excerpt to answer."
  [RETRIEVED_DOC_START]
  ... (2k-16k chunk)
  [RETRIEVED_DOC_END]
  "Question: According to the excerpt, what is the main compliance requirement for section 4?
  Answer:"

- retrieval-anchor-perturb_v2 (placement test):
  Conversation history (varies 4k-120k tokens) + INSERT_RETRIEVAL_HERE + question asking to cite the paragraph and exact sentence where the answer appears.

Failure modes observed (concrete):
- Retrieval-prefix-drop: when retrieval insertion happens at token index close to the buffer edge and the retrieval contains long sentences, the chunker sometimes trims the first few hundred tokens silently (tracked to chunker v1.2.3 bug). Action: open PR to chunker to add explicit boundary markers and lengths. (linked: github PR in artifacts).
- Citation drift: model returns a title or citation from a different doc that shares a substring with the retrieved doc when >40k history precedes the retrieval. Likely attention/context mixing; requires attention-map tracing.
- Mid-insert re-ranking mismatch: when we insert retrieval in the middle of a prior response, reranker occasionally scores a related doc higher than the exact-match doc leading to hallucinated references.

Manual inspection notes (examples):
- Sample A (32k run): retrieval contained sentence: "The license requires an annual audit by an independent CPA." Model answer: "The license requires quarterly internal audits." (hallucination; wrong frequency).
- Sample B (128k int8): retrieval split across chunk boundary; model returned a paragraph that concatenated end of doc A and start of doc B without separator; cites a non-existent section number. (stitch hallucination)

Regression summary vs baseline (serve@6f3c2d1):
- rw-infer-alpha-32k-v2: small regression in citation_exact_match_rate (-4 percentage points) driven by a single commit in attention-kernel selection (PR #712). Repro steps attached in linked PR.
- rw-infer-alpha-128k-quant: larger regression in ACI and hallucination_rate after quantization heuristics were enabled; likely tied to quant packing order.

Action items / Proposed fixes:
- Engineering: add explicit chunk-boundary markers in the retrieval pipeline (owner: Mateo). Target: +1 day for patch, +3 days for full regression.
- Research: run attention-map tracing tools on 32k runs where citation drift >10% to locate cross-attention anomalies (owner: Priya).
- Eval: expand manual eval set for 128k samples to 1k and add structured rubric for stitch hallucination (owner: Jordan).
- Ops: add p99 memory and ACI alerts to Redwood Console dashboards for dedicated runs with context>64k.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Is the ACI metric sensitive to tokenization artifacts (we saw spikes correlated with unusual punctuation sequences)? Research to confirm.
- Decide whether to enable truncated-retrieval-fallback (send shorter retrievals rather than risking mid-sentence truncation) as an interim mitigation.
- Validate whether the quantized attention kernel ordering change in PR #712 is the root cause for 128k instability.

Appendix: quick checklist for a new onboarding run
- [ ] checkout serve@<target-commit> and log hash in job metadata
- [ ] run prompt_harness_v3 with exact seed and dataset splits (document seeds in results)
- [ ] attach attention maps and p95/p99 latencies to results artifacts
- [ ] manual review of 200 random samples for hallucination and stitch errors

Notes to self: this doc is a working artifact — keep iterations here before moving a clean summary to Confluence.

If you need to reproduce a specific failing sample, ping me (Ava) and I will attach the seed and raw request/response; I kept the debug folder under s3://redwood-evals/long-context/debug/2026-02-18/ (internal).

-- Ava

title: KMS implementation decision log and evidence pipeline
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This working doc captures decisions and operational notes for KMS/HSM/BYOK adoption, key rotation procedures, and how we produce tamper-evident evidence for audits and SIEM ingestion. Audience: security eng, infra, SRE, legal/compliance. Status: working draft, expect iterative updates and small experiments in the next 60 days.

High-level choices (decision log):
- Decision 2025-11-10: Use envelope encryption for all customer data at rest. Root (KEK) stored in managed HSM (cloud provider or dedicated HSM) and used only to wrap/unwrap data encryption keys (DEKs). Rationale: limits exposure of KEKs and allows lightweight rewrap on rotation.
- Decision 2025-11-18: Support BYOK for enterprise customers as a first-class flow (customer uploads key material into a dedicated HSM tenant or uses a wrapped KEK). Rationale: compliance requests and regulated customers required BYOK. Implementation notes in subsequent sections.
- Decision 2025-12-02: KEKs rotate annually by default; DEKs rotate on a 90-day cadence or when triggered by key compromise. Non-destructive rotation adopted (re-wrap DEKs rather than re-encrypt all objects immediately).

Threat & tradeoff summary:
- Self-managed HSM: most control, higher ops burden, slower onboarding.
- Provider-managed HSM: lower ops, integrated audit logs, potential exposure to provider operations.
- BYOK: increases compliance coverage but complicates key lifecycle (customer escrow, rotation coordination, and revocation flows).

Implementation notes: KMS / HSM architecture
- Key hierarchy: account-KEK (master key per tenant group) -> application KEK (per-product) -> DEKs (per-dataset/object).
- DEKs stored as metadata in object storage encrypted with application KEK. No plaintext keys persisted.
- For caches/redis: encrypt values with local DEKs; memcached payloads include key id for lookup.
- Backups: backup blobs contain rewrapped DEKs with their version metadata. Restore process consults KMS to unwrap.

RBAC v2 and separation of duties (ops model):
- Roles: kms-admin, kms-operator, kms-auditor, application-service, svc-account.
- kms-admin: can create KEKs, configure HSM cluster, assign roles. Limited to 2 individuals by policy and key ceremony.
- kms-operator: can rotate and rewrap DEKs, and trigger emergency rotation. No key export rights.
- kms-auditor: read-only access to KMS audit logs and evidence buckets, cannot perform crypto ops.
- application-service: runtime ability to request DEK unwrap for specific object ids; access limited by scope and policy conditions (time-limited tokens, IP constraints).
- Enforcement: IAM policies + RBAC v2 service that maps Logical Roles to cloud service accounts. All role grants require automated approval ticket in Jira and a retention record.

Audit log schema (authoritative internal mapping):
- We will emit structured audit events to stdout/cloud logging, then forward to SIEM after enrichment.
- Canonical fields we must include (one-line per field):
  timestamp: ISO8601 UTC
  event_id: unique UUID
  principal: principal id (svc-account or user)
  principal_type: human | service | external (BYOK)
  action: key.create | key.rotate | key.unwrap | key.wrap | key.delete | key.policy.change | key.access_granted | key.access_revoked
  resource_type: kms_key | key_version | policy | hsm_cluster
  resource_id: logical key id (tenant/app/key-id)
  key_version: v123 (if applicable)
  request_origin: aws_region / ip / hostname
  correlation_id: request tracing id from routing layer
  outcome: success | failure | partial
  outcome_code: numeric code for quick filtering
  actor_metadata: role=...;ticket=JIRA-...;approval=auto/manual
  raw_payload_digest: sha256 hex of request payload (for evidence linking)
  evidence_ref: s3://... or gs://... pointing to rotation manifest or signed artifact
  note: free text for human ops notes

Sample audit event (log text form, not machine-parseable here but follows the schema above):
  timestamp: 2026-01-12T18:23:44Z
  event_id: b6f3e2a4-7c1d-4f9a-9ea2-1f3d2c7a6d55
  principal: svc-account-data-processor
  principal_type: service
  action: key.unwrap
  resource_type: key_version
  resource_id: tenant-42/app-embed/key-a
  key_version: v2026-01-12-1
  request_origin: us-west2
  correlation_id: req-0a1b2c3d
  outcome: success
  outcome_code: 0
  actor_metadata: role=kms-operator;ticket=SEC-9123;approval=auto
  raw_payload_digest: da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709
  evidence_ref: s3://redwood-compliance/kms-evidence/tenant-42/rotation-2026-01-12.json

Evidence automation: what we collect and how
- For every rotation event (automatic or manual) we produce a signed rotation manifest (json) that contains: event_id, key_id, old_version, new_version, principal, signatures (operator pubkeys), timestamp, rewrap_count, verification_checks (list). The manifest is signed by the rotation pipeline's private key and uploaded to the compliance bucket.
- Rotation pipeline steps (automated):
  1) Validate rotation schedule and preconditions (no pending critical incidents).
  2) Fetch keys and perform rewrap via HSM API.
  3) Run verification jobs that try to unwrap sample DEKs and perform checksum on selected objects.
  4) Generate manifest and sign with pipeline key.
  5) Push manifest and logs to compliance bucket and emit audit event referencing artifact.
  6) Trigger SIEM alert if any verification steps fail.
- Evidence retention: manifests and signed logs kept for 7 years in immutable storage with WORM enabled for regulated customers.

SIEM export & mapping notes:
- Forwarder will ship canonical audit events to SIEM (Splunk/ELK). We will use an enrichment step to add tenant context and risk-scoring.
- Sensitive fields redaction: principal metadata may contain emails; strip or hash PII before forwarding to lower-retention indexes. Full raw logs (with PII) live only in locked compliance bucket with strict ACL.
- Key SIEM alerts to implement: unauthorized key access attempt, repeated unwrap failures, key deletion without approved ticket, unapproved policy changes, HSM health anomalies.
- Example Splunk query (plain-text intent): count by outcome where action=key.unwrap and outcome=failure and timewindow=1h -> trigger pager if count > 5 for a single principal.

Operational playbooks (short):
- Normal rotation: ops pipeline does automatic rewrap, manifest created, QA verification green, ticket closed with evidence refs.
- Emergency rotation (compromise suspected):
  1) Create emergency ticket, annotate with incident id.
  2) Freeze all new unwrap requests by toggling policy to deny-list.
  3) Promote a backup KEK, rewrap DEKs, publish manifest with 'emergency' flag.
  4) Notify legal and customers as required by contract.
  5) Post-incident: forensic job to collect HSM attestation, audit logs, and signed manifests for evidence package.

Operational metrics to emit to Console and Compliance dashboards:
- rotations_per_day, rotations_per_tenant, rotation_success_rate, average_time_to_rotate, unwrap_latency, unauthorized_access_rate, audit_log_delivery_lag.

Compliance checklist (initial):
- HSM attestation records captured and stored.
- All key operations produce an audit event that contains evidence_ref.
- BYOK onboarding process includes escrow options and revocation guidance.
- RBAC v2 mapped to cloud IAM, with automated ticketing for any role grant.
- SIEM pipeline configured with redaction rules and retention tiers.

Implementation tasks and owners:
- Infra: implement rotation pipeline and signing keys (owner: Marco, due: 2026-01-31).
- SRE: integrate audit-forwarder into SIEM and test full flow (owner: Priya, due: 2026-02-14).
- Legal/Compliance: finalize retention policy and BYOK contract appendix (owner: Daniella, due: 2026-02-01).
- Product: define tenant UX for BYOK and emergency rotation notices (owner: Sasha, due: 2026-02-20).

Open questions / risks:
- How do we handle HSM vendor change mid-contract? Need cross-wrapped KEKs or a migration plan that maintains auditability.
- BYOK customer-supplied keys with unsupported algorithms: define minimal supported algorithms and fail-fast during onboarding.
- Long-tail restores: rewrap-on-restore vs on-access re-encryption cost analysis.

Next steps (short term):
- Prototype evidence manifest signer and upload flow (week 1).
- Add canonical audit emitter library to all KMS clients (week 2).
- Run tabletop for emergency rotation and evidence collection (week 3).

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally prescriptive but pragmatic; feedback requested on rotation cadence defaults, retention windows, and BYOK escrow language.
- If you are an SRE or infra engineer and want to test the manifest signature verifier, see github:infra/kms-config#142 for the testing harness.

End of document.
text:
Purpose and scope:
This working doc captures decisions and operational notes for KMS/HSM/BYOK adoption, key rotation procedures, and how we produce tamper-evident evidence for audits and SIEM ingestion. Audience: security eng, infra, SRE, legal/compliance. Status: working draft, expect iterative updates and small experiments in the next 60 days.

High-level choices (decision log):
- Decision 2025-11-10: Use envelope encryption for all customer data at rest. Root (KEK) stored in managed HSM (cloud provider or dedicated HSM) and used only to wrap/unwrap data encryption keys (DEKs). Rationale: limits exposure of KEKs and allows lightweight rewrap on rotation.
- Decision 2025-11-18: Support BYOK for enterprise customers as a first-class flow (customer uploads key material into a dedicated HSM tenant or uses a wrapped KEK). Rationale: compliance requests and regulated customers required BYOK. Implementation notes in subsequent sections.
- Decision 2025-12-02: KEKs rotate annually by default; DEKs rotate on a 90-day cadence or when triggered by key compromise. Non-destructive rotation adopted (re-wrap DEKs rather than re-encrypt all objects immediately).

Threat & tradeoff summary:
- Self-managed HSM: most control, higher ops burden, slower onboarding.
- Provider-managed HSM: lower ops, integrated audit logs, potential exposure to provider operations.
- BYOK: increases compliance coverage but complicates key lifecycle (customer escrow, rotation coordination, and revocation flows).

Implementation notes: KMS / HSM architecture
- Key hierarchy: account-KEK (master key per tenant group) -> application KEK (per-product) -> DEKs (per-dataset/object).
- DEKs stored as metadata in object storage encrypted with application KEK. No plaintext keys persisted.
- For caches/redis: encrypt values with local DEKs; memcached payloads include key id for lookup.
- Backups: backup blobs contain rewrapped DEKs with their version metadata. Restore process consults KMS to unwrap.

RBAC v2 and separation of duties (ops model):
- Roles: kms-admin, kms-operator, kms-auditor, application-service, svc-account.
- kms-admin: can create KEKs, configure HSM cluster, assign roles. Limited to 2 individuals by policy and key ceremony.
- kms-operator: can rotate and rewrap DEKs, and trigger emergency rotation. No key export rights.
- kms-auditor: read-only access to KMS audit logs and evidence buckets, cannot perform crypto ops.
- application-service: runtime ability to request DEK unwrap for specific object ids; access limited by scope and policy conditions (time-limited tokens, IP constraints).
- Enforcement: IAM policies + RBAC v2 service that maps Logical Roles to cloud service accounts. All role grants require automated approval ticket in Jira and a retention record.

Audit log schema (authoritative internal mapping):
- We will emit structured audit events to stdout/cloud logging, then forward to SIEM after enrichment.

  key_version: v123 (if applicable)
  request_origin: aws_region / ip / hostname
  correlation_id: request tracing id from routing layer
  outcome: success | failure | partial
  outcome_code: numeric code for quick filtering
  actor_metadata: role=...;ticket=JIRA-...;approval=auto/manual
  raw_payload_digest: sha256 hex of request payload (for evidence linking)
  evidence_ref: s3://... or gs://... pointing to rotation manifest or signed artifact
  note: free text for human ops notes

Sample audit event (log text form, not machine-parseable here but follows the schema above):
  timestamp: 2026-01-12T18:23:44Z
  event_id: b6f3e2a4-7c1d-4f9a-9ea2-1f3d2c7a6d55
  principal: svc-account-data-processor
  principal_type: service
  action: key.unwrap
  resource_type: key_version
  resource_id: tenant-42/app-embed/key-a
  key_version: v2026-01-12-1
  request_origin: us-west2
  correlation_id: req-0a1b2c3d
  outcome: success
  outcome_code: 0
  actor_metadata: role=kms-operator;ticket=SEC-9123;approval=auto
  raw_payload_digest: da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709
  evidence_ref: s3://redwood-compliance/kms-evidence/tenant-42/rotation-2026-01-12.json

The manifest is signed by the rotation pipeline's private key and uploaded to the compliance bucket.

title: Onboarding Impact Snapshot - KV Slice Calculator
preview:
Purpose:
This working sheet estimates incremental GPU and KV memory impact for onboarding a new autoregressive chat model (vX.3) across regions. It's a lightweight 'slice' calculator to help decide whether we need additional constrained-capacity slots vs. capacity reclamation (quantization, cache tuning). Primary audience: infra, serving-runtime, and release leads.

Executive summary (short):
- Baseline fleet: 320 active A100(80GB) equivalents across NA/EMEA/APAC (effective capacity = 300 after reserved infra).
- New model vX.3 increases per-request KV by ~3.2x vs current baseline model due to longer context and wider key/value dims.
- If we keep current routing and maintain a 70% KV cache hit target, expected GPU working set increases by ~22% globally; worst-case cold-starts push demanded GPUs +42% for 24–72h after rollout without cache warming.
- Immediate actions: (1) enable targeted prefix caching for high-traffic customers, (2) validate 4-bit quantized variant for cold-fallback, (3) schedule temporary burst capacity in eu-west-1 during rollout window.

Key assumptions used in calculations:
- Model vX.3 tokens_per_request (mean): 280 tokens (includes prompt + response).
- Average request sequence length used for KV sizing: 200 tokens.
- KV state size per token (after Fused KV layout, before quantization): 1,280 bytes/token (estimated from 4096 hidden-dim, 32-bit fused K/V).
- Avg QPS (global) for this model expected: 75 r/s (day 1, pilot) -> 600 r/s (steady plan month+).
- Target GPU KV memory headroom: 10% for misc allocations and scheduler buffers.
- Current cache hit rate baseline target: 70% (can be tuned 50%–90%).

Calc snippet (plain):
1) Single-request KV bytes (live tokens) = avg_seq_len * kv_bytes_per_token
   = 200 * 1,280 = 256,000 bytes (~250 KB) per active request context.
2) Active contexts per GPU (existing scheduling profile) = 640 contexts @ 24-token average seq in current models; with longer contexts we must reduce contexts to keep within 80GB device memory:
   effective_contexts_new = floor((device_mem_bytes - headroom) / avg_kv_per_context)
   -- device_mem_bytes = 80 * 1024^3 = 85,899,345,920 bytes
   -- headroom 10% => usable = 77,309,411,328 bytes
   => contexts = floor(77,309,411,328 / 256,000) = 301, approx.
   => contraction from 640 -> 301 contexts per GPU (53% capacity per-GPU for concurrent contexts).
3) GPU throughput impact (context-constrained):
   If previously 1 GPU supported 640 concurrent short contexts corresponding to X tokens/s, now supporting 301 contexts with longer seq means effective concurrency drop. Rough mapping: expected per-GPU QPS support for vX.3 = baseline_per_gpu_qps * (301/640) * (short_seq_norm/long_seq_norm)
   (detailed perf sims needed for exact).
4) KV cache sizing per region (cold / steady):
   - Steady working set = hot_contexts * avg_kv_per_context * adjustment_for_shared_prefixes
   Example eu-west-1 pilot sizing (steady):
     projected_traffic_share = 25% of global steady QPS (600 r/s) => 150 r/s steady
     assume average concurrent contexts needed = 4 * r/s * latency_window(s) (window = 2.5s) -> 4 * 150 * 2.5 = 1500 active concurrent contexts (approx);
     steady_KV_bytes = 1500 * 256,000 = 384,000,000,000 bytes (~358 GiB) at 100% unique keys;
     with 70% hit rate (i.e., 70% covered by cache), required cache footprint to reach that hit = steady_KV_bytes * 0.7 = ~250 GiB.

Scenario matrix (global):
- Conservative pilot (cold-start-heavy):
  - Day 0–3 traffic = 75 r/s global, cache miss 60% => transient working set = 75 * concurrency_factor * avg_kv_per_context -> results in transient KV demand ~150 GiB additional across regions; GPUs needed +45 GPUs to avoid OOMs under conservative constraints.
- Likely steady rollout:
  - Global 600 r/s, 70% cache hit -> additional device count needed: ~+68 GPUs (to preserve p99 latency targets and concurrency headroom).
- Aggressive worst-case (no prefix caching, 50% hit, higher seq 320 tokens):
  - GPU requirement delta +140 GPUs and KV cache footprint doubles vs steady estimate.

Regional hot spots and routing implications:
- eu-west-1: large pilot traffic share; plan to pre-warm prefixes for top 10 customers one hour before rollout, and request dedicated prefetch windows.
- us-east-1: latency-sensitive customers — prioritize quantized fallback route if model cold.
- ap-southeast-1: limited spare capacity; plan for cross-region overflow to us-east-1 with higher cost multiplier.

Quantization and mitigation levers considered:
- 4-bit activation + 8-bit KV storage projection reduces avg_kv_per_token by ~2.6x (empirical on similar models). Validate quality/regressions on core prompts (eval set EVAL-ROLL-71).
- Aggressive prefix caching for conversational sessions: storing first N turns (N=3) reduces new unique KV by ~45% for support-chat style traffic.
- Session stitching + KV dedupe: can reduce effective unique KV footprint when many concurrent sessions share identical system/prompt prefixes.

Operational checklist (rollout playbook snippets):
- Pre-roll (D-7 to D-1): run kv-sim job with sampled traffic from prod-traffic-snapshot-2026 (ticket JIRA-INFRA-1210).
- D-1: open temporary Dedicated burst pool in eu-west-1 (estimate +30 GPUs) and tag capacity with model:vX.3-canary.
- D0: enable 10-min prefix-warmers for the top-20 customers, monitor KV hit-rate (metric: kv.cache.hit_rate per-model per-region).
- D0+1 to D0+7: maintain elevated autoscale thresholds for model:vX.3 routes; watch p99 latency and OOM anomalies.
- Post-roll (D+7): re-run capacity sim with observed traffic, adjust per-region reservations and decommission transient burst.

Open unknowns and risks:
- Hidden memory overheads from runtime per-request metadata and new kernel buffers (could add 8% to observed KV usage). Need runtime instrumentation PR (github#74).
- Behavioral changes from clients (longer multi-turn sessions) could inflate sustained KV more than QPS-driven estimates.
- Fallback quality regressions from aggressive quantization may require routing to larger instances.

Recommended immediate experiments (week 1):
- A/B: Quantized fallback route (4-bit) vs full-precision for 5% traffic — measure quality delta and OOM incidence.
- Prefill test: run synthetic prefix prefill for top 5 customers and measure cache hit delta and traffic warmth time constant.
- KV pressure test: synthetic ramp to 2x expected QPS for 3 hours to validate autoscaler and eviction behavior.

Appendix: quick numeric reference (rounded)
- Avg_kv_token: 1,280 bytes
- Avg_seq_len: 200 tokens
- KV/context: 256 KB
- Usable device mem (80GB w/10% headroom): ~72 GB usable
- Contexts/GPU (new model): ~300
- Baseline contexts/GPU (old short-context model): 640
- Steady global QPS target (month+): 600 r/s
- Estimated additional GPUs (likely steady): +68 GPUs global

Next steps & owners:
- Marcus Li: run kv-sim extended run for vX.3, include kernel overhead flags (ETA 2026-02-20).
- Sofia Alvarez: coordinate eu-west-1 burst reservation and pre-warm schedule (ETA 2026-02-18).
- Ravi Patel: spin up quantized eval bench and report quality deltas vs eval set EVAL-ROLL-71 (ETA 2026-02-21).

Notes to self / scratches:
- Consider storing compacted KV in zstd streams for rare long-horizon cold-storage; latency implications unknown.
- Check if Dedicated reservations can be tagged temporarily for model-level cost accounting (billing ticket FIN-231).

References and quick links:
- kv-sim README: github.com/redwood-inference/kv-sim
- Capacity deck (Q4 2025): confluence.redwood/infra/capacity-q4-2025
- Related incidents: postmortem-INFR-2025-09 (OOM storm during large-surge onboarding)

(End of sheet)

- Fallback quality regressions from aggressive quantization may require routing to larger instances.

Recommended immediate experiments (week 1):
- A/B: Quantized fallback route (4-bit) vs full-precision for 5% traffic — measure quality delta and OOM incidence.
- Prefill test: run synthetic prefix prefill for top 5 customers and measure cache hit delta and traffic warmth time constant.
- KV pressure test: synthetic ramp to 2x expected QPS for 3 hours to validate autoscaler and eviction behavior.

Appendix: quick numeric reference (rounded)
- Avg_kv_token: 1,280 bytes
- Avg_seq_len: 200 tokens
- KV/context: 256 KB
- Usable device mem (80GB w/10% headroom): ~72 GB usable
- Contexts/GPU (new model): ~300
- Baseline contexts/GPU (old short-context model): 640
- Steady global QPS target (month+): 600 r/s
- Estimated additional GPUs (likely steady): +68 GPUs global

Next steps & owners:
- Marcus Li: run kv-sim extended run for vX.3, include kernel overhead flags (ETA 2026-02-20).
- Sofia Alvarez: coordinate eu-west-1 burst reservation and pre-warm schedule (ETA 2026-02-18).
- Ravi Patel: spin up quantized eval bench and report quality deltas vs eval set EVAL-ROLL-71 (ETA 2026-02-21).

Notes to self / scratches:
- Consider storing compacted KV in zstd streams for rare long-horizon cold-storage; latency implications unknown.
- Check if Dedicated reservations can be tagged temporarily for model-level cost accounting (billing ticket FIN-231).

References and quick links:
- kv-sim README: github.com/redwood-inference/kv-sim
- Capacity deck (Q4 2025): confluence.redwood/infra/capacity-q4-2025
- Related incidents: postmortem-INFR-2025-09 (OOM storm during large-surge onboarding)

(End of sheet)

title: Collective Velocity: 30/60/90 Compact and All-Hands Runbook (draft)
preview:
PURPOSE

A compact planning artifact to align the company on the next 90 days of work (30/60/90 cadence), the set of proposed company-level OKRs for exec review, and the all-hands agenda + presenter notes for the town. This is intentionally lightweight — a single doc for execs and facilitators to run from during the next staging and rehearsal cycle.

TL;DR
- Proposed company OKRs (draft) — 3 objectives, each with 2–4 measurable KRs.
- All-hands format: 40m exec updates + 20m Q&A + 20m breakout demos/pulse checks.
- Communication timeline and rehearsal plan for week of Feb 16.
- Primary risks and mitigations + clear owner list for follow-ups.

CONTEXT / WHY NOW
- We need a tighter cadence between product bet alignment, infra capacity prep (Dedicated & Private lanes), and customer-facing SLAs.
- Recent spikes in hosted usage during experiments highlighted routing fallback gaps (see JIRA-1873).
- Execs want a short, decisive set of OKRs to cascade into Q2 planning and resource commitments.

PROPOSED COMPANY-LEVEL OKRs (DRAFT)
Objective A: Make inference predictable and cost-efficient for production workloads
- KR A1: 20% reduction in median per-token CPU/GPU cost across top 10 customer workloads (benchmarked).
- KR A2: Decrease 95th percentile serving latency by 15% for our hosted API across p50-p95 windows.
- KR A3: Ship two new Optimize recommendations (auto-batching/caching presets) and measure adoption in 8 pilot customers.

Objective B: Expand enterprise readiness and Private deployment adoption
- KR B1: Close three Paid Private pilots (VPC/on-prem) with documented deployment runbooks and SLAs.
- KR B2: Audit and remediate top 10 compliance items for Private (KMS, audit logs) — evidence in compliance tracker.
- KR B3: Reduce onboarding time for Dedicated customers from signup to first successful inference job by 30%.

Objective C: Developer experience and ecosystem velocity
- KR C1: Ship language SDK parity (feature gap < 5%) across Python/TS/Go with updated examples.
- KR C2: Cut average time to reproduce a customer incident (first repro in SLO timeframe) by 40%.
- KR C3: Publish two reference apps that demonstrate cost-effective serving patterns, with benchmarks.

NOTES ON METRICS & ASSUMPTIONS
- Cost/KR measurements will use the billing baseline from Jan 2026 and the infra telemetry bucket tagged \"okrs-bench-v1\".
- Latency baseline gathered from hosted-api/perf-dashboard; small sample variance expected across regions.
- Adoption pilot = >3 customers integrating optimize presets into a production route for >= 7 days.

ROLL-OUT & COMMUNICATION TIMELINE
- Feb 12: Finalize draft OKRs with exec edits (this doc).
- Feb 15: Pre-brief managers + leads; send pre-read to all staff. (Owners: Ava / Marco)
- Feb 17: Exec rehearsal (30m) + Town run-through (facilitators). (Owners: Priya / Samir)
- Feb 19: Company all-hands + public blog mini-post summarizing top-level OKRs and asks. (Owner: Elena)

title: Prefetch + Prefix Collapse Sensitivity Probes (8k / 32k / 128k)
preview:
Purpose and quick summary:

This doc collects exploratory probes focused on an under-observed failure mode we started seeing in onboarding runs: \"prefix collapse\" when retrieved context is pre-fetched into KV cache and contains repeated structural tokens (TOC-like headers, repeated legal boilerplate, verbose metadata blocks). The tests also stress the interaction of prefetching and attention sink behaviour at 8k/32k/128k context sizes. Goal is to produce a small, reproducible prompt set and scoring heuristic to catch regressions in model variants and quantization profiles.

High-level hypothesis:
- When retrieval prefetch merges large structured blocks into the prefix (via automatic prefetch/caching), attention distribution can over-concentrate on repeated delimiters/headings. The runtime's attention-kernel selection + kv-cache placement causes effective token-sink behavior where the model repeats or collapses into a short, high-likelihood subsequence (e.g., \"SECTION 1... SECTION 1...\").
- Higher seq lengths (32k/128k) amplify the failure because more repeated structure is likely to be present and batching/bucketed-kernel fallbacks change attention precision.
- Prefetch window size and prefetch order (most-recent-first vs scored-relevance) change the severity: LIFO caching of retrieved chunks tends to surface the structural blocks early and causes collapse sooner.

Quick definitions we use here:
- prefix collapse: model output devolves into repetition/short-mode (low diversity) anchored to structural tokens from retrieved prefix.
- attention sink ratio (ASR): fraction of attention mass directed at a 256-token window that contains >3 repeated heading tokens. Higher is worse.
- recall@k_on_prompt: whether the expected retrieved snippet that should answer the question appears verbatim within first N tokens of generation. Useful for retrieval fidelity check but can be decoupled from collapse.

Test matrix (runs we executed):
- Models: onboarding-v1 (fp16 baseline), onboarding-v1-4bit (QAT sim), onboarding-v2-8bit (mixed-precision fallback)
- Context sizes: 8k, 32k, 128k (window truncated or streamed depending on runtime)
- Prefetch modes: none (on-demand retrieval), prefetch-LIFO, prefetch-score (highest relevance first), prefetch-batched (merge then shard)
- Retrieval payloads: clean-article (single narrative), tox-headers (repeated H1/H2 / TOC tokens), legal-boiler (long repeated clause headings), metadata-spam (json-like small tokens repeated)
- Metrics recorded: PPL (over generation), ASR (attention sink ratio), repetition-rate (n-gram repeat in first 512 tokens), recall@50, latency p50/p95, failure-mode labels (collapse, truncation-drop, correct-answer)

Example probe prompts (representative, anonymized):
1) Question w/ retrieval context: \"Summarize the policy changes relevant to user data retention in the retrieved document. Provide bullets and a one-line TL;DR.\" (uses legal-boiler retrieval set)
2) Conversational probe: \"We pasted the following doc (begin DELIM) ... (end DELIM). Create an executive summary oriented to a product lead.\" (uses tox-headers retrieval set)
3) Ground-truth recall probe: short factual Q that requires exact snippet present in retrieval (clean-article set)

Key findings (summary):
- Prefetch-LIFO + tox-headers: Most severe collapse. At 32k and 128k the ASR increased by 3–6x vs on-demand and the model entered a short-mode repetition within 80–200 tokens in ~42% of trials for v1 fp16. The collapse pattern often repeated the nearest heading token sequence.
- Prefetch-score: better than LIFO but worse than on-demand when retrieval set contained many short tokens (metadata-spam). Score-based ordering still surfaced many small repeated tokens early because BM25/semantic scores favored headings.
- Onboarding-v1-4bit: quantized variant shows earlier collapse (median collapse at ~110 tokens at 32k) and higher repetition-rate. This suggests quantization amplifies attention kernel precision loss for repeated-token discrimination.
- 8k runs frequently avoided collapse unless the retrieval chunk contained repeated headings adjacent to the prompt (because less context means fewer repeated tokens injected). However 8k runs had more truncation events where the retrieved answer was removed by cut-off — a different failure mode.
- Attention kernel fallbacks at 128k on some hardware produced spiky ASR traces that correlate strongly with collapse onset.

Representative numeric table (aggregated across 200 probes each):
- Prefetch mode: on-demand | LIFO | score
- ASR median (32k): 0.06 | 0.28 | 0.12
- Collapse incidence (32k): 4% | 42% | 17%
- Repeat-rate first 512 tokens (32k): 1.8% | 9.2% | 4.8%
- recall@50 (32k): 86% | 79% | 84%

Qualitative examples (short excerpts):
- Collapse example (tox-headers, LIFO, 32k): generation begins: \"SECTION 4: DATA POLICY
SECTION 4: DATA POLICY
SECTION 4: DATA POLICY\" then follows a short repeated paragraph.
- Truncation drop (8k): expected answer snippet absent; model apologizes: \"I don't have that in context\" even though retrieval returned it — indicates runtime truncation order bug when merging prompt+prefetch.

Observed root causes (working hypotheses):
1) KV insertion order: prefetch-LIFO places the most recent retrieved chunk closest to prompt; if that chunk contains structural tokens, attention heads may overweight them and downstream MLPs find repetition high-likelihood path.
2) Kernel precision and quantization: quantized matrix-math plus alternative kernels (for longer sequences) reduce token discrimination leading to greedier top-next-token choices.
3) Caching boundary effects: when a retrieval chunk crosses a kv-cache shard boundary, some attention weights get remapped by runtime and produce local peaks.
4) Scoring bias in retriever: retrieval scoring often favors headings because they match query terms — so heading-heavy chunks are more frequent in top-K retrieval.

Short experiments to triage (what we ran):
- Swap insertion order: LIFO vs FIFO vs interleave-by-score — LIFO worst, interleave-by-score reduced collapse to under 10% in tests.
- Add heading-normalization filter: rewrite retrieved tokens to remove repeated heading markers before prefetching. This reduced ASR by ~60% in tox-headers set and eliminated collapse in many trials.
- Kernel forcing: pin kernel to high-precision path for first 512 generation tokens (where collapse often starts). This mitigated early collapse in 32k runs but increased latency by ~30ms.
- Quantization ablation: run 4-bit with higher calibration steps — reduced collapse incidence but cost tradeoffs exist.

Proposed automated checks to add to onboarding/regression suite:
1) ASR probe: synthetic doc with repeated headings; fail if ASR > 0.15 within first 256 generation tokens on any prefetch mode for 32k/128k.
2) Collapse detection: detect >3 exact-heading repeats within first 200 tokens and flag as regression.
3) Recall vs truncation check: pair a recall@50 test with a runtime-trace that confirms retrieved chunks were actually mapped into kv-cache (detect ordering/truncation bug).
4) Kernel-path telemetry correlation: require kernel-id + quantization-mode included in CI logs for long-context runs to correlate regressions with specific kernels.

Action items (owners):
- eng-serving-runtime (Devon): add ASR metric emission to long-context trace and implement simple heading-normalizer prefetch pipeline flag (ETA: 2 wks).
- applied-ml (Ava + Marcus): fold the tox-headers probe into onboarding harness and add pass/fail thresholds (ETA: 1 wk).
- infra/bench (Lina): schedule repeated 128k runs across hardware families to capture kernel-fallback patterns (ETA: 3 wks).
- product/quality: decide whether heading-normalization is safe default or opt-in; create short biz-impact note for decision (ETA: 1 sprint).

Open questions / next experiments:
- Is heading-normalization safe across arbitrary legal/policy text? Need small-scale eval with human raters to ensure we don't remove semantics (plan: 50-doc human check).
- How does retrieval reranking (explicit penalize short repeated tokens) impact end-user quality? Could reduce recall for valid heading-based answers.
- Can we combine a lightweight attention-regularizer in the runtime (drop attention mass on repeated single-token delim tokens) with minimal latency cost?

Appendix: sample synthetic generator description (for CI):
- Build base paragraph templates with heading tokens like \"SECTION n:\" repeated at varying density. Add noise tokens between headings. Create 200 variants per context-size. Randomize insertion location relative to prompt (adjacent vs far).
- Tag expected-answer tokens and ensure at least one clean snippet answers the probe question. This lets us classify collapse vs recall failures.

Notes to reviewers:
- This doc is an exploratory catalogue of probes and immediate mitigations. It's intentionally rough and contains run-level shorthand. Please comment where you'd like more raw traces (we have attention weight dumps and kernel logs zipped and linked in the github PR above).
- For final regression integration we should convert probes into a small deterministic set (<=40 cases) to keep CI runtime reasonable.
