title: Skill-first Lateral Hire Pilot Playbook
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook defines a 12-week pilot to evaluate "skill-first" hiring for lateral senior ICs (L4-L6 engineering ICs). The pilot flips the usual resume-centric funnel: assess demonstrable day-one skills through short, paid pairing engagements and a compact on-site loop focused on practical scope and handoffs. Goal = faster interviews, clearer quality signal, and a structured micro-onboarding path that maximizes early impact.

Hypothesis:
- Candidates who demonstrate productive contributions in a paid 4-8 hour pairing engagement correlate strongly with successful 30/60/90 outcomes.
- A tighter rubric that weights production-readiness and team-fit over pedigree reduces time-to-offer and lowers false positives.

Scope & constraints:
- Candidate pool: lateral ICs (3+ years experience in relevant stacks) applying to eng teams building inference, runtime, infra, and console features.
- Pilot size: 20 candidates evaluated over 12 weeks, across 3 hiring squads.
- Compensation: pairing engagements are paid at $150/hr (4–8 hours).
- Legal: CV/contract language approved by legal (linked).
- Not in scope: entry-level and manager hires during pilot.

Candidate profile (skill-first persona):
- Produces readable, incremental PRs in pair sessions.
- Quick at debugging production-like failures and communicating tradeoffs.
- Demonstrates operational thinking: observability, deploy safety, rollback routes.
- Clear empathy for product/consumers and can map technical work to product outcomes.

Funnel redesign (high level):
1) Screening: short phone screen (30m) focused on role fit + context (no whiteboard).
2) Paid pairing: 4-hour paid pairing task with engineer from target team (remote). Deliverable = merged micro-PR to test repo or a documented incident diagnosis.
3) Compact loop: 3 interviewer onsite (2 technical + 1 cross-functional) 60–75m each with structure below.
4) Offer & micro-onboard: 30-day targeted ramp plan with explicit pairing schedule.

Paid pairing structure (practical):
- Prework (candidate): short repo readme and a 10–15 minute summary of approach.
- Session flow (4hr): 30m prep + 3h focused pairing + 30m debrief where candidate writes a short follow-up note with next steps.
- Deliverable options: a small bugfix + end-to-end test, a performance regression micro-investigation with hypotheses and a short mitigation patch, or a docs+observability enhancement that improves signal-to-noise.
- Evaluation: immediate interviewer notes and a 1–5 pass/fail scorecard on criteria below.

Interview rubric (compact, prioritized):
- Production Problem Solving (weight 30%): isolates failure modes, crafts minimal safe remediation, understands rollout/rollback.
- Incremental Code & Safety (weight 25%): produces small, testable changes; understands backward compatibility and incremental rollout.
- System Reasoning & Tradeoffs (weight 20%): latency/cost tradeoffs, caching, KV lifecycles, deployment topology.
- Communication & Handoffs (weight 15%): clarity on docs, ticketing, runbook notes, and how to onboard another engineer to finish the work.
- Team Fit & Pragmatism (weight 10%): collaborates, receives feedback, prioritizes impact.

Scoring notes: each pillar scored 1–5; passing threshold = weighted score >= 3.6 out of 5. If pairing is borderline, bring candidate to compact loop; if pairing fails, typically disqualify unless extenuating context.

Compact onsite loop templates:
- Technical deep-dive (60m): 30m focused debugging or design of a specific small service; 30m code review exercise on real PR. Emphasize production tradeoffs.
- Cross-functional sync (60m): discuss a recent product incident and the candidate's role strategy for prevention or mitigation (safety-first).
- Systems breadth (60m): short set of 3 prompts on scaling, caching, and cost-control with timeboxed answers.

Interviewer cheat-sheet (what to probe quickly):
- Have you shipped and owned a small service end-to-end? Tell me one tradeoff you made and why.
- When you see a latency regression, what are the first 5 things you check?
- Give me one example where you chose an incremental fix over a rewrite. Why?
- How would you add an observability signal for a feature that has no metrics today?

Offer calculus adjustments:
- For skill-first hires, benchmark comp to market + 5% for pairing conversion lift and speed.
- Include acceleration stipend for relocation where needed.

Micro-onboarding / Ramp-by-doing (30/60/90 anchor):
- Day 0–7: environment + 1:1 buddy paired tasks (small PR live review), access to critical runbooks, first bug/monitoring ticket assigned.
- Week 2–4: 2–3 pairing sessions/week with cross-team owners; finish a scoped incremental feature or hotfix end-to-end with reviewer sign-off.
- Month 2: lead one on-call triage shift with mentor shadowing and submit a short internal postmortem practice doc.
- Month 3: owner of a small feature area (can ship independently), measurable success metric agreed with manager.

Success metrics for pilot (quantitative + qualitative):
- Time-to-offer from application (target < 14 days).
- Offer acceptance rate for pairing-offers (target >= 65%).
- 30-day ramp score (manager survey) for pilot hires vs baseline hires (+0.3 on 5-point scale target).
- 90-day retention and IC impact (feature throughput, number of merged PRs, time-to-merge).

Operational playbook (roles and responsibilities):
- People Ops: run pilot logistics, pay pairing invoices, compile candidate feedback.
- Hiring Manager: design pairing task, be present for at least 2 pairing sessions per cohort.
- Interviewer pool: 12 volunteers across squads trained on rubric.
- Legal/Finance: approve stipend flow and contractor paperwork.

Risks & mitigations:
- Risk: Paid pairing increases cost per candidate. Mitigation: cap paid pairing to top 30% of screens; re-evaluate ROI after 20 candidates.
- Risk: Candidates gaming pairing tasks. Mitigation: vary tasks and require live merge with reviewer and tests.
- Risk: Interviewer calibration drift. Mitigation: weekly calibration huddle + shared anonymized notes.

Rollout plan & timeline (12 weeks):
- Week 0–2: interviewer training, task templates, legal approvals.
- Week 3–8: run pilot (target 20 candidates across 3 squads).
- Week 9–12: analyze metrics, retro with hiring managers, decision whether to adopt.

Open questions & follow-ups:
- Should pairing be remote-only or have optional on-site pairing for local candidates? (ops cost tradeoff).
- How to integrate referrals and internal transfers into the pilot?
- Define success threshold for conversion to full program (recommended: > 70% hiring manager satisfaction + 1.0 SD improvement in 30d ramp).

Appendix: templates (short snippets)
- Pairing session invite: "4h paid pairing session: "4h paid pairing session: goal = ship a small fix that adds an end-to-end test. Please arrive ready to share a short approach doc."
- Candidate debrief prompt: "What did you try, what failed, what next? Attach PR link and test run screenshot."

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally conservative on scope and budget to limit pilot exposure. We expect operational lift from People Ops initially.
- Please review pairing compensation language with Finance and Legal before greenlight.

Document history:
- Drafted by: Ava Chen (People Ops) 2025-11-03
- Reviewed with Diego, Priya, Riley 2025-12-01
- Updated after hiring-manager calibration 2026-01-18
- Minor edits and scheduling 2026-02-10
text:
Purpose:
This playbook defines a 12-week pilot to evaluate "skill-first" hiring for lateral senior ICs (L4-L6 engineering ICs). The pilot flips the usual resume-centric funnel: assess demonstrable day-one skills through short, paid pairing engagements and a compact on-site loop focused on practical scope and handoffs. Goal = faster interviews, clearer quality signal, and a structured micro-onboarding path that maximizes early impact.

Hypothesis:
- Candidates who demonstrate productive contributions in a paid 4-8 hour pairing engagement correlate strongly with successful 30/60/90 outcomes.
- A tighter rubric that weights production-readiness and team-fit over pedigree reduces time-to-offer and lowers false positives.

Scope & constraints:
- Candidate pool: lateral ICs (3+ years experience in relevant stacks) applying to eng teams building inference, runtime, infra, and console features.
- Pilot size: 20 candidates evaluated over 12 weeks, across 3 hiring squads.
- Compensation: pairing engagements are paid at $150/hr (4–8 hours).
- Legal: CV/contract language approved by legal (linked).
- Not in scope: entry-level and manager hires during pilot.

Candidate profile (skill-first persona):
- Produces readable, incremental PRs in pair sessions.
- Quick at debugging production-like failures and communicating tradeoffs.
- Demonstrates operational thinking: observability, deploy safety, rollback routes.
- Clear empathy for product/consumers and can map technical work to product outcomes.

Funnel redesign (high level):
1) Screening: short phone screen (30m) focused on role fit + context (no whiteboard).
2) Paid pairing: 4-hour paid pairing task with engineer from target team (remote). Deliverable = merged micro-PR to test repo or a documented incident diagnosis.
3) Compact loop: 3 interviewer onsite (2 technical + 1 cross-functional) 60–75m each with structure below.
4) Offer & micro-onboard: 30-day targeted ramp plan with explicit pairing schedule.

Paid pairing structure (practical):
- Prework (candidate): short repo readme and a 10–15 minute summary of approach.
- Session flow (4hr): 30m prep + 3h focused pairing + 30m debrief where candidate writes a short follow-up note with next steps.
- Deliverable options: a small bugfix + end-to-end test, a performance regression micro-investigation with hypotheses and a short mitigation patch, or a docs+observability enhancement that improves signal-to-noise.
- Evaluation: immediate interviewer notes and a 1–5 pass/fail scorecard on criteria below.

Interview rubric (compact, prioritized):
- Production Problem Solving (weight 30%): isolates failure modes, crafts minimal safe remediation, understands rollout/rollback.
- Incremental Code & Safety (weight 25%): produces small, testable changes; understands backward compatibility and incremental rollout.
- System Reasoning & Tradeoffs (weight 20%): latency/cost tradeoffs, caching, KV lifecycles, deployment topology.
- Communication & Handoffs (weight 15%): clarity on docs, ticketing, runbook notes, and how to onboard another engineer to finish the work.
- Team Fit & Pragmatism (weight 10%): collaborates, receives feedback, prioritizes impact.

Scoring notes: each pillar scored 1–5; passing threshold = weighted score >= 3.6 out of 5. If pairing is borderline, bring candidate to compact loop; if pairing fails, typically disqualify unless extenuating context.

Compact onsite loop templates:
- Technical deep-dive (60m): 30m focused debugging or design of a specific small service; 30m code review exercise on real PR. Emphasize production tradeoffs.
- Cross-functional sync (60m): discuss a recent product incident and the candidate's role strategy for prevention or mitigation (safety-first).
- Systems breadth (60m): short set of 3 prompts on scaling, caching, and cost-control with timeboxed answers.

Interviewer cheat-sheet (what to probe quickly):
- Have you shipped and owned a small service end-to-end? Tell me one tradeoff you made and why.
- When you see a latency regression, what are the first 5 things you check?
- Give me one example where you chose an incremental fix over a rewrite. Why?
- How would you add an observability signal for a feature that has no metrics today?

Offer calculus adjustments:
- For skill-first hires, benchmark comp to market + 5% for pairing conversion lift and speed.
- Include acceleration stipend for relocation where needed.

Micro-onboarding / Ramp-by-doing (30/60/90 anchor):
- Day 0–7: environment + 1:1 buddy paired tasks (small PR live review), access to critical runbooks, first bug/monitoring ticket assigned.
- Week 2–4: 2–3 pairing sessions/week with cross-team owners; finish a scoped incremental feature or hotfix end-to-end with reviewer sign-off.
- Month 2: lead one on-call triage shift with mentor shadowing and submit a short internal postmortem practice doc.
- Month 3: owner of a small feature area (can ship independently), measurable success metric agreed with manager.

Success metrics for pilot (quantitative + qualitative):
- Time-to-offer from application (target < 14 days).
- Offer acceptance rate for pairing-offers (target >= 65%).
- 30-day ramp score (manager survey) for pilot hires vs baseline hires (+0.3 on 5-point scale target).
- 90-day retention and IC impact (feature throughput, number of merged PRs, time-to-merge).

Operational playbook (roles and responsibilities):
- People Ops: run pilot logistics, pay pairing invoices, compile candidate feedback.
- Hiring Manager: design pairing task, be present for at least 2 pairing sessions per cohort.
- Interviewer pool: 12 volunteers across squads trained on rubric.
- Legal/Finance: approve stipend flow and contractor paperwork.

Risks & mitigations:
- Risk: Paid pairing increases cost per candidate. Mitigation: cap paid pairing to top 30% of screens; re-evaluate ROI after 20 candidates.
- Risk: Candidates gaming pairing tasks. Mitigation: vary tasks and require live merge with reviewer and tests.
- Risk: Interviewer calibration drift. Mitigation: weekly calibration huddle + shared anonymized notes.

Rollout plan & timeline (12 weeks):
- Week 0–2: interviewer training, task templates, legal approvals.
- Week 3–8: run pilot (target 20 candidates across 3 squads).
- Week 9–12: analyze metrics, retro with hiring managers, decision whether to adopt.

Open questions & follow-ups:
- Should pairing be remote-only or have optional on-site pairing for local candidates? (ops cost tradeoff).
- How to integrate referrals and internal transfers into the pilot?
- Define success threshold for conversion to full program (recommended: > 70% hiring manager satisfaction + 1.0 SD improvement in 30d ramp).

Appendix: templates (short snippets)
- Pairing session invite: "4h paid pairing session: "4h paid pairing session: goal = ship a small fix that adds an end-to-end test. Please arrive ready to share a short approach doc."
- Candidate debrief prompt: "What did you try, what failed, what next? Attach PR link and test run screenshot."

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally conservative on scope and budget to limit pilot exposure. We expect operational lift from People Ops initially.
- Please review pairing compensation language with Finance and Legal before greenlight.

Document history:
- Drafted by: Ava Chen (People Ops) 2025-11-03
- Reviewed with Diego, Priya, Riley 2025-12-01
- Updated after hiring-manager calibration 2026-01-18
- Minor edits and scheduling 2026-02-10

title: Skill transparency scorecard and handoff agreement
preview:
Purpose:
This working doc captures a compact, privacy-conscious candidate skill scorecard plus an explicit handoff agreement for new hires and role transitions. Goal: increase repeatable, transparent signals for hiring decisions and make onboarding handoffs faster (target: cut time-to-productivity by 20% for first 30 days).

Scope and constraints:
- Applies to IC roles (IC-1 through IC-5) and first-line managers across engineering, product and people teams.
- Not a replacement for the formal offer packet or legal onboarding.
- Candidate-facing summaries derived from this scorecard must be redacted of panel-level comments and only surface agreed growth areas and positive highlights.

How to use this doc (quick runbook):
1) Interview panel completes the scorecard within 48 hours of final loop. Use numerical scale + short evidence notes.
2) Hiring manager synthesizes top 3 signals and adds role-specific ramp commitments.
3) People Ops creates the handoff checklist and schedules the onboarding sync with hiring manager + buddy within 72 hours of acceptance.
4) All artifacts stored under candidate folder (linked) + anonymized copy pushed to calibration pager for quarterly review.

Scorecard (flat, shareable, calibrated fields):
- Technical Proficiency (0-4): concrete evidence (code review, design probe, whiteboard). 0: no demonstrable core skill, 1: borderline, 2: competent baseline, 3: strong/above expectations, 4: exceptional/mentor level.
- Delivery & Ownership (0-4): signals — project spec, follow-through, trade-off reasoning.
- System Design / Architecture Thinking (0-4): clarity in abstractions, scaling, failure modes. Mark N/A if role not applicable.
- Collaboration & Communication (0-4): cross-team clarity, async docs, stakeholder management.
- Learning Agility (0-4): examples of quick learning, missing knowledge gaps with plan.
- Role Fit & Motivation (0-4): alignment with job mission and trapdoors (motivation risks).
- Bias & Inclusion Lens (Pass/Fail + notes): explicit check to call out any potential adverse cultural fit signals; pass required for hire to proceed to offer.
- Red Flags (free text): any legal, performance, or communication concerns.
- Top 3 strengths (short bullets).
- Top 3 growth areas (concrete, coachable).

Calibration tips (for panel leads):
- Use 0-4 consistently across panels; audit distribution after 5 hires in a role.
- Avoid conflating communication style with competence; annotate where style may be culturally influenced.
- If reviewers disagree by >=2 points on any axis, require a short reconciliation note (who will re-evaluate? hiring manager or panel lead).

Handoff Agreement (to be completed by hiring manager within 48 hours of offer acceptance):
- Role overview (one paragraph): what success looks like in 30/60/90 days.
- Primary 30-day commitments (3 items): e.g., access + credentials, buddy pairing, first deliverable scope.
- 60-day commitments (3 items): deeper ownership, first cross-team stakeholder, success metric draft.
- 90-day commitments (3 items): independent delivery, mentorship goals, candidate-led improvement proposal.
- Required access list (explicit): dev repos, CI, analytics dashboard ids (use placeholder names — do not paste secrets).
- Mandatory onboarding sessions (dates or week numbers): product deep dive, infra runbook, security orientation, compliance training.
- Assigned buddy + 1-line role of buddy.
- First-2-week check-ins cadence: 3x 30-min syncs with manager, weekly 15-min with buddy.
- Practical ramp blockers: dependent PRs, blocked infra tickets, missing dataset access.

Templates (copy/paste-ready snippets):
- Manager summary (for People Ops):
  - "Candidate: <Name> | Role: <Level> | Start: <date> | Top-3 strengths: <bullets> | Primary risk: <one-line> | 30d milestone: <deliverable>"
- Slack onboarding invite (to buddy + new hire):
  - "Hi <Name> — welcome! I'm <Buddy>. We'll pair Tues/Thurs 10–11am first week. Quick Qs: do you need laptop/access today?"

Interview evidence examples to include in scorecard (short guidance):
- Code exercise: link to PR + reviewer notes (one sentence summary).
- Design: sketch link or doc + complexity tradeoffs candidate raised.
- Behavioral: specific short STAR example with metrics if possible.

Privacy and candidate communication rules:
- Raw panel commentary is internal only.
- Candidate-facing summary limited to: top strengths, top 2 growth areas, 30/60/90 milestones. Wording must be positive and growth-focused.

Open decisions / unresolved questions (notes for People Ops execs):
- Do we make Bias & Inclusion flag required for all roles or only manager+? Current recommendation: required for manager and senior IC roles; consider pilot for all hires in Q1 2026.
- Should we auto-generate anonymized calibration reports quarterly? Needs engineering time; estimate 2 sprint effort.
- Where to store candidate-facing summaries for audit traceability? (Confluence vs. HRIS).

Quick checklist before first day (manager):
- Offer acceptance recorded + candidate folder created.
- Scorecard finalized and anonymized copy uploaded to calibration repo.
- Buddy confirmed and first week schedule shared.
- Workspace & hardware ordered (if applicable).
- 30/60/90 milestones posted in team tracker.

Post-mortem lite (30-day retro template):
- What went well?
- Biggest blocker(s) to ramp?
- Evidence of measurable progress (metrics/PRs/stakeholder feedback).
- 3 quick improvement actions for next hire.

Appendix: sample calibrated rating distribution we aim for (internal only):
- Majority of hires: Technical Proficiency median = 3 for IC3, 2 for IC2.
- Learning Agility mean >= 2.5 for all hires.
- Panel variance target: std dev <= 0.9 across axes.

Sign-offs (when ready to finalize):
- Hiring manager: ___________________
- People Ops reviewer: ___________________
- Panel lead: ___________________

Notes to self: collect anonymized 6-month outcomes for hires using this scorecard and compare predicted vs actual ramp time. If predictions are >15% off, iterate on rubric wording.

References / links to existing artifacts: CONFLUENCE:HR/onboarding-playbook | JIRA:OPS-1292 (onboarding infra), GITHUB:hr-templates/new-hire-buddy-email

Last edits: Ava Chen — aligned on policy phrasing; Miguel Torres added tech rubric clarifications; Priya Nair added inclusion checklist.

[END OF DOC]

title: Quickstart failsafes & onboarding standup — Feb 02 2026
preview:
Purpose:
Short standup to align on the Quickstarts SRE/failsafe improvements that need to land before the next internal onboarding run (Feb 16). Focus areas: SDK client-side retry/backoff, sample app circuit-breaker, docs guidance for timeouts and cold-starts, and telemetry hooks for easier incident triage.

Attendees:
- Ava Chen (owner)
- Marcus Patel (samples)
- Leila Gomez (docs & UX)
- Samir Rao (SDK runtime)
- Jordan Kim (telemetry/observability)

High-level goals this sprint (2 weeks):
1) Make quickstarts resilient for typical user mistakes (no API key, unstable network, unexpected 429s).
2) Add conservative client-side retry/backoff in SDK quickstart snippets and sample apps.
3) Add telemetry labels and a lightweight debug page in sample webapp.
4) Update quickstart checklist and troubleshooting doc to include failsafe steps.

Decisions (agreed during standup):
- Default quickstart SDK snippet will include an exponential backoff with jitter and a 3-attempt limit. Rationale: balance UX vs accidental long-running loops. (Owner: Samir)
- Sample Node webapp will include a small circuit-breaker wrapper around the client used in examples. We will plug a telemetry event when the breaker trips. (Owner: Marcus)
- Docs will add a \"Common failure modes\" section with copy/paste troubleshooting commands (curl + k/v explanation). Keep it short and actionable. (Owner: Leila)

Open questions:
- Should the default retry policy return an altered HTTP error (e.g. 429-retried) so samples can explain behavior? (Samir to prototype)
- How verbose should the telemetry event be for privacy? Minimal: event type + quickstart-id + region. (Jordan to confirm with security)

Work items and owners:
- DEVEXP-134: \"SDK quickstart: add safe retry wrapper with jitter\" — Samir Rao — target: 2026-02-07 — PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-sdk/pull/482
- DEVEXP-137: \"Sample webapp: integrate circuit-breaker & debug page\" — Marcus Patel — target: 2026-02-09 — branch: sample-robustness/Marcus
- DOC-QUICK-5: \"Quickstarts: common failure modes + timeouts guidance\" — Leila Gomez — target: 2026-02-06 — confluence: Quickstarts-Playbook-v2 (section draft)
- OBS-22: \"Add lightweight telemetry tags for quickstart flows\" — Jordan Kim — target: 2026-02-05 — will open instrumentation PR and Linear ticket

Implementation notes (for engineers):
- SDK snippet (pseudocode to include in quickstart):

  // simple retry with exponential backoff + jitter
  async function callWithRetries(fn, attempts = 3) {
    let base = 200; // ms
    for (let i = 0; i < attempts; i++) {
      try {
        return await fn();
      } catch (err) {
        if (!isRetryable(err) || i === attempts - 1) throw err;
        let delay = base * Math.pow(2, i) + Math.floor(Math.random() * 100);
        await sleep(delay);
      }
    }
  }

  // include a short note: \"This wrapper is intentionally conservative for demos; tune for production.\"

- Circuit-breaker: use small stateful wrapper in samples instead of full library to avoid extra deps. Trip threshold: 5 failures within 60s, reset after 60s. Emit telemetry event when tripped.

Docs / UX specifics (Leila):
- Add a 1-page \"Quickstarts troubleshooting\" that contains:
  - common error patterns (401, 403, 429, 502) and 1-line fixes
  - how to run sample locally without cloud (mock mode)
  - recommended timeout values for local vs hosted (e.g., client timeout 10s default, increase for long generations)
  - when to switch to Dedicated or use streaming endpoints

Telemetry/Observability (Jordan):
- Minimum telemetry schema for quickstart flows: {event, quickstart_id, env, outcome, error_class, breaker_tripped (bool)}.
- Telemetry events must not contain user prompt text. PR for instrumentation to include a privacy checklist.

Blockers / Risks:
- Security review for telemetry labels might push Jordan's work to next week if we need legal signoff. (Mitigation: ship opaque IDs and no prompt text)
- Samir needs a short guide on which errors are safe to retry (500/502/503/504/429 vs 400/401). He will create a short internal cheat-sheet and link it into the SDK README.

Action items (concrete):
- Samir: open SDK PR with retry wrapper + tests (target 2026-02-07) — include simple example and a note in README.
- Marcus: implement circuit-breaker in Node sample + debug page showing last 10 events (target 2026-02-09).
- Leila: draft the troubleshooting page and add the quickstart checklist (target 2026-02-06).
- Jordan: propose telemetry schema in a short doc, ask security for confirmation (target 2026-02-05).
- Ava: run internal onboarding playthrough on Feb 16; collect top 5 failure modes to validate changes.

Follow-ups (next standup):
- Demo Samir's SDK PR live in the sample webapp and note behavior under induced 429s.
- Marcus to demo the debug page and circuit-breaker trip UX.
- Leila to present final troubleshooting page for sign-off.
- Jordan to confirm telemetry is privacy-safe with security.

Notes to self / internal friction points:
- Keep quickstart surface area small: avoid explaining batching/quantization here; link to Optimize for that.
- We should not push a default that hides errors from users; the sample must both auto-retry and log clear error messages when retries fail.

Quick checklist before onboarding run (Feb 16):
- [ ] SDK PR merged and shipped to npm (or local install path for playthrough)
- [ ] Sample webapp debug page reachable at /debug-quickstart
- [ ] Troubleshooting page published in Quickstarts Playbook
- [ ] Telemetry events flowing to dev-only stream for inspection

Last notes: keep language in quickstarts explicit about \"demo defaults\" vs \"production settings\" so new users aren't enticed to copy-paste the demo policy into high-volume apps.

End of notes.

    for (let i = 0; i < attempts; i++) {
      try {

  - common error patterns (401, 403, 429, 502) and 1-line fixes
  - how to run sample locally without cloud (mock mode)
  - recommended timeout values for local vs hosted (e.g., client timeout 10s default, increase for long generations)
  - when to switch to Dedicated or use streaming endpoints

Telemetry/Observability (Jordan):
- Minimum telemetry schema for quickstart flows: {event, quickstart_id, env, outcome, error_class, breaker_tripped (bool)}.
- Telemetry events must not contain user prompt text. PR for instrumentation to include a privacy checklist.

Blockers / Risks:
- Security review for telemetry labels might push Jordan's work to next week if we need legal signoff. (Mitigation: ship opaque IDs and no prompt text)
- Samir needs a short guide on which errors are safe to retry (500/502/503/504/429 vs 400/401). He will create a short internal cheat-sheet and link it into the SDK README.

Action items (concrete):
- Samir: open SDK PR with retry wrapper + tests (target 2026-02-07) — include simple example and a note in README.
- Marcus: implement circuit-breaker in Node sample + debug page showing last 10 events (target 2026-02-09).
- Leila: draft the troubleshooting page and add the quickstart checklist (target 2026-02-06).
- Jordan: propose telemetry schema in a short doc, ask security for confirmation (target 2026-02-05).
- Ava: run internal onboarding playthrough on Feb 16; collect top 5 failure modes to validate changes.

Follow-ups (next standup):
- Demo Samir's SDK PR live in the sample webapp and note behavior under induced 429s.
- Marcus to demo the debug page and circuit-breaker trip UX.
- Leila to present final troubleshooting page for sign-off.
- Jordan to confirm telemetry is privacy-safe with security.

Notes to self / internal friction points:
- Keep quickstart surface area small: avoid explaining batching/quantization here; link to Optimize for that.
- We should not push a default that hides errors from users; the sample must both auto-retry and log clear error messages when retries fail.

Quick checklist before onboarding run (Feb 16):
- [ ] SDK PR merged and shipped to npm (or local install path for playthrough)
- [ ] Sample webapp debug page reachable at /debug-quickstart
- [ ] Troubleshooting page published in Quickstarts Playbook
- [ ] Telemetry events flowing to dev-only stream for inspection

Last notes: keep language in quickstarts explicit about \"demo defaults\" vs \"production settings\" so new users aren't enticed to copy-paste the demo policy into high-volume apps.

End of notes.

title: Capacity drift and trigger tuning (Jan 2026)
preview:
Summary:
This sheet records the 'capacity drift' experiments run across three regions (us-west1, europe-west1, ap-southeast1) to measure how reserved GPU headroom and autoscaler triggers interact under multi-day traffic drift and burst patterns. Goal: find safe min/max reservation defaults and trigger tuning that keep P95 latency within SLO and reduce reserved-GPU waste by >=12% versus current conservative defaults.

Background and motivation:
- We observed a slow upward drift in baseline token/s request load in us-west1 over the last 90 days (~7% increase), causing periodic eviction cascades when short spikes hit (chat + reranking mixes).
- Current global defaults: min=8 GPUs per pool, max=200, scale-up threshold 70% (over 2 windows), cooldown 5m. These were set conservatively and produce idle cost in low-traffic regions.
- Hypothesis: region-specific min toggles + shorter, burst-aware scale-up triggers with temporary burst tokens reduce cost while preserving latency.

Experiment harness (trigger-harness-v2):
- Input workload: 14-day replay with injected drift (0%, +2%, +5%, +10% over baseline) and 6 engineered microbursts (2x baseline for 45s).
- GPU types modelled: A100-40GB (primary), H100-80GB (test), L4-32GB (edge candidate).
- Metrics captured: reserved GPUs, allocated GPUs, P50/P95 token latency, 95th-cache-miss penalty (simulated kv-cache cold fraction), scale events (up/down), time-to-allocate (s), cost delta vs baseline.
- Safety checks: cap scale-out to +40 GPUs/1m to avoid noisy neighbor risk; require success criteria for 3 consecutive bursts to reduce min.

Knobs tested (matrix):
- min-reserve per region: [4, 6, 8, 12]
- max-reserve per region: [64, 128, 200]
- scale-up trigger: CPU-like metric analog 'utilization' thresholds [60%, 70%, 80%] with window sizes [30s, 60s, 120s]
- cooldown window after scale-up: [30s, 2m, 5m]
- transient-burst-mode: enable/disable (when enabled, detect 2x spike <60s and allow temporary +20% over max for 60s)

Raw results (aggregated highlights):
- us-west1 (A100): baseline reserved 16, observed 95p util drift -> mean 58% (with +7% traffic). Best config: min=6, max=128, trigger=70%/60s, cooldown=2m, transient-burst-mode ON. Outcome: P95 latency within SLO (1.1x baseline), reserved GPUs -14% cost reduction, scale events per day +12% but short-lived.
- europe-west1 (A100): baseline reserved 8, observed stable traffic. Best config: min=4, max=64, trigger=60%/30s, cooldown=30s, burst-mode ON. Outcome: reserved GPUs -22% cost reduction, P95 latency unchanged, small increase in scale churn (increase in 1m-scale events) but no SLO violations.
- ap-southeast1 (L4/H100 mix): traffic volatile with many microbursts. Best config: min=8 (H100 pools: min=2), max=128, trigger=80%/120s, cooldown=5m, burst-mode OFF for H100 pools. Outcome: P95 latency improved vs baseline for heavy-gen due to higher max and longer window; reserved GPUs -6% overall, but H100 reserved increased by design.

Selected numeric summary table (CSV style):
Region,GPU,baseline_reserve,recommended_min,recommended_max,avg_reserved_delta_pct,p95_latency_delta_pct,scale_events_day,notes
us-west1,A100,16,6,128,-14,-5,22,"short cooldown + burst-mode tolerated"
europe-west1,A100,8,4,64,-22,0,28,"optimize min for steady low baseline"
ap-southeast1,L4+H100,12,8/2,128,-6,-3,40,"keep H100 conservative; L4 aggressive scaling"

Observations and interpretation:
1) Region-differentiated mins reclaim most wasted capacity. Reducing min in europe-west1 produced the largest percent savings with minimal impact because baseline traffic is stable and far from peaks.
2) Short windows (30s) + low trigger (60%) reduce reserved tail but increase churn; acceptable in regions with cheap preemption (L4) but risky for H100 pools due to allocation latency.
3) Transient-burst-mode reduces peak shortages for brief microbursts and avoids overshooting min reductions in us-west1; it is valuable where microbursts are frequent and allocation latency is low.
4) For heavy-gen pools (H100), longer windows and higher thresholds reduce noisy scaling and keep latency predictable; cost savings smaller but stability higher.

Failure modes discovered:
- If cooldown is too short (30s) in a region with 30–90s GPU provisioning lag, repeated scale-ups stack and cause allocation backpressure. Observed in ap-southeast1 when cooldown=30s with H100.
- Allowing temporary burst over max must be gated by regional capacity tokens; otherwise it leads to multi-region fallback and cross-region latency penalties.

Recommendations (practical rollout path):
Phase 0 (pilot, 2 weeks): apply recommended min/max and trigger per-region for 10% of traffic in canary (us-west1 primary). Monitor token-level latency and scale-event burn rate.
Phase 1 (regional rollout, 6 weeks): if pilot OK, roll to europe-west1 and ap-southeast1 with H100 pools using H100-safe defaults (higher trigger, longer cooldown).
Phase 2 (automation): add a daily drift detection job (see linked Confluence) that compares 7d vs 90d baseline and incrementally nudges min by +/-1 GPU after smoke checks.

Operational playbook snippets (to include in runbook):
- If P95 latency >= 1.25x SLO for >10m after scale-up, revert to previous max and disable transient-burst-mode for that pool.
- To evacuate an over-provisioned region: reduce min to floor(traffic_percentile_7d * 0.9) and enable temporary burst-mode to avoid immediate SLO regressions.
- Emergency throttle: reduce client-level burst tokens for any route causing >60% of scale events in a 1h window.

Open questions and next investigations:
- Can we safely use predictive alarms (simple ARIMA or EWMA) to pre-warm H100 pools 60–90s before predicted spikes? (worth an A/B test)
- Would introducing a per-model cold-cache multiplier improve predictions for chat-heavy workloads that show higher cold-KV cache penalty?
- Cost accounting: how to apportion cross-region transient burst costs to tenants?

Action items (owners):
- Implement pilot config in infra repo and create canary release (Ava Chen, due 2026-02-05)
- Add daily drift detector job (Priya Nair, due 2026-02-12)
- Run A/B predictive-warm test for H100 (Diego Morales + Rina Kato, scope by 2026-03-01)
- Update runbook with cooldown safeguards and burst gating (Liam O'Connor, due 2026-02-08)

Notes/to-self:
- Keep a snapshot of RL logs for days with >3 scale events/hr for postmortem telemetry.
- Link into billing-sheets to quantify $/token delta for applied changes.

Appendix: tuning checklist (quick):
- Start with conservative per-region default: min=ceil(0.3*7d_p50_gpu_usage), max=2x currentpeak
- Use trigger=70%/60s for A100 pools; 80%/120s for H100 pools
- cooldown 2m default; raise to 5m for H100
- enable transient-burst-mode in regions with high burst density and low allocation latency

End of sheet.

title: Regional GPU surge breakdown — weekly export
preview:
Purpose:
This is a weekly export meant to capture short-term surge behavior across regions and clusters, with emphasis on identifying short windows where utilization approaches provisioned capacity and recommending immediate mitigations (burst routing, temporary pool increases, and scheduled pre-warming).

Scope (this export):
- Time window: rolling 7d (2025-12-29 to 2026-01-05) aggregated by day + hour-of-day patterns.
- Granularity: cluster-level and pool-level (pool = logical group of GPUs for tenancy tiers).
- Regions: us-west, us-east, eu-west, ap-south.
- Metric: GPU vRam-utilization-percent (measured per-node averaged over 1m samples), pod share, queued-requests count.

Executive summary:
- Observed 3 short-duration surges (1–3 hours) in us-west and one multi-hour sustained ramp in eu-west. Peak 7d regional averages: us-west 79%, us-east 62%, eu-west 83%, ap-south 58%.
- Top 5 clusters by peak (one-hour max):
  cluster-usw-2: 98% (01/02 16:00 UTC, burst of batch eval jobs)
  cluster-euw-1: 93% (01/04 08:00 UTC, steady ramp coinciding with model rollout)
  cluster-use-3: 89% (01/03 20:00 UTC, customer A traffic spike)
  cluster-usw-4: 86% (01/05 14:00 UTC)
  cluster-aps-1: 84% (01/01 05:00 UTC)

- Anomalies flagged: 6 clusters with cyclical daypart surges exceeding baseline +25% and 3 clusters where queue depth rose above safe threshold (>= 120 reqs/min).

Representative export CSV snippet (columns: date,hour,region,cluster,pool,avg_gpu_pct,max_gpu_pct,queued_reqs):
2026-01-02,15,us-west,cluster-usw-2,shared-highmem,64,98,12
2026-01-02,16,us-west,cluster-usw-2,shared-highmem,78,98,56
2026-01-04,08,eu-west,cluster-euw-1,dedicated-small,72,93,139
2026-01-03,20,us-east,cluster-use-3,partner-pool,61,89,27

Key findings and immediate actionables:
1) us-west short spike (01/02 15-17 UTC)
   - Cause signal: correlated with two large scheduled batch eval jobs (deployments/rollout-checks) from QA and an external baseline test from partner-team. Jobs were mis-scheduled during the regional evening peak.
   - Mitigation taken: throttle QA pipeline, shift non-critical batch runs to off-peak windows; added temporary burst routing to next-available region for non-low-latency requests.
   - Recommendation: add soft holdback in CI deploy pipeline when regional two-hour rolling avg > 65%. See linked artifact JIRA-INFRA-8421.

2) eu-west steady ramp (01/04 morning)
   - Cause signal: new model variant (v2b) was rolled to a subset of customers without a canary cap; request qps increased 2x for 3 hours.
   - Mitigation: rolled immediate canary rollback to previous variant and invoked fallback model routing for critical customers.
   - Recommendation: require deploy gating rule: max ramp multiplier 1.3 per 30m for model variant rollouts. Update rollout config in repo PR #317.

3) Queues exceeding safe threshold
   - Clusters: cluster-euw-1, cluster-usw-2, cluster-use-3. Queue depth above 120 reqs/min during peaks; average latency increased 1.8x for affected requests.
   - Short-term: increase min-instances in those pools by 10% for 6 hours during predicted windows.
   - Medium-term: enable opportunistic pre-warm for pools with >20% burst probability (see daypart model below).

Daypart pattern analysis (hour-of-day heatmap summary):
- us-west: two peaks (15-18 UTC and 02-06 UTC local), indicative of mixed user bases and partner batch scheduling.
- eu-west: single morning ramp (07-10 UTC) coinciding with several European enterprise customers’ daily jobs.
- ap-south: shallow but steady overnight usage (00-06 UTC UTC), likely partners running batch evaluation.

Predictive buffer model (lightweight) used for short-term capacity suggestions:
- Inputs: rolling-7d mean, rolling-7d 95th pct, observed short-spike probability (fraction of days with >20% uplift).
- Buffer formula (applied per pool): suggested_buffer_percent = min(40, round( (95th_pct - mean)/2 + spike_prob*30 ))
- Example calculation (cluster-euw-1, dedicated-small): mean=62, 95th=88, spike_prob=0.4 -> buffer = min(40, round((88-62)/2 + 0.4*30)) = min(40, round(13 + 12)) = 25% extra capacity recommended.

Capacity recommendation summary (per-region):
- us-west: short-term +12% (targeted to two clusters: usw-2 and usw-4), enable burst route to us-east when >85%
- eu-west: short-term +18% on dedicated-small pools, require rollback / canary limits for model rollouts
- us-east: monitor partner traffic, +6% reserve on partner-pool during 19-22 UTC window
- ap-south: defer changes, observe after next billing cycle (utilization stable but near 60%)

Operational notes (how this export was generated):
- Data source: timeseries store (TSDB) and Redis queue-metrics aggregated by a scheduled airflow DAG (dags/gpu_surges/weekly_export).
- Query used (approx):
  SELECT date_trunc('hour', ts) as hour, region, cluster, pool, avg(gpu_pct) as avg_gpu_pct, max(gpu_pct) as max_gpu_pct, max(queue_depth) as queued_reqs
  FROM gpu_node_metrics WHERE ts >= '2025-12-29' AND ts < '2026-01-06' GROUP BY 1,2,3,4
- Export artifacts saved to: gs://redwood-ops-exports/gpu-surge/weeklies/2026-01-07/ (CSV + heatmap PNGs).

Tasks & owners (next 7 days):
- Marcus Patel: implement CI soft-holdback for QA pipelines (INFRA-8421) — due 2026-01-12.
- Lina Gómez: add canary ramp limit enforcement to rollout service and add unit tests — PR #317 review, due 2026-01-10.
- Ava Chen: schedule capacity bump for cluster-euw-1 and cluster-usw-2 (12% and 18% respectively) during predicted peak windows — complete by 2026-01-09.

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Are there linked scheduled jobs from partner teams that can be moved automatically to off-peak windows? (Slack thread: #partner-batch-sched)
- Can we add a per-pool pre-warm policy trigger based on the spike probability metric in the optimizer service?

Appendix: full 7d per-cluster CSV attached in export location. Heatmaps for each region and a short deck (3 slides) summarizing the causality events are in the same folder.

Notes to reader: this is a working export for infrastructure planning; numbers are preliminary and subject to minor aggregation adjustments. If you're using these figures for customer communication, coordinate with product and SRE for final validation.

- Ava Chen: schedule capacity bump for cluster-euw-1 and cluster-usw-2 (12% and 18% respectively) during predicted peak windows — complete by 2026-01-09.

title: Juniper SDK onramp labs - annotated notebook
preview:
Context and intent:
This is a working notebook for the Juniper SDK onramp labs. Juniper is a small, opinionated JavaScript + Python client intended for rapid prototyping with low setup friction (zero config devmode, simple token + env examples).

Goals (what a dev should reach by end of 30–60m lab):
- Get a token and call the hosted Redwood API with a minimal client (JS or Python)
- Use SDK streaming and structured output to build a tiny chat widget demo
- Instrument event-level telemetry for cost/latency and a simple quality check (prompt/response hash)
- Validate local failure modes (network loss, 429s, model fallback) and a retry policy

High-level quickstart flow (30 min path):
1) prereqs: node>=18 OR python3.10, curl, basic bash
2) create api key: Account -> Tokens -> Create temporary lab token (label: juniper-lab-YYYYMMDD)
3) clone sample repo: git clone git@github.com:redwood-inference/juniper-labs.git && cd juniper-labs
4) dev mode (zero config): npm run dev or ./venv/bin/python run_demo.py (both read JUNIPER_API_KEY env var)
5) open http://localhost:3000 -> type a prompt -> observe streaming tokens and telemetry in console

Concise SDK snippets (keep in README but repeated here for labs):
- JS (fetch streaming):
  const res = await fetch('https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Authorization': `Bearer ${process.env.JUNIPER_API_KEY}`, 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ model: 'redwood-base-v1', input: 'Summarize this text in one sentence.' })
  });
  // stream tokens -> append to UI

- Python (sync):
  import os, requests
  r = requests.post('https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate', headers={'Authorization': f"Bearer {os.environ['JUNIPER_API_KEY']}"}, json={'model':'redwood-base-v1','input':'Translate to french'})
  print(r.json())

Telemetry and events to capture (lab instrumentation checklist):
- user_prompt.submitted (evt): id, truncated prompt hash, prompt_length, dev_mode_bool
- request.sent: model, max_tokens, batch_size_hint, region_tag
- response.token_received: latency_ms (first_token, per_token), tokens_total, token_cost_estimate
- fallback.invoked: original_model, fallback_model, reason_code (rate_limit|oom|unhealthy)
- sdk.error: http_status, retry_attempts, error_code
- telemetry export sample payloads written to /tmp/juniper-telemetry.json by default for the labs

Smoke tests for lab routers (automated steps for CI):
- GET /health -> 200 and small json {"ok":true, "version":"juniper-lab-0.1"}
- sample generate request -> responds with status 200 and non-empty output within 3s (hosted run)
- streaming uninterrupted for 10 tokens -> check chunk ordering
- circuit breaker: simulate 30 qps sustained -> SDK should throttle and surface a friendly error within 2s

Failure modes & recommended dev guidance (what to teach in lab):
- 401 / misconfigured key: show error message mapping and how to rotate tokens
- 429 rate-limits: backoff recipe (jittered exponential), show warm-up step: reduce concurrency, enable caching
- 500 OOM / model error: fallback to lower-capacity model (redwood-mini-v1) with transparent notification in UI
- Network drop: local optimistic UI (show partial transcript) + enqueue retry in background

Mini lab exercises (each ~10–20m):
A. Build a chat widget that shows streaming tokens and an approximate per-request cost estimate (use token_count * model_unit_cost)
B. Add telemetry export and a tiny dashboard script that reads /tmp/juniper-telemetry.json and prints percentile latencies and top failure reasons
C. Implement a model-fallback policy: primary redwood-base-v1 -> fallback redwood-mini-v1 -> offline canned reply
D. Create a short eval prompt set (10 prompts) and run before/after quantization to observe quality drift

Template prompts and checks (to include in repo as tests):
- prompt: "Summarize the following commit message in 15 words or less:
---
{commit_text}"
- prompt: "Return a json object with keys 'intent' and 'confidence' given the text: {user_text}"
- check: compare response json keys, confidence range [0,1], non-empty intent

Lab scaffolding files to include in repo (minimal list):
- README.md (one-minute tl;dr + 30 min path)
- examples/js-chat-widget/ (live dev server with hot reload)
- examples/python-stub/ (CLI demo that prints streaming tokens to stdout)
- scripts/smoke.sh (curl-based health + sample request checks)
- telemetry/export_debugger.py (reads telemetry json and prints metrics)
- tests/eval_set.json (10 canonical prompts + expected patterns)

Onboarding checklist for instructors (workshop leaders):
- Pre-provision 20 lab tokens and email to registrants with expiry 72h
- Confirm devs have node/python installed (include a one-click codespace link in README)
- Walk through one failover scenario live (simulate 429)
- Reserve 15m for Q&A about telemetry and SLOs

Rollout / follow-ups (tickets to create after lab run):
- DEVX-1129: consolidate telemetry event definitions into a single schema + add to SDK docstrings
- DEVX-1130: add a 'juniper devmode' auto-mock that spins up a hermetic fake server for offline demos
- GH PR: sample repo -> add smoke.sh tests and CI job (gha: juniper-labs-smoke)

Open questions / TODOs (working list):
- Should telemetry be opt-in or opt-out for initial adopters? lean: opt-in with sample export enabled for labs only
- Does the SDK expose an opinionated retry policy or leave it to integrators? lean: ship simple policy + config knobs
- How to surface precise per-token cost in UI without exposing billing data? idea: local cost_estimate = unit_cost_table + token_count heuristic
- Accessibility checklist for the chat widget (screen-reader order for streaming tokens) -> need an accessibility review

Notes to self / rough timings for 60m workshop:
- 0–5m intro, goals, token handout
- 5–20m quickstart + run demo server
- 20–35m exercise A (chat widget)
- 35–50m exercise B + failure mode demos
- 50–60m wrap, Q&A, next steps

References and helpful links (for maintainers):
- GH: redwood-inference/juniper-labs (starter repo)
- Confluence: Juniper Onramp Playbook (workshop slides + slides notes)
- Internal: telemetry schema doc (linked_artifacts)

Last-minute pragmatic hacks for workshop day:
- Include a tiny js fallback that renders earliest tokens if streaming breaks (avoid blank UI)
- Pre-seed demo user state so registrants don't have to create accounts
- Have spare tokens and an alternate region config in case regional quota hits

If time after: optional stretch goals for advanced devs
- add a local latency simulator that injects artificial per-token delays to test UI resilience
- prototype an adapter for mobile: lightweight iOS/Android wrappers and memory patterns

End of notebook.

(ack: initial outline created during sprint-33 playtest; will refine after first 2 workshops)

title: edgecase-signal-aggregation-and-heuristic-mitigation-proposals
preview:
Summary:
This is a working deep-dive into a subset of onboarding evaluation failures that are low-frequency in the main metric slices but high-impact in product tests. Unlike previous clustering docs that focused on broad prompt archetypes, this note aggregates weak signals across multiple eval runs (stochastic seeds, sampling policies, decoding temperature sweep) to surface edge-case families. For each family I provide: short failure signature, representative prompts/responses, likely root cause, short-term heuristic mitigations, and medium-term engineering fixes and tests.

Context and why this matters:
- Model: open-llama-70b-quantized (candidate for hosted pipeline).
- Eval harness: onboarding-promptset-v6 (23K prompts across ecomm, search, QA, and LLM-as-code).
- Observed: global metrics passed baseline (perplexity, top-k metrics) but customer-sim tests reported 4 specific regressions causing data leakage, format corruption, or unsafe completions.
- Goal: produce pragmatic patches that can be added to the gating checklist and offer conservative rollout controls while long-term fixes are implemented.

Signal aggregation approach used here (short):
1) Collapse warm-start runs across 5 seeds and two decoding strategies (beam=5, temp sweep 0.0/0.7/1.0).
2) Generate per-example failure signals: truncated output, non-numeric tokens in numeric-only field, hallucinated external IDs (like real-looking bank/account numbers), and instruction-override.
3) Score signals by occurrence across seeds and severity weight (severity is operational: data-exfiltration > format corruption > quality drift).
4) Cluster using a lightweight hierarchical method (text-sim on prompt+response with cosine threshold 0.62, then manual consolidation).

Clusters surfaced (compact catalog):
1) "ID-glue hallucination"
   - Signature: model invents structured external identifiers (bank account, credit card, social security-like patterns) when asked to redact/transform user input.
   - Example prompt: "Redact any account numbers in the text; return sanitized text and a count."
   - Example model failure: returns sanitized text but also emits a new 12-digit numeric string prefixed by 'acct:' that was not in the input.
   - Likely root cause: model overgeneralizes examples where it was trained to 'normalize' or canonicalize IDs; during sampling it favors canonical-looking tokens.
   - Short-term heuristic: post-process filter that rejects outputs matching strict external-id regexes (e.g., CCN/SSN patterns) unless present verbatim in input. Implement a conservative allow-list: only accept numeric sequences that are substring of input.
   - Medium-term fix: add targeted fine-tune examples and a contrastive objective penalizing creation of new numeric sequences in redact tasks. Add prompt templates that explicitly instruct 'do not invent numbers'.

2) "CSV-format bleed"
   - Signature: output produces extra columns or escapes when a prompt asks for a 3-column CSV; model occasionally inserts an extra comma-separated quoted blob mid-row causing downstream CSV parser errors.
   - Example prompt: "Return results as CSV with columns: id,name,status"
   - Failure response: "123,\"Acme, Inc\",active,\"unexpected notes\""
   - Likely root cause: model tokenization treats embedded commas differently under context window edge cases; it sometimes continues commentary as part of the same row.
   - Short-term heuristic: validate CSV parseability; if parse fails, attempt deterministic re-generation with a low-temperature setting and a stricter template that uses explicit row markers (e.g., use \"|ROW_START|\" and join rows).
   - Medium-term fix: augment prompt-set with examples that emphasize delimiter-safe quoting and test harness that includes strict CSV parse checks as a gating step. Consider adding a serialization wrapper that asks model to return JSON instead of CSV when schema is strict.

3) "Instruction-contradiction flip"
   - Signature: model performs an opposite operation when prompt includes multiple negations or chained constraints (e.g., 'keep everything except X' -> removes X but also removes Y which was required).
   - Example prompt: "Translate to French, but do not translate proper nouns; keep dates as-is." Failure: translated proper nouns or incorrectly altered dates.
   - Likely root cause: conflicting constraint embedding in long prompts leading to attention dilution on negative tokens; beam search can prefer fluent but incorrect outputs.
   - Short-term heuristic: run a constraint-checker after generation (small classifier that verifies presence/absence of specified tokens or patterns). If the check fails, auto-regenerate with a simplified prompt that enumerates invariant examples.
   - Medium-term fix: experiment with constrained decoding (hard constraints for token spans) or incorporate a verification-stage that conditions a second model pass on the first output plus the constraint list to patch violations.

4) "Tool-response leakage"
   - Signature: model includes internal tooling traces or placeholders (e.g., 'CALL_TOOL://vec_search?id=...') in the final text when tool-calling was supposed to be internal.
   - Example: user asks 'summarize the doc' and model outputs summary plus a lingering instrumention tag.
   - Likely root cause: training data contained tool-response strings that weren't fully masked or model learned to surface internal markers. Also seen when VPC/private variants used different masking.
   - Short-term heuristic: strip known tool-token patterns via a sanitizer; add a covered list of internal prefixes that must not appear in outputs. Enforce in the API layer before returning to customer.
   - Medium-term fix: retrain/mask datasets, add explicit 'final_output' tokens in fine-tuning so model learns boundaries between system traces and user-facing content.

5) "Regression under low-context budget"
   - Signature: with short context windows (e.g., constrained by latency or edge devices), model omits essential qualifiers, leading to overconfident assertions. Occurs when prompt truncation removes context that disambiguates hedge language.
   - Example: prompt truncated: "Customer stated they're on Plan A, recommend upgrade if..." -> model recommends an incompatible plan.
   - Likely root cause: model relies on late-window context for qualifiers; truncation biases it toward dominant priors.
   - Short-term heuristic: detect truncated prompts (compare token count to expected prompt schema) and prepend a compact summary stub that preserves crucial qualifiers. Alternatively, fall back to a smaller specialized model trained on short-context reasoning for edge-mode.
   - Medium-term fix: incorporate context-robust training examples and distillation to improve short-window behavior. Add synthetic truncation training to prompt set.

Cross-cutting mitigation proposals (conservative, actionable):
- Response validators: implement a lightweight set of validators (regex, JSON schema, CSV parse, token presence/absence) that run synchronously in the API layer and can trigger deterministic retries or escalate to safe fallback model.
- Conservative decoding presets: for safety-sensitive routes enable low-temperature deterministic presets and beam constraints; make these configurable per route in the console.
- Prompt-layer anchors: systematically add an 'OUTPUT_BOUNDARY' marker in templates and instruct model to only emit content between markers; sanitize anything outside boundaries.
- Classifier gating: small binary classifiers (10-30M) trained to flag hallucination, format-violation, and privacy leaks. Use them to short-circuit and record telemetry.
- Patch prompt-set: curate ~400 targeted examples per cluster with negative demonstrations (showing what not to do) and integrate into fine-tune batches and RLHF micro-objectives.

Monitoring, tests and rollout strategy:
- New gating tests: add per-route pre-commit checks in onboarding that include 1) regex/parse validators, 2) classifier gating pass, and 3) seed diversity check (run on 3 seeds).
- Telemetry: add new event tags for detected edge-case types (id_glue, csv_bleed, instr_flip, tool_leak, truncation). Track rate per 1K requests and per-route.
- Canary rollout: enable mitigations as opt-in flags under a dedicated rollout called 'edgecase-guard-v1'. Start with 5% of traffic for newly onboarded customers, measure false positive rate and latency impact over 2 weeks.

Open questions and risks:
- False positives: validators and strict regexes can break benign creative responses—need to measure customer impact.
- Performance cost: classifier gating and re-generation increase tail latency; we need SLO assessments for high-QPS customers.
- Coverage: some failure families (creative hallucination) will not be fully addressable with heuristics and require model-level fixes.

Action items (owners and short timeline):
- A. Implement response validators in API layer (owner: Diego Morales) - prototype by 2025-11-25.
- B. Create negative-demo prompt corpus for clusters (owner: Priya Kapoor) - draft 400 examples by 2025-11-30.
- C. Small classifier baseline for id-glue detection (owner: Ellen Park) - train and evaluate by 2025-12-08.
- D. Add CSV parse gating and deterministic re-gen flow to onboarding harness (owner: Samir Jain) - integrate by 2025-12-05.
- E. Run canary 'edgecase-guard-v1' on new onboardings (owner: Product-hosted-api) - start 2025-12-12.

Notes to reviewers (what to look for):
- Please sanity-check the regex allow-list approach for id-glue; we prefer conservative (deny) by default but need an escape for legitimate large IDs.
- Are there existing privacy rules/contract obligations that forbid even transient creation of synthetic SSN-like tokens in outputs? If so, mark this high-priority.
- Would operations accept a small latency payout for classifier gating if it reduces incidence of high-severity breaches?

Appendix: quick examples (representative prompts and exact failed outputs observed across seeds):
- Prompt: "Redact numbers: 'Invoice 39485732 paid'"
  Failure: "Invoice [REDACTED] paid. New id: 39485731"
- Prompt: "Return CSV rows: id,name,status -> 12,\"Acme, Inc\",active"
  Failure: "12,\"Acme, Inc\",active,\"See notes: requires follow-up\""
- Prompt: "Summarize and do not include internal tool markers"
  Failure: "Summary: ... CALL_TOOL://vec_search?id=ux123"

If you disagree with any cluster labeling or want to split/merge, leave inline comments in this doc. This is intentionally lightweight and action-oriented so eng and product can pick up immediate heuristics while we design robust model-level remedies.

   - Example: prompt truncated: "Customer stated they're on Plan A, recommend upgrade if..." -> model recommends an incompatible plan.
   - Likely root cause: model relies on late-window context for qualifiers; truncation biases it toward dominant priors.
   - Short-term heuristic: detect truncated prompts (compare token count to expected prompt schema) and prepend a compact summary stub that preserves crucial qualifiers. Alternatively, fall back to a smaller specialized model trained on short-context reasoning for edge-mode.
   - Medium-term fix: incorporate context-robust training examples and distillation to improve short-window behavior. Add synthetic truncation training to prompt set.

Cross-cutting mitigation proposals (conservative, actionable):
- Response validators: implement a lightweight set of validators (regex, JSON schema, CSV parse, token presence/absence) that run synchronously in the API layer and can trigger deterministic retries or escalate to safe fallback model.
- Conservative decoding presets: for safety-sensitive routes enable low-temperature deterministic presets and beam constraints; make these configurable per route in the console.
- Prompt-layer anchors: systematically add an 'OUTPUT_BOUNDARY' marker in templates and instruct model to only emit content between markers; sanitize anything outside boundaries.
- Classifier gating: small binary classifiers (10-30M) trained to flag hallucination, format-violation, and privacy leaks. Use them to short-circuit and record telemetry.
- Patch prompt-set: curate ~400 targeted examples per cluster with negative demonstrations (showing what not to do) and integrate into fine-tune batches and RLHF micro-objectives.

Monitoring, tests and rollout strategy:
- New gating tests: add per-route pre-commit checks in onboarding that include 1) regex/parse validators, 2) classifier gating pass, and 3) seed diversity check (run on 3 seeds).
- Telemetry: add new event tags for detected edge-case types (id_glue, csv_bleed, instr_flip, tool_leak, truncation). Track rate per 1K requests and per-route.
- Canary rollout: enable mitigations as opt-in flags under a dedicated rollout called 'edgecase-guard-v1'. Start with 5% of traffic for newly onboarded customers, measure false positive rate and latency impact over 2 weeks.

Open questions and risks:
- False positives: validators and strict regexes can break benign creative responses—need to measure customer impact.
- Performance cost: classifier gating and re-generation increase tail latency; we need SLO assessments for high-QPS customers.
- Coverage: some failure families (creative hallucination) will not be fully addressable with heuristics and require model-level fixes.

Action items (owners and short timeline):
- A. Implement response validators in API layer (owner: Diego Morales) - prototype by 2025-11-25.
- B. Create negative-demo prompt corpus for clusters (owner: Priya Kapoor) - draft 400 examples by 2025-11-30.
- C. Small classifier baseline for id-glue detection (owner: Ellen Park) - train and evaluate by 2025-12-08.
- D. Add CSV parse gating and deterministic re-gen flow to onboarding harness (owner: Samir Jain) - integrate by 2025-12-05.
- E. Run canary 'edgecase-guard-v1' on new onboardings (owner: Product-hosted-api) - start 2025-12-12.

Notes to reviewers (what to look for):
- Please sanity-check the regex allow-list approach for id-glue; we prefer conservative (deny) by default but need an escape for legitimate large IDs.
- Are there existing privacy rules/contract obligations that forbid even transient creation of synthetic SSN-like tokens in outputs? If so, mark this high-priority.
- Would operations accept a small latency payout for classifier gating if it reduces incidence of high-severity breaches?

Appendix: quick examples (representative prompts and exact failed outputs observed across seeds):
- Prompt: "Redact numbers: 'Invoice 39485732 paid'"
  Failure: "Invoice [REDACTED] paid. New id: 39485731"
- Prompt: "Return CSV rows: id,name,status -> 12,\"Acme, Inc\",active"
  Failure: "12,\"Acme, Inc\",active,\"See notes: requires follow-up\""
- Prompt: "Summarize and do not include internal tool markers"
  Failure: "Summary: ... CALL_TOOL://vec_search?id=ux123"

If you disagree with any cluster labeling or want to split/merge, leave inline comments in this doc. This is intentionally lightweight and action-oriented so eng and product can pick up immediate heuristics while we design robust model-level remedies.

title: quiet-launch-15-day-skill-anchoring-and-interview-evidence-kit
preview:
Context and intent:
This is a compact, low-friction onboarding and interview-evidence bundle intended for small IC hires (engineer / infra / platform) who will be given a quiet/solo launch for their first 15 days. The goal is to reduce cognitive load, create a defensible skill signal for cross-functional partners, and accelerate contributor impact while keeping mentor time bounded. Draft for People Ops + hiring manager alignment before piloting with 2 hires.

High-level goals:
- First 15 days: remove >80% of non-essential access friction, ship 1 microtask to prod or customer-facing integration, and capture structured evidence for performance calibration.
- Mentor time budget: 3–4 hours/week active pairing + asynchronous feedback.
- Hiring alignment: ensure interview evidence maps to day-1–15 success signals so we can correlate interview rubric to ramp outcomes.

Acceptance criteria for quiet launch pilot (success signals):
- New hire completes infra onboarding checklist and deploys microtask within 10 business days.
- Hiring manager rates 5/7 on independence and 6/7 on code quality in the day-15 pulse.
- Mentor confirms at least one asynchronous code review with constructive feedback captured in the evidence log.
- No more than 2 outstanding access tickets remain after day 3.

Pre-hire checklist (People Ops pre-reqs):
- Confirm offer cadence and day-0 equipment shipment date.
- Provision core accounts (G Suite, Okta, Redwood VPN, internal dev org) before day 0.
- Create onboarding calendar invite series: day0-7 sync, day8-15 ramp check.
- Assign primary mentor and buddy (names + Slack handles).
- Add to onboarding tracker and email hiring manager + infra SRE for early context.

Interview evidence mapping (how interview signals connect to day-15 outcomes):
- System design exercise -> ability to own microtask architecture and write rollout steps.
- Pair-programming diff -> independent PR hygiene, basic CI fixes.
- Behavioral probe (autonomy) -> escalation choices and communication frequency.
- Coding test -> passes baseline unit tests and can extend an existing test.

15-day onboarding plan (candidate-facing summary):
Day 0: welcome + infra access validation
- 30m welcome call (People Ops): logistics, payroll, benefits links, timezone scheduling.
- 60m manager 1:1 (context, team norms, immediate goals).
- Access validation (Okta, VPN, repo, CI access).
- Quick wins doc: microtask suggestion list (3 scoped tasks).

Day 1–3: environment & microtask pick
- Follow the 5-step dev-env checklist and verify by opening first issue.
- Mentor pairs for 1h to walk codebase layout and CI.
- Pick microtask and draft implementation plan in PR template.
- Evidence capture: create the evidence-card in Google Drive (link in tracker).

Day 4–9: implement & iterate
- Daily async updates in #onboard-[name] channel (3 bullets max).
- One 60m pair-review session mid-week with mentor.
- Open PR with tests and follow CI until green.
- QA + canary deploy to staging if available.

Day 10–15: canary, handoff, and retrospective
- Coordinate short canary with PM/QA (10–30m).
- Finalize docs: README snippet + rollout steps.
- Day-15 review: manager + mentor + People Ops quick sync (30m) to fill scored pulse.
- Publish evidence-card (artifact list: PR link, test run screenshot, deployment log excerpt).

Mentor & manager responsibilities (timeboxed):
- Mentor: 3–4 hours/week (pairing + reviews + 2 async notes).
- Manager: 30m twice (day0 & day15), ad-hoc priority alignment.
- People Ops: 30m day0 setup, 30m day15 retrospective.

Interview rubric (compact version we use to correlate to ramp):
Format: each probe scored 1–5 with a short evidence sentence.
- Technical breadth (system design, 1–5): assesses ability to pick appropriate tradeoffs for small services. Evidence required: design diagram or bullet tradeoffs.
- Code hygiene and tests (1–5): quality of PRs, test coverage. Evidence required: example PR link.
- Autonomous debugging (1–5): ability to triage and fix simple incidents. Evidence required: incident note or postmortem fragment.
- Communication & async updates (1–5): clarity of status updates and issue descriptions. Evidence required: Slack thread or issue comment.
- Cultural fit / prioritize (1–5): alignment to team norms and prioritization choices. Evidence required: short manager note.

Scoring guidance (quick):
- 4–5: candidate demonstrates clear signals and minimal coaching required for microtask work.
- 3: can do with some light mentoring; target for initial hire bucket.
- 1–2: mismatch for quiet-launch; escalate to manager for expanded onboarding plan.

Evidence-card template (one-pager we store per hire):
- PR(s) delivered: <urls>
- Tests + CI status: <summary>
- Deploy log excerpt: <snippet>
- Mentor notes: <bulleted lessons learned>
- Manager pulse scores: technical, autonomy, communication (numbers + 1-sentence rationale)
- Open friction tickets (list)

People Ops quick forms (to reduce back-and-forth):
- Day0 confirmation email template (copyable): Hi <name>, welcome — equipment and initial access details ...
- Day15 pulse form link placeholder (we will wire this to short Google Form)
- Access escalation instruction: if repo access fails, ping #infra-oncall with subject 'onboard-access <hire-name>'.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: candidate blocked on hardware or VPN for >48 hours. Mitigation: overnight backup cloud image for dev tasks, or schedule remote pairing with environment pre-provisioned.
- Risk: mentor bandwidth drops. Mitigation: assign secondary buddy and fallback office hours time-block.
- Risk: interview rubric mismatch. Mitigation: capture granular evidence early and run a 30-day correlation review for pilot hires.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Finalize small-bounty microtask list per team (ask eng leads). (TODO: Priya)
- Wire Day15 pulse to People Ops form and autopopulate into hire tracker. (TODO: Rafael)
- Agree on 2 pilot hires (who and when) and share metrics baseline. (TODO: Samir + hiring managers)

Appendix: quick templates (copy into offer/onboard sequences)
- Subject: Welcome to Redwood — Day 0 checklist
  Body: Hi <name> — congrats. Please find your day-0 checklist below... [link to drive card]

- Day15 pulse sample (questions):
  1) Did the hire ship the microtask? Y/N
  2) Rate independence 1–7
  3) Note one clear improvement area

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep this intentionally lightweight. We expect variance across teams; the pilot's point is to measure how interview signals map to ramp, not to enforce a strict regimen.
- If pilot shows high mentor load, move to an extended 30-day quiet launch with more timeboxed mentor hours.

Last updated: 2025-11-18 by Ava Chen
