title: HITL-fallback-trigger-eval-playbook
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook defines an evaluation suite and prompt corpus for human-in-the-loop (HITL) fallback triggers in multi-step toolchains and function-calling scenarios. The goal is to a) identify when an LLM should delegate to a human, b) ensure the trigger criteria minimize false positives while catching edge-case failures, and c) provide a regression test harness that tracks performance across model versions and runtime configs.

Scope and motivation:
- Scope: tool selection accuracy, argument parsing correctness, multi-step flow completion, and HITL fallback decision quality. Covers hosted API and Dedicated inference paths; excludes private on-prem deployment-specific network/ops constraints for now.
- Why: We continue to see subtle regressions where models either over-defer (too many unnecessary human escalations) or under-defer (miss real failure cases). Without a formalized corpus and thresholds we cannot reliably detect regressions in CI.

High-level objectives (measurable):
1) Tool selection precision >= 94% on curated acceptance set.
2) Argument parse exact-match >= 92% on canonical arg formats, and arg-F1 >= 0.95 for partial matches.
3) Multi-step end-to-end success (correct tool outputs at each step) >= 88% on complex flows (3+ tools).
4) HITL trigger ROC-AUC >= 0.90 on labeled binary decision set, and operating point with false positive rate <= 6% at recall >= 0.90.
5) Latency impact when HITL is invoked should be measured (median added latency) and kept within runbook thresholds for interactive UIs.

Definitions and metrics:
- Tool selection accuracy: whether the first tool call chosen by the model matches the ground truth label for a prompt. Multi-tool choices count as correct if chosen set contains the ground truth primary tool.
- Argument parse exact-match: full string/JSON equality compared to canonical argument representation (after whitespace normalization).
- Arg-F1: token-level F1 over serialized key/value tokenization for best-effort partial credit.
- Stepwise success: each step in a multi-step flow must succeed (output meets task oracle). We report per-step and flow-level metrics.
- HITL trigger: binary label (escalate=yes/no) created by expert annotators. We evaluate classifier metrics and calibration.

Corpus composition and labeling:
- Acceptance set (1,200 examples): high-quality, hand-labeled examples across three buckets: simple (400), medium (500), complex (300).
  - Simple: single-tool calls, canonical args, short prompts.
  - Medium: ambiguous tool choice, optional args, conditional steps.
  - Complex: multi-step flows (3+ tool calls), argument composition, context carries across steps, adversarial paraphrases.
- Edge-case addendum (250 examples): intentionally malformed args, truncated JSON bodies, mixed encodings, very long instruction prefixes, and noisy user messages.
- HITL decision set (1,000 examples): balanced positive/negative labels for escalation needs (500/500) drawn from production sampling and simulated failure injections.
- Regression track set (500 examples): stable core cases that must be re-evaluated every model push; seeded with past-regression triggers.

Prompt formats / examples (canonicalized):
- Example 1 (simple tool):
  \"\"\"
  User: \"Schedule a 30m sync with engineering next Wednesday at 10am. Use calendar.create_event with title 'Sync: infra' and attendees [alice@example.com].\"
  Expected: call calendar.create_event(title='Sync: infra', start='2025-12-03T10:00:00-08:00', duration_minutes=30, attendees=['alice@example.com'])
  HITL: no
  \"\"\"

- Example 2 (ambiguous tool selection):
  \"\"\"
  User: \"Find out why the nightly job failed and create a ticket if it's a recurring error.\"
  Ground truth: run diagnostics.run_job and if error.pattern_count>1 then ticket.create(summary=..., priority='P2')
  Failure modes: model picks ticket.create only, or mis-parses diagnostics output schema.
  HITL: yes (when diagnostics output is ambiguous)
  \"\"\"

- Example 3 (multi-step / composed args):
  \"\"\"
  User: \"Pull the last 10 error logs for service X, summarize main exception types, and for the top exception, open a triage doc with affected endpoints and a rollback flag if more than 5% error rate.\"
  Expected flow: logs.fetch(service='X', limit=10) -> logs.summarize(...) -> metrics.query(...) -> docs.create(title=..., body=...) -> optionally rollout.set_flag('service-X', rollback=true)
  HITL: yes (if metrics.query results are near threshold or log parsing uncertain)
  \"\"\"

Annotation instructions (for labelers):
- Provide canonical tool and arg formats; normalize timestamps to ISO; preserve canonical enum values.
- For ambiguous prompts, annotate the most conservative automated action (what the system should do without human) and separately whether a human should be involved.
- For multi-step flows, annotate per-step oracle output; mark which step causes escalation if any.

HITL trigger taxonomy (candidate signals):
1) Low-confidence model outputs (calibrated prob < 0.35)
2) Argument schema violations (missing required keys, type mismatch)
3) Negative tool execution feedback (tool error codes) during simulation
4) Ambiguity in tool selection (top-2 tool prob gap < 0.10)
5) Safety / PII heuristics triggered in args or outputs
6) Cross-step contradiction (later step input contradicts earlier step output)

Decision logic prototype (simplified):
- If any schema violation OR tool-error -> escalate.
- Else if model_confidence<conf_threshold OR top2_gap<threshold_top2_gap -> escalate when combined with medium/complex flow.
- Else run simulated dry-run (where possible) and escalate on simulated error.

Evaluation harness and CI integration:
- Harness: python test runner that can spin up model variants via the Redwood API harness, apply prompts, and record structured signals (tool label, args, confidence, simulated tool stderr/stdout). Outputs are compared against oracle annotations.
- Regression integration: each PR that touches model config, parsing logic, or the inference runtime should run the full acceptance set. CI step publishes a JSON metrics summary to S3 and creates/updates JIRA-ALPHA-982 with delta flags when thresholds degrade > 2% absolute for primary metrics.
- Nightly job: run full acceptance + edge-case addendum; post to a Slack channel and generate a weekly digest table for product/ops.

Alerting and runbook thresholds:
- If tool selection precision drops by >3% from baseline on regression track set -> pager to eng-applied-ml on-call.
- If HITL false positive rate increases >4% at operating point -> create incident and roll back to previous validated model version.

Calibration and operating point selection:
- Use the HITL decision set to compute ROC and choose an operating point that meets false positive constraint while keeping recall >= 0.90. Recompute monthly as model changes.

Adversarial & fuzz tests (automated):
- JSON truncation mutations, value-type swaps, long-prefix paraphrase injection, html-encoded args, mixed-encoding prompts.
- Run in CI with randomized seed; sample failures are added to edge-case addendum after triage.

Human annotation QA process:
- Dual-annotate 20% of new labels for agreement checks. Disagreement >10% triggers label adjudication by senior annotator.
- Maintain label-change log with rationale (linked to jira).

Regression reporting format (example fields):
- model_tag, run_id, date, tool_selection_precision, arg_exact_match, arg_f1, flow_success_rate, hitl_precision, hitl_recall, latency_median_ms, notes
- Persist bullet diffs in Confluence and store CSV for product reviews.

Rollout plan for new models / rules:
1) Run acceptance + edge-case locally and on CI.
2) Canary on 5% traffic for hosted API with HITL logging enabled. Monitor metrics for 48h.
3) If degradation <= threshold, expand canary to 25% for 24h.
4) Full rollout with continued daily regression checks for a week.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Formalize simulation environment for tool dry-runs in Dedicated infra (ticketed: JIRA-ALPHA-1011).
- Decide whether to include private deploys' operational failures into the default edge-case addendum.
- Add quantitative latency budgets by product surface (chat vs async batch).

Appendix: quick checklist for adding new prompts to the corpus:
- New prompt added to draft.csv with owner, complexity tag, expected oracle, and HITL label.
- Two annotators annotate and QA step executed.
- Example added to appropriate bucket (acceptance/edge/regression).
- CI test updated with new sample id and regression baseline recorded.

Contact / owners: Ava Chen (owner) - avachen@redwood.ai; applied-ml on-call: eng-applied-ml@pagerduty.com.

Notes: This is a working doc and intended to collect engineering decisions and prompt examples for the HITL trigger evaluation. Feedback welcome — drop comments or link PRs.

Annotation instructions (for labelers):
- Provide canonical tool and arg formats; normalize timestamps to ISO; preserve canonical enum values.
- For ambiguous prompts, annotate the most conservative automated action (what the system should do without human) and separately whether a human should be involved.
- For multi-step flows, annotate per-step oracle output; mark which step causes escalation if any.

HITL trigger taxonomy (candidate signals):
1) Low-confidence model outputs (calibrated prob < 0.35)
2) Argument schema violations (missing required keys, type mismatch)
3) Negative tool execution feedback (tool error codes) during simulation
4) Ambiguity in tool selection (top-2 tool prob gap < 0.10)
5) Safety / PII heuristics triggered in args or outputs
6) Cross-step contradiction (later step input contradicts earlier step output)

Decision logic prototype (simplified):
- If any schema violation OR tool-error -> escalate.
- Else if model_confidence<conf_threshold OR top2_gap<threshold_top2_gap -> escalate when combined with medium/complex flow.
- Else run simulated dry-run (where possible) and escalate on simulated error.

Evaluation harness and CI integration:
- Harness: python test runner that can spin up model variants via the Redwood API harness, apply prompts, and record structured signals (tool label, args, confidence, simulated tool stderr/stdout). Outputs are compared against oracle annotations.
- Regression integration: each PR that touches model config, parsing logic, or the inference runtime should run the full acceptance set. CI step publishes a JSON metrics summary to S3 and creates/updates JIRA-ALPHA-982 with delta flags when thresholds degrade > 2% absolute for primary metrics.
- Nightly job: run full acceptance + edge-case addendum; post to a Slack channel and generate a weekly digest table for product/ops.

Alerting and runbook thresholds:
- If tool selection precision drops by >3% from baseline on regression track set -> pager to eng-applied-ml on-call.
- If HITL false positive rate increases >4% at operating point -> create incident and roll back to previous validated model version.

Calibration and operating point selection:
- Use the HITL decision set to compute ROC and choose an operating point that meets false positive constraint while keeping recall >= 0.90. Recompute monthly as model changes.

Adversarial & fuzz tests (automated):
- JSON truncation mutations, value-type swaps, long-prefix paraphrase injection, html-encoded args, mixed-encoding prompts.
- Run in CI with randomized seed; sample failures are added to edge-case addendum after triage.

Human annotation QA process:
- Dual-annotate 20% of new labels for agreement checks. Disagreement >10% triggers label adjudication by senior annotator.
- Maintain label-change log with rationale (linked to jira).

Regression reporting format (example fields):
- model_tag, run_id, date, tool_selection_precision, arg_exact_match, arg_f1, flow_success_rate, hitl_precision, hitl_recall, latency_median_ms, notes
- Persist bullet diffs in Confluence and store CSV for product reviews.

Rollout plan for new models / rules:
1) Run acceptance + edge-case locally and on CI.
2) Canary on 5% traffic for hosted API with HITL logging enabled. Monitor metrics for 48h.
3) If degradation <= threshold, expand canary to 25% for 24h.
4) Full rollout with continued daily regression checks for a week.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Formalize simulation environment for tool dry-runs in Dedicated infra (ticketed: JIRA-ALPHA-1011).
- Decide whether to include private deploys' operational failures into the default edge-case addendum.
- Add quantitative latency budgets by product surface (chat vs async batch).

Appendix: quick checklist for adding new prompts to the corpus:
- New prompt added to draft.csv with owner, complexity tag, expected oracle, and HITL label.
- Two annotators annotate and QA step executed.
- Example added to appropriate bucket (acceptance/edge/regression).
- CI test updated with new sample id and regression baseline recorded.

Contact / owners: Ava Chen (owner) - avachen@redwood.ai; applied-ml on-call: eng-applied-ml@pagerduty.com.

Notes: This is a working doc and intended to collect engineering decisions and prompt examples for the HITL trigger evaluation. Feedback welcome — drop comments or link PRs.

title: Recruit prioritization matrix and probe bank
preview:
Purpose: quick decision-support for short-term hiring sprints (2–6 weeks) that balances business impact, role difficulty, and candidate fill-time. Working doc: use this during weekly recruiting syncs and interview calibration sessions.

Notes to self / context:
- Many teams need faster TTM for high-leverage ICs (infra, serving runtime, SRE). We only have limited recruiter hours and interview slots.
- Objective: create a lightweight, repeatable way to prioritize reqs and a small bank of behavioral probes that maps to our competency rubric.
- Keep interview probes short (30–90s read) so interviewers can land follow-ups rapidly.

1) Recruit Prioritization matrix (one-pager)
- Inputs (for each open req): business impact (1–5), time-to-value (weeks estimate), role difficulty (1–5), market scarcity (1–5), internal ramp cost (1–5).
- Scoring: weighted sum = (impact * 3) + (1 / time-to-value * 2) + (5 - role difficulty) + (5 - scarcity) + (5 - ramp_cost). The math is intentionally simple; higher = higher priority.
- Quick thresholds (for sprint):
  - Green: score >= 35 -> immediate top push (expedite interviews, offer lanes)
  - Amber: 25–34 -> standard cadence, increase sourcing if stagnates
  - Red: <25 -> deprioritize / freeze outbound until later sprint

Example (mock):
- Req: Serving Runtime Senior Engineer
  - business impact 5, time-to-value 6 weeks, role difficulty 5, scarcity 5, ramp cost 3
  - score = (5*3) + (1/6*2~=0) + (0) + (0) + (2) => ~17 -> Amber/Red -> consider warming pipeline but focus on internal mobility + stretch hires

2) Interview funnel micro-adjustments for sprint hires
- Slot budget per week (example): 8 first-round, 4 second-round, 2 final/offers. Reallocate from low-priority reqs when green flagged.
- Fast-track rules: if candidate meets >4 on both technical and product sense AND scorecard >= 18/25 from first-round, promote directly to hiring manager loop (skip take-home unless conflicting signals).

3) Core competency rubric (summary for scoring)
- Technical craft (0–5): depth of system knowledge, debugging pattern, architecture tradeoffs.
- Product sense & tradeoffs (0–5): ability to weigh lat/cost/reliability tradeoffs, user impact focus.
- Collaboration & ownership (0–5): async communication, cross-team influence, incident ownership examples.
- Learning velocity & humility (0–5): concrete examples of learning hard concepts quickly, code review examples.
- Communication clarity (0–5): ability to explain complex things simply.

Scoring note: aim for 3+ on at least three categories for mid-level ICs; 4+ across categories for senior.

4) Behavioral probe bank (30+ short probes grouped by competency)
- Technical craft probes:
  - \"Tell me about a time you diagnosed a production latency issue end-to-end. What data did you collect first?\"
  - \"Describe a decision you made to sacrifice latency for cost or vice versa. What tradeoffs did you test?\"
  - \"When you learned a new infra concept under time pressure, what did you read/try first?\"

- Product & tradeoffs probes:
  - \"Give an example where you pushed back on a product request for technical reasons. How did you articulate risk to PMs?\"
  - \"How have you prioritized performance work that benefits multiple teams vs a single team?\"

- Collaboration & ownership probes:
  - \"Walk me through a time you led a postmortem. How did you ensure blamelessness?\"
  - \"Describe a cross-functional disagreement you resolved. What concessions did you make?\"

- Learning & humility probes:
  - \"Tell me about a technical area you previously misunderstood. How did you correct course?\"
  - \"Give an example of feedback you received that changed how you work.\"

- Communication clarity probes:
  - \"Explain a complex system you built to a non-technical stakeholder in two minutes.\"
  - \"Describe an instance where you simplified a design to make it easier for the team to maintain.\"

Probe usage guidance:
- Pick 1–2 probes per competency for a 45m first-round. Reserve deeper scenario probes (root-cause debugging, architecture tradeoff) for second-round.
- Use follow-up template: \"What was the problem? What options did you evaluate? What did you choose? What changed after?\"

5) Fast scorecard template (copyable for interviewers)
- Section: notes (free text)
- Scores: Technical craft / Product sense / Collaboration / Learning velocity / Communication (0–5 each)
- Recommendation: Strong hire / Hire / Borderline / No-hire (with 1–2 line rationale)

6) Onboarding mini-checklist for sprint hires (first 30 days, minimal viable ops)
- Pre-day 0: clear repo access, seeded runbook links, 1:1 schedule with manager and scribe assigned, first-week micro-project defined (small measurable deliverable).
- Week 1: local dev loop, pager readouts, join team slack channels, pair with buddy on first PR, run a small infra smoke test.
- Week 2–4: rotate through two key services, shadow on-call, demo micro-project to team at end of 30 days.

7) Quick templates (email/interview cadence)
- Outbound short message: \"Quick intro — we're fast-moving on [team]. Looking for engineers who ship infra improvements and care about latency/cost. Would you be open to a 30m chat this week?\"
- Interview schedule (example): 30m intro + cultural fit -> 60m technical deep-dive -> 45m system design -> brief hiring manager debrief (internal).

8) Open questions / unknowns (to resolve at next sync)
- Weighting formula needs calibration across 3 hiring managers (Priya, Diego, Riley) — propose running retrospective next sprint with five closed reqs.
- Need recruiter time allocation dashboard (can recruiting ops expose live slots?). JIRA ticket created (JIRA-ENG-7812).
- Candidate score normalization: propose pilot where three interviewers score same 2 candidates each sprint to quantify inter-rater variance.

9) Next steps (owners & timeline)
- Priya: run priority matrix for active 16 reqs by Thu (owner: Priya) — due 2025-11-07.
- Diego: add 10 probes into rubric doc in Confluence and map to competency tags (owner: Diego) — due 2025-11-10.
- Riley: schedule recruiter sync to reallocate slots for top-tier green reqs (owner: Riley) — due 2025-11-12.

Roughs / TODOs / scratch notes:
- Consider adding a short take-home for senior infra roles only; default = no take-home unless candidate is borderline or distributed remote and we need deeper signal.
- Experiment: 15m \"ops triage\" exercise in first-round for candidates who will join on-call (timebox scenario).

Footer: this is a living document — copy into team folder when mature. Keep probes short and conversational.

title: talent-rotation-rubric-and-90-day-buddy-sprint
preview:
Context and purpose:
This is a working doc for an experimental cross-role \"talent rotation\" hiring and onboarding pattern intended for early-stage hires and lateral ICs joining cross-functional pods. The goal is to: shorten time-to-productive, preserve hiring signal via a structured interview/debrief rubric, and validate fit quickly with a 90-day buddy/rotation sprint instead of a traditional 1:1 onboarding loop.

Who this is for:
- IC hires (SWE, ML infra, Platform, SRE, DevRel) at IC2–IC4 levels where initial scope is pod-level.
- Hiring managers who want a repeatable debrief template and scoring guidance.
- People Ops and Hiring Committee as an evidence package for fast offers.

High-level flow (timeline):
1) Screen -> take-home (optional) -> panel interviews (2 rounds) -> decision within 7 business days.
2) Offer accepted -> preboarding (one-week pre-arrival checklist).
3) Day 1–7: orientation + paired shadow sessions with assigned buddy.
4) Week 2–6: rotational sprints (2-week micro-projects across platform, infra, and product touchpoints).
5) Week 7–12: owner of a small scoped ticket and final presentation to pod + hiring manager.
6) Week 12: 90-day debrief and final confirmation for long-term placement.

Interview rubric (single-sheet guidance for interviewers):
Use 1–5 scale where 3 = meets expectations for role level, 4 = comfortably exceeds, 2 = below expectations but trainable, 1 = unacceptable / critical gap, 5 = exceptional.
Scoring axes (short definitions and sample prompts):
- Technical competence (depth): Can they reason about core system components? Prompt: \"Walk me through a recent design or debugging incident.\"
- Architectural judgment (system design): Trade-offs and capacity planning; prompt: \"Design a shard+cache strategy for a high-throughput KV workload.\"
- Problem decomposition (clarity of thought & experiment design): Prompt: \"How would you instrument and iterate on a flakey inference pipeline?\"
- Collaboration & communication: Cross-team empathy, conflict handling; prompt: \"Tell me about a time you changed a product decision after stakeholder feedback.\"
- Ownership & execution: Drive from idea to delivery; prompt: \"Describe a time you delivered a project with unclear requirements.\"
- Learning velocity & curiosity: Evidence of rapid ramp; prompt: \"What's a technical skill you taught yourself in <3 months and how did you apply it?\"
- Culture add / values alignment: Redwood values (ship, measure, iterate) — call out any red flags.

Scoring guidance (example):
- 5: Strong, specific evidence + impact metrics + shaping of team processes.
- 4: Multiple strong examples, measurable impact, minor gaps in breadth.
- 3: Solid on most axes, limited scale examples appropriate for level.
- 2: Has some skill but no clear impact or requires significant ramp.
- 1: Lack of required fundamentals or unsafe behaviors.

Debrief template (use in interviewer sync):
- Candidate name / role: 
- Interviewer: 
- Date/time: 
- Summary (2–3 sentences): quick synopsis of impression.
- Strengths (bulleted): concrete examples.
- Concerns / gaps (bulleted): clear mitigations or red flags.
- Role fit recommendation: hire / neutral / no-hire.
- Level recommendation: IC2 / IC3 / IC4 / escalate to manager.
- Required follow-ups: 1–2 targeted references or clarifying questions for an extra loop.

Evidence package for hiring committee (what to attach):
- Completed debriefs from at least 3 interviewers across disciplines.
- Example deliverable (take-home or past project repo) or code snippet with short explanation.
- Candidate-supplied learning plan or growth goals (optional).

90-day buddy rotation checklist (Day-level and goal-oriented):
Preboarding (week before start):
- Set up accounts + access matrix assigned by IT (list of required systems).
- Intro email + calendar invites for Day 1 + buddy pairing.
- Pre-read: short product/infra primer pack (2–3 pages).

Day 1–7: first impressions and shadowing
- Day 1: orientation, lunch with buddy, team standup, quick wins ticket assigned.
- Days 2–4: paired sessions: one-half day with infra lead, one-half day with product PM, one-half day with SRE.
- Day 5: 1:1 with hiring manager to clarify expectations and goals.

Week 2–6: rotational sprints (two-week cycles)
- Sprint 1 (Week 2–3): Diagnostics sprint — replicate a production incident in staging (mentored). Deliverable: postmortem-style note + remediation plan.
- Sprint 2 (Week 4–5): Feature-adjacent sprint — contribute a small feature/bug fix end-to-end. Deliverable: PR + short demo.
- Optional Sprint 3 (Week 6): Ops-focused sprint — create a dashboard or alert tuning doc. Deliverable: metric dashboard + runbook snippet.

Week 7–12: owner transition and final output
- Week 7–9: ownership of a small scoped ticket (same tech stack as pod). Deliverable: shipped change and monitoring.
- Week 10: prep final 15-min demo and a 1-page reflection (what went well, what I need).
- Week 12: 90-day review meeting (hiring manager + buddy + people ops). Decision: confirm placement / extend probation / reassign.

Success metrics for the experiment (quantitative + qualitative):
- Quant: Time-to-first-meaningful-merge (target <21 days), Number of production incidents caused by new hire (target 0 in first 90 days), % of hires confirmed to long-term placement after 90 days (target >85%).
- Qual: Hiring manager satisfaction score, buddy satisfaction score, candidate NPS after 90 days.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Candidate gets siloed; Mitigation: mandatory cross-pairing schedule and weekly rotation retrospective.
- Risk: Lack of hiring signal due to short interviews; Mitigation: mandatory sample deliverable + ref check targeted at recent project.
- Risk: Manager capacity to mentor; Mitigation: cap rotations per manager to 2 simultaneous new joiners and stipend for buddy time.

Open questions / TODOs (short-term):
- Define concrete access checklist for private deployments team (security approvals timeline).
- Draft a one-page preboarding packet tailored for ML infra hires.
- Pilot with 4 hires in Q1 2026 and measure success metrics.

Appendix: example interview prompts (copy-paste friendly)
- \"Explain a time you debugged an OOM in an inference pipeline. What were your first three investigations?\"
- \"Given a model with 10ms median latency and 95th percentile spikes to 200ms under load, how would you approach stabilizing tail latency?\"
- \"You have 2 GPUs and a bursty request pattern. How do you design cost-aware routing to balance latency and credits?\"

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep doc short for hiring managers; convert to checklist card in HR board.
- Sync with infra leads (Ravi) and ML Eng (Zoe) to finalize tech prompts.
- If pilot goes well, fold into Offer Flow and Hiring Committee rubric page.

End of working draft.

title: Onboarding Playbook: Day 0–30 Handoff & Early Impact Kit
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook is designed to standardize the first 30 days for new ICs and managers at Redwood Inference. It focuses on fast functional ramp, clear handoffs, and measurable micro-goals so hiring managers and buddies can coordinate without many back-and-forths.

Scope:
Applies to all full-time engineering hires, applied ML hires, and product managers hired into pods. For contractors and interns, use the abbreviated checklist (lower priority for system access and long-term comms).

Quick overview (one-line):
- Day 0: access, welcome, intro to pod and buddy assignment.
- Day 1–7: orientation sprints, 2 small measurable tasks, key readings and infra walkthrough.
- Day 8–30: stakeholder meetings, first PR, first oncall shadow if relevant, first 1:1s established.

Hiring rubric (to attach to offer & interview packet):
We use a weighted rubric to make offers consistent across hiring pairs. Scores 1–5 for each dimension, multiply by weight, total out of 50.
Weighted competencies:
- Core technical ability (code/architecture) — weight 3
- Problem decomposition & communicate tradeoffs — weight 2
- Product sense / user impact awareness — weight 1.5
- Ownership & reliability (evidence of delivery) — weight 2
- Collaboration & cross-team communication — weight 1.5
- Role-specific depth (ML, infra, SRE) — weight 2
Calibration guidance for interviewers:
- 4+: strong evidence in examples, concrete impact numbers.
- 3: competent, some evidence, small gaps.
- 2 or less: concerns, needs growth plan.
Minimum hire threshold: combined weighted score >= 33, with no dimension below 2 (unless hiring manager documents a targeted growth plan).

Interview flow (recommended for IC mid-senior):
1) Recruiter screen (30m): role fit, comp expectations, relocation/remote.
2) Technical take-home (optional) or paired live problem (60m).
3) System-design / architecture (60m) — include ops/scale questions for infra-facing roles.
4) Leadership / collaboration sync (45m) — cross-functional scenarios.
5) Hiring manager final (30m).

Scorecard template (copyable):
- Candidate name: 
- Role applied: 
- Interview date: 
- Interviewer: 
- Scores: Core tech [1-5], Decomp [1-5], Ownership [1-5], Collaboration [1-5], Role depth [1-5]
- Notes: examples, missing signals, recommended level and adjustments
- Decision: yes/maybe/no

Offer checklist (for people ops + hiring manager):
- Compensation band confirmation (HR + hiring manager sign-off)
- Manager acceptance of relocation/time-zone implications
- Draft onboarding plan (first 30 days milestones) attached to offer letter
- Slack + email invite sequence pre-start (welcome note + first-week schedule)

Day 0: immediate access and welcome (owner: People Ops + IT)
- Accounts created: Google, Slack, Redwood Console access, HR portal, code host (GitHub/GitLab).
- Laptop shipped or confirmed pickup with imaging notes (if remote, include hardware checklist).
- Add to pod mailing lists, calendar groups, and oncall schedule (if role requires).
- Welcome message template: manager introduces new hire + buddy in #<team>-welcome.

Day 1–7: orientation sprint (owner: manager + buddy)
- Day 1 morning: 30m manager welcome, 30m buddy tour, 60m required security & compliance readout (recorded).
- Access verification checklist: can clone the repo, run dev server, access CI dashboards, read monitoring runbooks.
- Micro-goal A: Complete and merge a one-hour labeled doc/README improvement PR.
- Micro-goal B: Ship a small infra-safe code change (test-only or docs) with CI passing.
- Required readings: product one-pager, infra runbook, service-level targets for pod models.
- Admin tasks: benefits enrollment, tax forms, calendar invites for recurring 1:1s.

Day 8–14: integration sprint
- Stakeholder 30m intro calls: product PM, SRE lead, analytics contact.
- Shadow rotation: 2-day rotation across codebases/modules most relevant to role.
- Pair-programing session: 90m with primary buddy on a live ticket.
- First retrospective entry: new hire writes 1-page 'first impressions & 30d goals' and shares with manager.

Day 15–30: first-deliverable sprint
- Deliverable: first substantive PR (feature, infra change, or eval harness) merged behind feature flag if applicable.
- Oncall shadow (if applicable): observe pager runbook and escalation path.
- 30-day review: manager completes a short assessment (ramp, blockers, 30d goals status).
- Roadmap sync: new hire presents a 5–7 min summary of what they will own in the next 90 days.

Buddy rotation matrix (example):
- Buddy week 0–1: Codebase tour + dev setup owner (buddy A)
- Buddy week 1–3: Product & experiment lifecycle orientation (buddy B)
- Buddy ongoing: Engineering culture, pull request norms (buddy C)
Notes: buddies should be different people to broaden social graphs and reduce single-person bottlenecks. Limit number to 3 for clarity.

Manager checklist (first 30 days):
- Prepare technical onboarding tickets that are self-contained, reviewable, and small (1–3 day scope).
- Schedule required stakeholder intros in week 1 and 2.
- Confirm training sessions (security, compliance, infra) and ensure recordings are available.
- Set explicit 30-day success criteria with measurable signals (first PR merged, test coverage baseline, monitoring alerts understood).

Remote-specific considerations:
- Ship a physical welcome pack where possible (company swag, quick reference printed).
- Schedule overlapping time blocks for core collaboration hours.
- Encourage async updates: daily 15–30m standup note in thread for first two weeks.

Reboarding / backfill quick-start (when hiring into a staffed pod):
- Check for legacy config that the outgoing IC owned (credentials, infra shortcuts) and add as onboarding tickets.
- Include a brief 'technical debt map' doc to orient the new hire to near-term priorities.

Calibration & mini-audit (recommended at hire + 30-day):
- People Ops runs a sample audit of 10% of scorecards to measure hiring consistency quarterly.
- Managers provide closed-loop feedback to interviewers on candidate ramp signals at 30 days.

Open questions and decisions still needed:
- Standardization of take-home test length across all engineering levels (proposed 2–4 hours max).
- Formalizing compensation approval SLAs for international hires.
- A small template for manager 30-day assessment (to be added as a linked Confluence page).

Appendix: quick templates (copy + paste)
- Welcome Slack message: "Please welcome <Name>, our new <Role> joining <pod>. They'll be pairing with <Buddy> this week — please say hi and share any urgent tips!"
- 30-day goals template: "Goal 1: Merge X; Goal 2: Shadow oncall; Goal 3: Present 90-day plan"

Notes to reviewer:
This doc intentionally mixes crisp checklists with suggested micro-goals rather than long checklists that managers ignore. Feedback requested on: minimum hire threshold score, whether to require take-homes for senior roles, and an approval SLA for laptop provisioning for remote hires.

Revision log:
- 2025-11-02: initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2025-11-06: added hiring rubric weights (Maya Patel)
- 2025-11-10: clarified buddy rotation and remote guidance (Liam O'Connor)

title: Q3 focus threads and delegate roster — all-hands prep v0.1
preview:
Purpose:

Quick working doc for the exec + all-hands narrative and the delegate roster for Q3 planning. We need a compact slate for the town, a clear set of draft OKRs that map to product and infra asks, and a lightweight owner matrix so follow-ups happen after the meeting. This version is intentionally rough — use it to iterate before the exec sync (Weds) and the all-hands (Fri).

Context / constraints:
- Company steering: keep two high-impact cross-company initiatives (Performance Economics and Private Deployments ramp) and 2 enabling bets (Console polish for rollouts, Optimize insights).
- Budget posture: heads and infra spend mostly flat; aggressive efficiency asks needed to avoid mid-quarter rebaseline.
- Timing: engineering release windows in late July — any OKR that requires model infra changes must be scoped to a single-sprint uplift or flagged as multi-quarter.

Outcome we want from event:
- Exec alignment on top-3 tactical priorities for Q3 (commit and signal to team).
- Public OKR draft for the company to react to within 48h after all-hands (comment period).
- A delegate roster with named owners and 2-week check-ins for early risk discovery.

Draft top-level OKRs (candidate list — short, measurable, owner):
1) OKR: Reduce hosted API 95th percentile latency by 20% on common tails
   - Why: customer SLAs and conversion on trial workloads
   - Success metric: 95p latency improves from 820ms -> <=660ms on p95 for gen endpoints (tracked per-model)
   - Owner: Marcus Rivera
   - Key results (suggested):
     - KR1: Kernel tuning + new batching policy deployed to 50% of traffic without SLO regressions
     - KR2: Prefix/KV cache hit rate +15% on inference corpus set
     - KR3: Completed observability hook for per-route token-level trace (console)

2) OKR: Lower per-token inference cost for mid-sized workloads by 15%
   - Why: unit economics for scale customers and Dedicated renewals
   - Owner: Priya Kapoor
   - KRs:
     - KR1: Shipping Optimize recommendations for batching/quantization for top-10 customers
     - KR2: Launch the 'burst pricing' experiment to validate 2% uplift in reserved utilization
     - KR3: Reduce model spin-up time median from 12s -> 7s for cold starts

3) OKR: Private deployments — baseline security and onboarding for 3 pilots
   - Why: Enterprise pipeline and revenue motion
   - Owner: Diego Morales
   - KRs:
     - KR1: 3 pilot customers onboarded with private control plane + audit logs
     - KR2: KMS/HSM integration validated against compliance checklist
     - KR3: Private health-check automation and rollback policy documented in Console

Enablers / company-level guardrails (non-OKR but must be tracked):
- Console rollout flow: reduce time-to-pin model <-> route by 40% (Product/Console team lead Lina Park).
- Reliability SLO cost tradeoff playbook: ops + SRE to publish a clear escalation and fallback policy for model fallbacks.
- Hiring/time: freeze discretionary hiring for cloud infra until Q3 midpoint unless critical (exec decision flag).

All-hands 40-minute micro-slate (Fri):
- 0:00–0:03 Opening (CEO quick frame on why these lanes) — Ava Chen
- 0:03–0:10 Exec snapshot: three priorities + one-liner impact statements — Marcus, Priya, Diego (1–2 mins each)
- 0:10–0:18 Concrete examples (2 short customer vignettes: Hosted perf improvement -> conversion; Private pilot -> compliance win) — Solutions Eng
- 0:18–0:28 Roadmap pointers and demo of Console quickflow (Lina Park) — 6–8 minute demo, 2 mins Q
- 0:28–0:34 Risks & tradeoffs: cost posture and contingency playbook — SRE lead
- 0:34–0:37 Ask to org: 48-hour comment window + how to propose changes to OKRs (PR/issue template) — Company Ops
- 0:37–0:40 Rapid close + call for volunteers for pilot squads

Communication and follow-up cadence:
- Day 0 (all-hands): Publish slides + 1-page with the 3 OKR drafts and delegate roster.
- Day 2: Comment period closes — compile feedback and push updated doc.
- Week 1: Exec sign-off and publish final company OKRs.
- Weeks 1–6: Biweekly delegate check-ins; 2-page status note captured each check-in in the shared roadmap page.

Delegate roster (working):
- Priority 1 (Latency): Marcus Rivera — engineering lead, + SRE liaison (Samir Patel), data lead (Ana Gomez)
- Priority 2 (Cost): Priya Kapoor — product + finance observer (Tao Lin), solutions-eng (Diego Morales)
- Priority 3 (Private): Diego Morales — security lead (Kavita Shah), customer-success owner (Maya Singh)
- Console polish: Lina Park — UX + docs (Riley Chen)
- Communication owner (post-hall): Ava Chen + Growth (Noah Feldman)

Open questions / TODOs before exec sync:
- Exec ask: confirm hiring freeze exception process (who signs?) — action: Legal/Finance to propose wording (due Tue)
- Dataset for the p95 latency claim: need canonical synthetic + production slices. Action: Marcus to drop dataset link and baseline numbers by Tue EOD.
- Customer vignettes: Solutions Eng to confirm permission on names/metrics — action: Maya (CS)
- Demo readiness: Lina to give a dry-run Wednesday (schedule invite)

Risks & mitigations (brief):
- Risk: Kernel tuning introduces a subtle regression on long-context flows. Mitigation: canary + automated regressions on eval set and auto-fallback to previous kernel.
- Risk: Private pilot timeline slips because of network provisioning. Mitigation: pre-approved checklist and a small 'blueprint' turnkey script for pilot infra.
- Risk: Team confusion if OKRs change post-all-hands. Mitigation: strict 48-hour comment window and single exec owner to approve amendments.

Decisions needed at exec sync (Weds):
- Approve top-3 priority slate as company signal (Y/N)
- Approve delegate roster and 2-week check-in cadence (Y/N)
- Approve hiring freeze exceptions process (Y/N)

Notes to self / rough bits: 
- Keep the all-hands short and ceremonial; actual operational work happens in follow-up squads.
- Consider small symbolic recognition for squads that hit early wins in Q3 (PR-friendly).

Attachments / links to draft materials (working):
- Slides: /drive/company/presentations/q3-micro-slate-v0.1 (placeholder)
- Baseline telemetry: /drive/eng/telemetry/q2-latency-baseline.csv (placeholder)
- Draft OKR doc (this file will be imported to confluence after sign-off)

If you are an owner listed above: add your 3-line plan in comments below and availability for a 30-min sync before Thurs EOD.

-- Ava

(edited: added private pilot owner + tightened dates 2025-11-07)

title: Compact signal prioritization and trace cost playground
preview:
Purpose and scope:
Short experimental playbook for prioritizing telemetry signals (metrics + traces) for low-cost dashboards and higher-value trace capture. Goal is to define a lightweight, defensible approach teams can copy for resource-constrained models/tenants and for early-stage feature rollouts.

Background / problem statement:
- Query budget keeps getting eaten by open-ended trace capture and high-cardinality widgets.
- Operators need fast root-cause without blowing storage or query budgets.
- Current sampling is either too coarse (random 1%) or too greedy (capture-all on errors). We need an importance-driven sampling signal and tighter dashboard surface area.

High-level ideas (experiments to run):
1) \"Importance scoring\" per request: combine error-severity, tail-latency-percentile, model-variant-fanout, and token-economics -> produce 0..1 importance score. Use to drive trace sampling and retention.
2) Cost-aware adaptive sampling: wall-clock trace-budget per hour per org; when burn rate > threshold, increase sampling aggressiveness for low-importance flows.
3) Compact SLI widgets: replace heavy per-tenant breakdowns with top-K tenant drilldown (default aggregated).
4) Microtrace capture: sample only span headers and metadata for low-importance flows, full span capture for high-importance ones.

Proposed metrics (definitions + rationale) - experimental set (unique names):
- token_serving_efficiency: \"tokens_served / GPU_seconds\" measured per model and per route. Rationale: captures token throughput efficiency and helps compare quantized vs FP models.
- kv_cache_warmth_score: \"weighted fraction of requests hitting prefix kv cache within 100ms\". Rationale: early indicator for cold-cache regressions.
- batch_utilization_p50_p95: \"percentage of batch slots used (p50/p95).\" Rationale: shows batching goodness and tail inefficiency.
- tail_fanout_index: \"sum of (downstream_model_calls * latency_ms) for requests in p99\". Rationale: identify expensive fanouts that cause tail latency.
- prefetch_hit_rate_kb: \"KB prefetched from prefix cache hits / KB requested\". Rationale: content size matters for inference cost.
- ops_importance_score: \"derived importance signal used for sampling (see formula below)\". Rationale: driving sampling and retention.

ops_importance_score (sketch):
- normalize components to 0..1: err = min(1, error_severity / 5), tail = min(1, latency_ms / 2000), fanout = min(1, downstream_calls / 10), cost = min(1, token_cost_usd / 0.1)
- ops_importance_score = max( err * 0.6 + tail * 0.2 + fanout * 0.1 + cost * 0.1, 0.05 )
Notes: floor at 0.05 so everything has glimmer of trace meta stored for retrospective sampling.

Tracing guidelines (span design + tags):
- Span naming: \"svc:<service>-op:<op-name>-phase:<phase>\" -> e.g., svc:runtime-op:serve-phase:kv_lookup
- Minimal required tags on root span: request_id, route, model_name, model_version, tenant_id (if available, else tenant_bucket), ops_importance_score, tokens_in, tokens_out, approximate_cost_usd
- Fanout spans: include tag \"fanout_target\" and numeric tag \"fanout_seq\" to preserve call order; annotate whether call used cached KV.
- Microtrace vs full-trace: microtrace = only root span + span_ids for downstream calls + basic tags (no payloads). Full-trace = include span timings and error stacks.

Sampling strategy (practical rules):
- default sample rate = 0.02 (2%) global
- sampled if ops_importance_score >= 0.25 -> sample rate 100% (full trace)
- if score in [0.1,0.25) -> microtrace capture with 10% probability to store full spans for debugging drift (heuristic: store 1 full trace per N microtraces)
- if score < 0.1 -> microtrace only with sampling floor 0.5%
- emergency override: any request that triggers a customer-visible error or a canary failure flag -> immediate full-trace capture and pinned retention.

Dashboard design notes (compactizing heavy dashboards):
- Replace 20 per-tenant panels with: tenant-ranked heatmap (top 10 by impact), selectable tenant detail view that loads expanded queries on click (reduces default query load).
- Provide micropanels that show: aggregated token_serving_efficiency, kv_cache_warmth_score, batch_utilization_p95, tail_fanout_index. Each micropanel has a minimize/expand switch that defers heavy queries until expanded.
- Query budget badges on dashboard header: show current hourly cost estimate and query count; expose a single button \"throttle-expensive-widgets\" for on-call.

Operational runbook snippets (quick actions):
- Investigation-first flow for tail latency spike:
  1) Open heatmap -> identify tenant or route -> check tail_fanout_index -> if > threshold, check fanout_target spans for large downstream calls.
  2) If kv_cache_warmth_score < 0.7, consider prefix-cache-warmup script or force prefetch for sample traffic.
  3) If batch_utilization_p95 < 50% -> look for skewed request sizes or misconfigured batching window.

Retention and cost controls:
- Store full traces for 14 days for high-importance flows, microtrace meta for 60 days, and aggregated metrics forever (subject to metric downsampling).
- Nightly job that down-samples microtraces to 1/10th for older-than-30d if total storage > budget.

Open questions / TODOs (action items):
- [ ] Validate ops_importance_score formula on 2 weeks of production traffic. Owner: Liam. ETA: 2025-11-10.
- [ ] Build microtrace schema and add to trace ingest path. Owner: Noah. ETA: 2025-11-17.
- [ ] Dashboard POC with minimize/expand widgets to test query deferral UX. Owner: Sofia. ETA: 2025-12-01.
- [ ] Run cost-sim to estimate savings from microtrace-first approach vs current capture profile. Owner: Ava. ETA: 2025-11-20.

Notes to self / workshop prompts (for next sync):
- Can we leverage existing model profiling pipeline to estimate token_cost_usd cheaply?
- How do we surface \"importance\" to product teams so they understand why some traces are micro-only?
- Consider a CI check that prevents dashboards from adding more than N heavyweight panels per route.

Appendix: quick SQL-ish snippets (pseudo) for metric prototypes:
- token_serving_efficiency = sum(tokens) / sum(gpu_seconds) by model_name, date
- kv_cache_warmth_score = sum(case when cache_hit and timestamp - cache_pop_time < 100 then 1 else 0 end) / count(*) by route
- batch_utilization_p95 = percentile(batch_slots_used / batch_size, 95)

End of doc (working draft, experimental).
text:
Purpose and scope:
Short experimental playbook for prioritizing telemetry signals (metrics + traces) for low-cost dashboards and higher-value trace capture. Goal is to define a lightweight, defensible approach teams can copy for resource-constrained models/tenants and for early-stage feature rollouts.

Background / problem statement:
- Query budget keeps getting eaten by open-ended trace capture and high-cardinality widgets.
- Operators need fast root-cause without blowing storage or query budgets.
- Current sampling is either too coarse (random 1%) or too greedy (capture-all on errors). We need an importance-driven sampling signal and tighter dashboard surface area.

High-level ideas (experiments to run):
1) \"Importance scoring\" per request: combine error-severity, tail-latency-percentile, model-variant-fanout, and token-economics -> produce 0..1 importance score. Use to drive trace sampling and retention.
2) Cost-aware adaptive sampling: wall-clock trace-budget per hour per org; when burn rate > threshold, increase sampling aggressiveness for low-importance flows.
3) Compact SLI widgets: replace heavy per-tenant breakdowns with top-K tenant drilldown (default aggregated).
4) Microtrace capture: sample only span headers and metadata for low-importance flows, full span capture for high-importance ones.

Proposed metrics (definitions + rationale) - experimental set (unique names):
- token_serving_efficiency: \"tokens_served / GPU_seconds\" measured per model and per route. Rationale: captures token throughput efficiency and helps compare quantized vs FP models.
- kv_cache_warmth_score: \"weighted fraction of requests hitting prefix kv cache within 100ms\". Rationale: early indicator for cold-cache regressions.
- batch_utilization_p50_p95: \"percentage of batch slots used (p50/p95).\" Rationale: shows batching goodness and tail inefficiency.
- tail_fanout_index: \"sum of (downstream_model_calls * latency_ms) for requests in p99\". Rationale: identify expensive fanouts that cause tail latency.
- prefetch_hit_rate_kb: \"KB prefetched from prefix cache hits / KB requested\". Rationale: content size matters for inference cost.
- ops_importance_score: \"derived importance signal used for sampling (see formula below)\". Rationale: driving sampling and retention.

ops_importance_score (sketch):
- normalize components to 0..1: err = min(1, error_severity / 5), tail = min(1, latency_ms / 2000), fanout = min(1, downstream_calls / 10), cost = min(1, token_cost_usd / 0.1)
- ops_importance_score = max( err * 0.6 + tail * 0.2 + fanout * 0.1 + cost * 0.1, 0.05 )
Notes: floor at 0.05 so everything has glimmer of trace meta stored for retrospective sampling.

Tracing guidelines (span design + tags):
- Span naming: \"svc:<service>-op:<op-name>-phase:<phase>\" -> e.g., svc:runtime-op:serve-phase:kv_lookup
- Minimal required tags on root span: request_id, route, model_name, model_version, tenant_id (if available, else tenant_bucket), ops_importance_score, tokens_in, tokens_out, approximate_cost_usd
- Fanout spans: include tag \"fanout_target\" and numeric tag \"fanout_seq\" to preserve call order; annotate whether call used cached KV.
- Microtrace vs full-trace: microtrace = only root span + span_ids for downstream calls + basic tags (no payloads). Full-trace = include span timings and error stacks.

Sampling strategy (practical rules):
- default sample rate = 0.02 (2%) global
- sampled if ops_importance_score >= 0.25 -> sample rate 100% (full trace)
- if score in [0.1,0.25) -> microtrace capture with 10% probability to store full spans for debugging drift (heuristic: store 1 full trace per N microtraces)
- if score < 0.1 -> microtrace only with sampling floor 0.5%
- emergency override: any request that triggers a customer-visible error or a canary failure flag -> immediate full-trace capture and pinned retention.

Dashboard design notes (compactizing heavy dashboards):
- Replace 20 per-tenant panels with: tenant-ranked heatmap (top 10 by impact), selectable tenant detail view that loads expanded queries on click (reduces default query load).
- Provide micropanels that show: aggregated token_serving_efficiency, kv_cache_warmth_score, batch_utilization_p95, tail_fanout_index. Each micropanel has a minimize/expand switch that defers heavy queries until expanded.
- Query budget badges on dashboard header: show current hourly cost estimate and query count; expose a single button \"throttle-expensive-widgets\" for on-call.

Operational runbook snippets (quick actions):
- Investigation-first flow for tail latency spike:
  1) Open heatmap -> identify tenant or route -> check tail_fanout_index -> if > threshold, check fanout_target spans for large downstream calls.
  2) If kv_cache_warmth_score < 0.7, consider prefix-cache-warmup script or force prefetch for sample traffic.
  3) If batch_utilization_p95 < 50% -> look for skewed request sizes or misconfigured batching window.

Retention and cost controls:
- Store full traces for 14 days for high-importance flows, microtrace meta for 60 days, and aggregated metrics forever (subject to metric downsampling).
- Nightly job that down-samples microtraces to 1/10th for older-than-30d if total storage > budget.

Open questions / TODOs (action items):
- [ ] Validate ops_importance_score formula on 2 weeks of production traffic. Owner: Liam. ETA: 2025-11-10.
- [ ] Build microtrace schema and add to trace ingest path. Owner: Noah. ETA: 2025-11-17.
- [ ] Dashboard POC with minimize/expand widgets to test query deferral UX. Owner: Sofia. ETA: 2025-12-01.
- [ ] Run cost-sim to estimate savings from microtrace-first approach vs current capture profile. Owner: Ava. ETA: 2025-11-20.

Notes to self / workshop prompts (for next sync):
- Can we leverage existing model profiling pipeline to estimate token_cost_usd cheaply?
- How do we surface \"importance\" to product teams so they understand why some traces are micro-only?
- Consider a CI check that prevents dashboards from adding more than N heavyweight panels per route.

Appendix: quick SQL-ish snippets (pseudo) for metric prototypes:
- token_serving_efficiency = sum(tokens) / sum(gpu_seconds) by model_name, date
- kv_cache_warmth_score = sum(case when cache_hit and timestamp - cache_pop_time < 100 then 1 else 0 end) / count(*) by route
- batch_utilization_p95 = percentile(batch_slots_used / batch_size, 95)

End of doc (working draft, experimental).

title: Onboarding smoke plan: multilingual translation + locale formatting (CJK, RTL)
preview:
Purpose:
This is a lightweight, reproducible smoke plan to run during model onboarding for multilingual text generation and translation scenarios with a focus on:
- CJK (zh, ja, ko) and RTL (ar, he) language families
- Translation adequacy and fluency checks
- Locale-sensitive formatting (dates, numbers, currency, phone numbers, percent signs, punctuation)

Scope:
- Intended as the first gate after unit tests and before full-scale eval suites.
- Runs in ~1–2 minutes on a single eval worker per model variant.
- Not exhaustive — surfaced failures should trigger deeper evals (full translation suites, char-level tests, synthetic locale edgecases).

Quick summary of goals:
- Detect gross mistranslations and hallucinations for short inputs (1–3 sentence prompts).
- Confirm locale formatting behavior for common tokens (YYYY/MM/DD vs localized, thousands separators, full-width vs half-width digits).
- Catch major directionality / punctuation regressions for RTL outputs (mirrored punctuation, wrong bidi ordering).
- Provide deterministic pass/fail signals and a short triage rubric.

Test matrix (minimal):
- 30 translation testcases across languages: 6 zh-CN, 6 ja-JP, 6 ko-KR, 6 ar, 6 he (mix of formal/informal style).
- 20 locale-formatting checks: dates (ISO, localized), numbers (int, float, thousands), currency examples (USD, CNY, JPY), phone formats.
- 10 mixed prompts that combine translation + formatting (e.g., translate sentence containing a date and a phone number).

Sample prompts + expected behaviours (short examples):
- Chinese (zh-CN):
  Prompt: "Translate to English: 会议定在2025年12月1日下午2:30举行。联系：张伟，+86 138 0013 8000。"
  Expected: English translation preserving numeric values and clarifying timezone if asked. Numeric substrings should remain numbers (2025-12-01 14:30) or natural english phrasing "Dec 1, 2025 at 2:30 PM". Do not expand phone digits into words.

- Japanese (ja-JP):
  Prompt: "英語に翻訳してください：価格は￥1,234,000（税込）です。納期は2026/01/05。"
  Expected: "The price is ¥1,234,000 (including tax). Delivery date is 2026-01-05." Maintain currency symbol and group separators; avoid converting to words.

- Korean (ko-KR):
  Prompt: "Translate to English: 총액은 1,200,000원입니다. 결제 마감일: 2026년 3월 15일."
  Expected: Numeric digits preserved, date normalized to ISO or natural English date.

- Arabic (ar):
  Prompt: "ترجم إلى الإنجليزية: الموعد ١٤٠١/٠٨/٢٥، الرجاء الاتصال على 050-123-4567."
  Expected: Latin digits in phone preserved/normalized (050-123-4567), date converted to Gregorian if asked or left as-is but digits normalized to Western numerals in English translation. Check for correct RTL handling and punctuation direction.

- Hebrew (he):
  Prompt: "תרגם לאנגלית: מחיר המוצר ₪2,345. תתקשר אל: 03-555-1234."
  Expected: Currency and phone numbers preserved, punctuation in correct order.

Formatting tests (explicit):
- Dates: input variants: "2025/12/01", "01.12.2025", "2025年12月1日", "٢٠٢٥-١٢-٠١". Expect consistent normalization policy per product config (prefer ISO for internal logs; natural locale format for user-facing translations).
- Numbers: inputs with full-width digits (e.g., "１２３４"), half-width, and comma separators. Expect correct preservation/normalization and no accidental digit scrambling.
- Currency: keep currency symbol and numeric value; do not invent conversion unless requested. JPY should not show decimals unless decimals present.
- Percent: "12,5%" (comma decimal in some locales) -> normalized to "12.5%" in English translations.

Scoring rules (fast heuristics suitable for smoke):
- Translation adequacy: use a short bilingual reference set, compute chrF and a fuzzy token overlap metric.
  - Pass if chrF >= 0.45 for short sentences OR fuzzy token overlap >= 70% for non-creative prompts.
- Formatting correctness: rule-based checks (regex) for expected tokens: dates normalized, currency retained, phone number pattern preserved.
  - Pass if >= 90% of formatting checks match expected patterns.
- RTL sanity: check for presence of correct bidi markers and punctuation placement.
  - Pass if no mirrored punctuation errors and overall directionality matches target language in >95% of cases.
- Overall gate: model passes smoke if >= 80% of translation testcases pass AND formatting checks pass.

Automation notes / eval harness integration:
- This smoke plan will be run by the onboarding pipeline script smoke_onboard.sh which calls the evals runner with --lite-check flag.
- Reference set stored in /eval_corpora/multilang/smoke/onboard_v1.tsv (short pairs + formatting labels).
- Quick metrics output JSON keys: chrF, fuzzy_overlap, formatting_pass_pct, rtl_sanity_pct, overall_pass (boolean).

Failure modes & triage steps (fast):
- Low chrF but high formatting_pass: translation quality regression. Tag as "linguistic-regression" and escalate to applied-ml for analysis. Attach sample examples.
- Formatting failures concentrated on numbers/dates: check tokenizer/normalizer pipeline. Run tokenization debug on failing examples.
- RTL punctuation inversion: reproduce with minimal example and check bidi handling code path; add failing prompt to bug ticket.
- Hallucinated dates/currencies: immediate fail for smoke gate; require human review and test augmentation.

Rollout gating & actions:
- If model fails smoke gate: block automatic rollout and create onboarding ticket with 'smoke-fail' label and top 5 failure examples.
- If model passes smoke gate but has medium-risk warnings (65–80% translation pass): allow canary with 10% traffic and enable monitoring with auto-alerts for token-level drop.

Quick runbook (for running locally):
1) git checkout evals && pip install -r requirements.txt
2) python evals/run_smoke.py --model-id <candidate> --ref /eval_corpora/multilang/smoke/onboard_v1.tsv --out ./smoke_results.json
3) Inspect smoke_results.json, run ./scripts/format_failures_for_ticket.sh ./smoke_results.json if overall_pass=false

Notes & open questions:
- Decide on canonical normalization policy for numbers in cross-locale translations (prefer preserving original digits vs normalizing to target script). Action: product-hosted-api to confirm by 2025-11-10.
- Need to add CJK full-width punctuation checks for a separate extreme edgecases suite.
- Consider adding automatic pronunciation/romanization check for names (low priority).

References and immediate next steps:
- Link to full multilingual onboarding playbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/AM/Multilingual+Onboarding
- Create JIRA subticket for failing patterns: JIRA-ML-5182 (placeholder)
- Owner: Ava Chen. Next checkpoint: run smoke plan on candidate X (v2.1-quant) and report by 2025-11-05.

Appendix: quick example table (input -> expected snippet):
- "发票日期：2025年4月9日" -> "Invoice date: April 9, 2025"
- "הזמנה נשלחה ב-01/02/2026" -> "Order shipped on 2026-02-01"
- "금액: 1,000,000원" -> "Amount: ₩1,000,000"
- "اتصل على ٠٥٠-١٢٣-٤٥٦٧" -> "Contact: 050-123-4567"

End of smoke plan.

title: Capacity rollup + cluster/pool drift analysis (weekly)
preview:
Summary:
This sheet is an export of the weekly cluster/pool utilization rollup with a short drift analysis and recommended capacity moves for the next 4 weeks. Focus is on NA/EU regional pools that show steady growth in steady-state production traffic and a new recurring spike in experimental batch jobs (tracked INF-748).

Key takeaways (TL;DR):
- Fleet-wide average GPU util (7d): 64% avg, 92% 95th percentile across peak windows.
- NA-west/generic-pool shows upward drift (+8 pp week-over-week) driven by two long-running batched eval jobs.
- EU-central/dedicated-low-latency has headroom but increasing tail latency for seq_len > 1024.
- Recommend short-term reservation: +12x A100-equivalent in NA-west for next 14 days; longer term consider autoscale policy adjustment for experimental batch label.

Detailed per-cluster rollup (most recent 7d window):
Cluster,Region,Pool,GPUs,total_hours_reported,avg_util_pct,95p_util_pct,peak_tokens_s,notes
cluster-na-3,NA-west,generic-pool,128,168,72,94,182k,\"Upward drift; 2 long eval jobs (INF-748); recommend investigation + preemption policy\"
cluster-na-1,NA-east,dedicated-ml,96,168,61,85,140k,\"Stable; steady production queries; 1 node under maintenance last Tue\"
cluster-eu-2,EU-central,dedicated-low-latency,64,168,49,78,118k,\"Tail latency increases on long context; consider kernel tune/quant profile\"
cluster-eu-1,EU-west,generic-pool,48,168,58,81,95k,\"New customer test burst on Thu night; isolate to testing tag\"
cluster-ap-1,AP-sg,generic-pool,32,168,37,62,42k,\"Low utilization; experimental models staged here\"
cluster-na-4,NA-west,span-pool,40,168,83,97,200k,\"Hotspot: 95th util >95% during peak; some requests experienced throttling\"
cluster-na-2,NA-central,shared-dedicated,56,168,66,89,155k,\"Normal\"
cluster-eu-3,EU-central,shadow-pool,16,168,22,36,12k,\"Shadow traffic only\"
cluster-na-gpu-test,NA-west,batch-evals,24,168,94,99,250k,\"Batch evals causing sustained high util; not preemptible currently\"
cluster-park,NA-east,spare-reserve,16,168,5,12,3k,\"Standby reserve\"

Weekly regional sparklines (avg util over past 7 days, 1 point per day):
NA: [58,61,62,64,68,70,72]  -> rising trend, daily growth +2.4%
EU: [46,47,49,50,51,49,48]   -> flat/soft increase around midweek
AP: [32,34,36,37,38,36,35]   -> small uptick driven by scheduled experiments

Anomalies and incidents (this window):
- 2025-10-15 03:00 UTC: Unexpected 6-hour spike in NA-west generic pools. Root cause: two large non-preemptible long-running evals launched by perf-team job run (INF-748). Impact: 12% increase in 95p token latency for prod routes routed to NA-west. Remediation: killed offending jobs, applied tag-based rate limit.
- 2025-10-28 18:00 UTC: EU-central latency tail increase for seq_len>1024. Mitigation: temporarily route long-context requests to EU-west; SRE to schedule kernel patch.

Forecast (4-week) and recommended moves:
Assumptions: continued 5–8% weekly organic growth in NA production traffic; two recurring weekly batch windows on Tue/Thu night; blackbox client tests on EU-west once per week.
Week 1 (2025-11-02 to 2025-11-08): predicted fleet avg util 68%. Recommend: reserve +12 A100-eq in NA-west (activate spare-reserve->generic-pool), change batch-evals pool to preemptible nodes, set soft cap on batch jobs to 75% util.
Week 2 (2025-11-09 to 2025-11-15): predicted fleet avg util 70%. Recommend: keep reservation, enable autoscale up threshold from 75%->70% for generic-pool to bring more nodes online earlier.
Week 3: predicted 71–73%, run capacity dry-run for holiday traffic.
Week 4: predicted 73–77% (uncertain). Consider negotiated reserved capacity contract if trend persists.

Risk matrix (high level):
- Continued non-preemptible batch jobs: HIGH -> reduces headroom, increases throttling risk.
- Kernel regressions for long context: MEDIUM -> increases tail latency, mitigated by routing.
- Regional failure (AZ) during peak: LOW-MED -> multi-region routing in place.

Open action items (owner, due):
- Marcus Patel: implement tag-based preemption policy for batch-evals and update job templates (due 2025-11-04).
- Eleanor Ruiz: add a metric in the console for per-job GPU-hours breakout (due 2025-11-07).
- Diego Alvarez: run placement dry-run to verify +12 A100-eq reservation activation on NA-west (due 2025-11-03).
- Ava Chen: follow up with perf-team on INF-748 recurrence and enforce quota (due 2025-11-05).

Notes for product/ops:
- If reservation is approved, update Dedicated reservation pricing sheet and alert billing OPS to standby for a potential new commit.
- Consider a routing rule: prefer dedicated-low-latency for seq_len>1024 where available to avoid long-kernel tail on generic pools.

Appendix: data sources and methodology:
- Source: per-cluster exporter + daily aggregation job (cap-rollup-v2) feeding the weekly dashboard export.
- Metrics used: gpu_util_avg, gpu_util_95p, tokens_per_second, request_tail_latency_p95 (seq_len buckets).
- Window: 7d rolling; spike detection uses 3-sigma on 24h sliding windows.

If anything looks off or you want CSV table adjusted for ingestion, ping me in #infra-capacity or comment below.

title: Welcome pathways + interview panel notes (sep29 2025)
preview:
Context:
Quick write-up from today’s cross-team sync (hiring + onboarding + health check) — pulled notes from the interview panel debrief (9/29) and the buddy rotation pilot check-in. Aim: capture friction points in first 6 weeks and a handful of concrete actions we can move on this quarter.

Attendees:
- Ava Chen (owner) — hiring/onboarding lead
- Miguel Torres — hiring panel chair, infra
- Priya Nair — buddy program lead, SRE
- Sam Patel — eng manager, serving-runtime
- Elena Rossi — people ops (observations / compliance)
- observers: two IC panelists (joined for specific candidate discussion)

High-level takeaways:
- Interview panel consistency still variable: some interviewers are not calibrated on rubric for system-design vs runtime-performance interviews. Several panels had different expectations for candidate depth.
- First-day checklist gaps: new hires without mac images delivered on day 1; network/VPN access delayed for private deployments folks. Impacts productivity and morale.
- Buddy program working but uneven handoffs: buddies are overloaded, unclear expectations for week-by-week touchpoints.
- Team health signals: small uptick in async burnout language on retros and Slack (\"heads-down\", \"crunching\"), most pronounced on teams shipping infra upgrades.

Interview panel notes (specific candidates removed):
- Panel calibration: re-run the brief 20-min calibration session (rubric walkthrough) every 6 weeks for active interviewers. Miguel volunteered to own the next session (target: Oct 10).
- Standardize scoring sheet: add explicit \"rampability\" and \"communication under ambiguity\" checkboxes. Priya will draft a one-pager and push to ops-playbooks repo (PR tracked: ops-playbooks PR #78).
- Candidate feedback turnaround: reduce to 48 hours. Action: hiring panel chairs to set block calendars for immediate debriefs.

Onboarding / first 6 weeks friction points:
- Machines & tooling:
  - Problem: provisioning lag for mac images and private-deploy infra keys.
  - Short-term fix: maintain a 2-machine warm pool for Mac M1/Pro images (Sam to scope).
  - Longer-term: infra ticket JIRA-RDW-4321 — estimate queued (Priya + infra).
- Security & KMS access:
  - Problem: private deployment folks waiting on KMS grant approvals (legal flow is slow).
  - Action: Elena to coordinate with legal for a templated SOW that includes pre-approved KMS access gating for standard hire roles.
- Documentation discoverability:
  - Problem: onboarding docs scattered between Confluence and ops-playbooks. New hires complain they \"don’t know which doc is canonical.\"
  - Action: create a consolidated \"Welcome Pathways\" landing page on Confluence linking canonical playbooks; owner: Ava.
- Buddy expectations:
  - Problem: buddies default to ad-hoc help; no explicit weekly agenda.
  - Action: Priya to propose a 6-week buddy agenda (week-by-week milestones + 30/60/90 checkpoints) and add to playbook.

Team health and signals:
- Observations from retros and eng-standups:
  - Threads referencing long weekends and missed PTO (signal: people deferring breaks).
  - A few junior ICs asking for more pairing time and clearer mentorship slots.
- Immediate actions:
  - Manager prompt: ask all managers to schedule one 1:1 focused on workload + time-off in the next two weeks (Sam to send reminder template).
  - Pairing hours: run a 2-week \"open pairing\" volunteer calendar where senior ICs sign up 2x slots for new hires. Priya to pilot with SRE team Oct 6–17.

Open questions / unresolved:
- Should we require a single \"welcome day\" repo that includes all admin links? Pros: single source of truth. Cons: maintenance burden.
- What’s the SLA for infra provisioning for remote hires across regions (EU/APAC)? Need input from infra ops on regional images and shipping timelines.
- How to measure buddy effectiveness beyond anecdotal feedback? Proposed metric: % of new hires who hit first-PR within 21 days; need historical baseline.

Action items (owners + deadlines):
- Miguel: run interviewer calibration session (target 2025-10-10) — add event to hiring calendar.
- Priya: draft standardized scoring sheet + 6-week buddy agenda; PR to ops-playbooks (due 2025-10-07).
- Sam: scope warm pool for Mac images and short-term provisioning checklist (due 2025-10-14).
- Elena: coordinate KMS SOW template with legal (first draft due 2025-10-20).
- Ava: create Confluence \"Welcome Pathways\" landing and redirect links (publish by 2025-10-09).
- All managers: schedule workload + PTO check 1:1s with direct reports (complete by 2025-10-10).

Notes to self / weird bits we captured:
- Candidate panel feedback occasionally referenced internal project code names — remind panel not to discuss confidential project details in scoring.
- One buddy noted they prefer a 15-min daily sync for week 1 instead of multiple ad-hoc messages — consider adding that as optional schedule.
- A few candidates asked about \"career ladder clarity\" on interview loop; add short slide to interview packet about leveling.

Follow-ups / next meeting:
- Quick check-in in two weeks to inspect progress on action items (tentative 2025-10-13).
- Add measurement backlog item: compute \"first-PR within 21 days\" metric for hires in last 12 months (data request to analytics).

Raw links / references (copied here for posterity):
- Playbook PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/ops-playbooks/pull/78
- Confluence draft: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/Welcome+Pathways+Playbook
- Infra ticket: JIRA-RDW-4321
- Slack thread about buddy pilot: https://slack-redwood.slack.com/archives/C01ABCDEF/thread/FGH123

If anything missing or if you want me to split this into a short PRD + checklist, say so. Felt like it was helpful to get everything in one doc so owners can pick up items.

-- Ava

title: Cross-Role Rapid Assessment Kit — 72hr Practicals, Interviewer Rubrics, Day-Zero Remote Setup
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This is a lightweight, reusable kit for running a 72-hour practical assessment across roles (SWE/backend, ML engineer/infra, product manager, SRE/ops). The goal is to: 1) get a sense for hands-on problem solving and communication, 2) reduce onsite time by pushing practicals earlier, 3) provide consistent scoring and a minimal day-zero checklist so accepted candidates can be productive on day 1.

Intended use and constraints:
- Use for mid-to-senior IC hires (IC3-IC5).
- Volunteer-based practicals: candidate chooses to accept practical phase after initial screen.
- Should take ~6–10 hours total for candidate over 72 hours (split tasks + writeups).
- Not a take-home whiteboard replacement — emphasize realistic artifacts (small PR, infra terraform snippet, decision memo, runbook).
- Timeboxed and scaffolded to respect candidate bandwidth; compensation offered when >6 hours.

How to pick tasks by role (examples):
- SWE (backend): 72-hour practical = small service feature + test + deploy notes
  - Deliverable: git repo with README, single endpoint implemented, 2 unit tests, and a short PR description that notes scaling considerations (max 500 words).
  - Data: starter repo template + sample dataset (100–500 rows).

- ML infra/Applied ML: 72-hour practical = model serving pipeline stub + latency/throughput note
  - Deliverable: Jupyter notebook or small repo that loads a provided model, wraps inference in a server (FastAPI) and includes a one-page infra diagram and perf/quant tradeoffs.

- Product Manager (platform/product): 72-hour practical = 1-page product brief + rollout plan + acceptance criteria
  - Deliverable: 1–2 page doc: problem statement, target personas, success metrics (2–3), rollout phases, launch risks and mitigation. Include 3 interview-style follow-up questions for cross-functional partners.

- SRE/Infra: 72-hour practical = incident runbook + short playbook + small infra-as-code snippet
  - Deliverable: runbook for a simulated high-latency GPU serving incident, a Terraform/CloudFormation snippet showing an autoscaling policy, and a short retro identifying the top 3 root causes and mitigations.

Candidate instructions (to send in initial email):
- You have 72 hours from receipt to submit the deliverables. Focus on clarity, tradeoffs, and a working artifact—not production polish.
- Budget: plan ~6–10 hours across the window. If you need more time, reply to request an extension (allowed once for up to 48 hours).
- We provide starter templates, a sandbox repo, and a dataset where applicable.
- Compensation: $200 for >6 hours of work (USD, paid if candidate completes the practical).

Interviewer / grader guidance:
- Reviewers should be two people from the core hiring team (one primary role expert + one cross-functional reviewer). Aim for independent grading first, then a short calibration call.
- Score each deliverable on a 1–5 scale for: Correctness, Design/Tradeoffs, Clarity/Communication, Practicality/Deployability. Record short notes (2–4 bullet lines).

Scoring rubric (flat, copyable):
- Correctness (1–5): Does the artifact meet acceptance criteria? 1 = irrelevant/doesn't run; 5 = meets requirements and handles edge cases.
- Design/Tradeoffs (1–5): Shows thoughtful design, understands constraints, quantifies tradeoffs.
- Clarity/Communication (1–5): README/PR text/doc is concise, easy to follow, decisions explained.
- Practicality/Deployability (1–5): Is the solution realistic to ship (infra, testing, rollback, monitoring considered)?

Pass threshold guidance (team-level decision):
- Strong pass: avg score >= 4.0 AND no single category < 3.
- Soft pass: avg score between 3.2—3.99, discuss in calibration and prioritize followup interviews focused on weaknesses.
- Reject: avg < 3.2 OR major safety/security concern.

Interviewer calibration notes (short):
- Keep calibration sessions under 30 minutes. Share red/amber/green flags in chat prior to sync.
- Avoid grading harshly on polish; prioritize reasoning and tradeoffs.
- For PM candidates, treat the brief as a conversation starter; evaluate ability to synthesize feedback.

Example red/amber/green quick list (copyable):
- Red: Security-sensitive mistake (e.g., logging secrets), or solution would cause major production regression.
- Amber: Missing test coverage for critical paths, unclear rollout plan, or undocumented assumptions.
- Green: Thoughtful monitoring plan, clear rollback, clear rationale for tradeoffs.

Communication templates (short) — to candidate:
- Invite to practical: "We enjoyed our initial conversation. As the next step we offer a short, paid practical to better understand how you approach hands-on problems. You have 72 hours and an estimated 6–10 hour budget. Please review the attached template and reply to confirm. Compensation is provided upon completion."
- Extension approved: "Extension granted until <new date>. Please prioritize clarity and submit when ready."

Day-Zero remote-first onboarding checklist (minimal, to attach to offer/acceptance email):
- Pre-arrival: account creation requests sent (SSO, email alias), laptop shipping scheduled (if needed), NDA signed, payroll/team paperwork done.
- Day 0 access (validate): Slack, GitHub, JIRA/Linear, internal wiki, repo access for starter template, VPN/VPC access (if private deployment candidate).
- First-3-days priorities: 1) Pair with assigned buddy for office tour and tooling; 2) Complete security orientation (KMS/HSM basics, secret handling); 3) Run local starter repo and create first tiny PR; 4) Schedule 1:1 with hiring manager and mentor.
- Hardware/software quick checklist: OS image, Docker + docker-compose, Python 3.10, Node LTS, kubectl, Terraform v1.x, preferred editor configs (dotfiles link).

Mentor pairing & onboarding sprint (example 10-day mini-sprint):
- Day 1: kickoff, access checks, run starter local project, 30m product orientation.
- Day 2–3: shadow buddy, land first small PR, security/infra intro.
- Day 4–6: small feature/bug assignment with mentor pairing.
- Day 7–10: handoff and autonomy ramp, demo to team, retro on onboarding friction.

Minimal acceptance criteria for fast hires (for hiring committee):
- Completed practical with clear artifacts and independent deployable proof-of-concept (or reproducible instructions).
- Two reviewers with overall non-reject and at least one strong pass in a core category.
- Candidate demonstrates asynchronous communication (concise PR/msg updates) during the practical window.

Notes on fairness and accessibility:
- Provide alt formats on request (e.g., longer times, phone-based walk-through).
- Explicitly state in invite that candidates can reach out to request accommodations.
- Keep tasks role-representative, not biased by prior internal tooling (provide adapters/starter scripts).

Operational checklist for People Ops coordinator (copy-and-paste in ticket):
1) Create candidate sandbox repo from template and grant permissions.
2) Send practical invite + templates + compensation note.
3) Assign two reviewers and schedule 20m calibration call window after submissions.
4) Track submission and trigger payment on completion.
5) Log scores + notes to hiring packet (Confluence or candidate folder).

Open questions / things to iterate on (working):
- Should PM practicals include a short user interview simulation? (pros/cons)
- Compensation thresholds—should senior bands get higher flat rate? (proposal: scale by band: mid=$200, senior=$350)
- Integrations: can candidate github apps be used for private repos without excess admin friction?

Appendix: quick templates (copyable snippets)
- PR description template: Summary, What I changed, How to run, Testing notes, Rollout plan, Open questions.
- Runbook skeleton: Symptoms -> Immediate checks -> Mitigation steps -> Escalation path -> Post-incident notes.
- One-page product brief headers: Problem, Why now, Target users, Metrics (north star + 2 supporting), Launch plan, Risks/mitigations.

Last updated by Ava Chen 2025-09-02 — comment below with feedback or proposed changes.
