- Design/Tradeoffs (1–5): Shows thoughtful design, understands constraints, quantifies tradeoffs.
- Clarity/Communication (1–5): README/PR text/doc is concise, easy to follow, decisions explained.
- Practicality/Deployability (1–5): Is the solution realistic to ship (infra, testing, rollback, monitoring considered)?

Pass threshold guidance (team-level decision):
- Strong pass: avg score >= 4.0 AND no single category < 3.
- Soft pass: avg score between 3.2—3.99, discuss in calibration and prioritize followup interviews focused on weaknesses.
- Reject: avg < 3.2 OR major safety/security concern.

Interviewer calibration notes (short):
- Keep calibration sessions under 30 minutes. Share red/amber/green flags in chat prior to sync.
- Avoid grading harshly on polish; prioritize reasoning and tradeoffs.
- For PM candidates, treat the brief as a conversation starter; evaluate ability to synthesize feedback.

Example red/amber/green quick list (copyable):
- Red: Security-sensitive mistake (e.g., logging secrets), or solution would cause major production regression.
- Amber: Missing test coverage for critical paths, unclear rollout plan, or undocumented assumptions.
- Green: Thoughtful monitoring plan, clear rollback, clear rationale for tradeoffs.

Communication templates (short) — to candidate:
- Invite to practical: "We enjoyed our initial conversation. As the next step we offer a short, paid practical to better understand how you approach hands-on problems. You have 72 hours and an estimated 6–10 hour budget. Please review the attached template and reply to confirm. Compensation is provided upon completion."
- Extension approved: "Extension granted until <new date>. Please prioritize clarity and submit when ready."

Day-Zero remote-first onboarding checklist (minimal, to attach to offer/acceptance email):
- Pre-arrival: account creation requests sent (SSO, email alias), laptop shipping scheduled (if needed), NDA signed, payroll/team paperwork done.
- Day 0 access (validate): Slack, GitHub, JIRA/Linear, internal wiki, repo access for starter template, VPN/VPC access (if private deployment candidate).
- First-3-days priorities: 1) Pair with assigned buddy for office tour and tooling; 2) Complete security orientation (KMS/HSM basics, secret handling); 3) Run local starter repo and create first tiny PR; 4) Schedule 1:1 with hiring manager and mentor.
- Hardware/software quick checklist: OS image, Docker + docker-compose, Python 3.10, Node LTS, kubectl, Terraform v1.x, preferred editor configs (dotfiles link).

Mentor pairing & onboarding sprint (example 10-day mini-sprint):
- Day 1: kickoff, access checks, run starter local project, 30m product orientation.
- Day 2–3: shadow buddy, land first small PR, security/infra intro.
- Day 4–6: small feature/bug assignment with mentor pairing.
- Day 7–10: handoff and autonomy ramp, demo to team, retro on onboarding friction.

Minimal acceptance criteria for fast hires (for hiring committee):
- Completed practical with clear artifacts and independent deployable proof-of-concept (or reproducible instructions).
- Two reviewers with overall non-reject and at least one strong pass in a core category.
- Candidate demonstrates asynchronous communication (concise PR/msg updates) during the practical window.

Notes on fairness and accessibility:
- Provide alt formats on request (e.g., longer times, phone-based walk-through).
- Explicitly state in invite that candidates can reach out to request accommodations.
- Keep tasks role-representative, not biased by prior internal tooling (provide adapters/starter scripts).

Operational checklist for People Ops coordinator (copy-and-paste in ticket):
1) Create candidate sandbox repo from template and grant permissions.
2) Send practical invite + templates + compensation note.
3) Assign two reviewers and schedule 20m calibration call window after submissions.
4) Track submission and trigger payment on completion.
5) Log scores + notes to hiring packet (Confluence or candidate folder).

Open questions / things to iterate on (working):
- Should PM practicals include a short user interview simulation? (pros/cons)
- Compensation thresholds—should senior bands get higher flat rate? (proposal: scale by band: mid=$200, senior=$350)
- Integrations: can candidate github apps be used for private repos without excess admin friction?

Appendix: quick templates (copyable snippets)
- PR description template: Summary, What I changed, How to run, Testing notes, Rollout plan, Open questions.
- Runbook skeleton: Symptoms -> Immediate checks -> Mitigation steps -> Escalation path -> Post-incident notes.
- One-page product brief headers: Problem, Why now, Target users, Metrics (north star + 2 supporting), Launch plan, Risks/mitigations.

Last updated by Ava Chen 2025-09-02 — comment below with feedback or proposed changes.

title: 15-minute-integration-knapsack-playbook-draft
preview:
Goal: single-developer can go from checkout -> working sampleapp -> instrumented smoke run in ~15 minutes. This is intentionally compact: surface only the must-do steps, fail-safe fallbacks, and a tiny set of prompts+telemetry keys.

Context and constraints:
- Host: Redwood hosted API + local stub runner for offline workflows.
- Languages: Node + Python minimal paths.
- Assumes dev has API key and basic git/ssh setup.
- Must include a deterministic smoke test (latency + token-cost check) and an example failure fallback (model downgrade).

Runbook (15-min split):
1) 0:00-2:00 - bootstrap repo
   - git clone <repo> ; cd quick-infra-sample
   - npm i || pip install -r requirements.txt
   - copy .env.example -> .env and paste API_KEY (note: mention secrets manager later)
   - quick sanity: node ./scripts/hello-redwood.js (or python run/hello.py) -> expect status 200

2) 2:00-6:00 - wire a sample endpoint and prompt template
   - open sampleapp/src/llm-client.{js,py}: swap in REDWOOD_API_URL if needed
   - paste minimal prompt template: SYSTEM: one-liner, USER: short input
   - run local dev server (npm run dev / python -m flask run)

3) 6:00-10:00 - run instrumented smoke test (automated)
   - run ./scripts/smoke.sh (node scripts/smoke.js) which:
      * fires 3 requests (short/medium/long) to same route
      * measures latency p50/p95 and tokenCount per response
      * logs telemetry to console and to ./telemetry/quick.json in compact format
   - expected thresholds: p50 < 350ms, p95 < 1200ms for hosted small model (tunable)

4) 10:00-12:00 - simulate error / fallback
   - toggle env FALLBACK_MODE=true or force a transient 503 via stub
   - verify automatic fallback to smaller model variant and observe token delta
   - note: smoke script should exit non-zero if fallback not triggered on simulated outage

5) 12:00-15:00 - quick telemetry check + notes to prodize
   - open ./telemetry/quick.json and look for keys: route, model, latency_ms, token_usage, trace_id
   - sanity check: token_usage delta corresponds to expected prompt+response size
   - commit sample change and open PR template (pre-filled labels: dx-quickstart, smoke-passed)

Minimal dev telemetry contract (copyable):
- route: string
- model: string
- latency_ms: number
- token_usage: number
- success: true|false
- trace_id: uuid

Prompts to include in repo (tiny bank):
- default - "Summarize the following text in two bullets."
- regenerate - "Shorten the text to a 1-sentence TL;DR preserving entities."
- explain-code - "Explain what this code does in plain English (2-3 sentences)."

Failure modes & quick mitigations (notes to self):
- auth errors: instruct dev to check API key and run 'redwood whoami' (CLI TODO)
- model rate-limit spikes: smoke script should retry with exponential backoff up to 3x, then trigger fallback route
- large prompt OOM: add prompt-length validator client-side (4000 token guard for default profile)

Open questions / TODOs (unresolved):
- Should the smoke harness ship as a GH Actions workflow or keep it local-first? pros: shift-left CI; cons: noise on early PRs.
- Minimal python path currently uses requests + simple json logging. Is that enough for enterprise customers who prefer more structured traces?
- Do we include an optional step to demonstrate Dedicated vs Hosted routing? good demo but adds time. Maybe "bonus" appendix.

Appendix (bonus flows):
- Dedicated routing demo: env DEDICATED_POOL=small-pool -> run smoke -> check pool id in telemetry.
- Canary rollout snippet: add header X-Redwood-Rollout: canary -> show different model version returned.

Notes to reviewers:
- keep this under 1 page; it's intended for workshop handout and inside-repo README.
- prioritize clarity over exhaustive options; the goal is velocity and predictable observable outcomes.

Scratch ideas for follow-ups:
- one-click "knapsack" bundle in Console that pre-populates repo with .env and API key binding
- small web UI showing realtime smoke metrics produced by the scripts

End of draft.

title: QuickWire: SDK ops concepts and first-run lab notes
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact, hands-on working note for the QuickWire SDK onramp: the minimal operational concepts, the first-run lab checklist, and common recovery steps for early users. Built as a single-pane reference for solutions engineers running 30–60 minute pairing sessions and for docs authors to extract canonical commands / error messages.

Audience:
- Solutions engineers and developer advocates running live demos
- Docs writers extracting small, copyable blocks
- SDK maintainers who want the minimal operational surface defined

High-level goals (one-liners):
- Get a developer from zero -> one working call in 10 minutes.
- Surface 3 common failure modes and remediation steps.
- Capture 5 telemetry events + what to look for in the Console.
- Provide a simple local repro for flakiness.

Quick assumptions (context):
- Using Redwood Hosted API for the first session (hosted, not dedicated)
- SDK language: JavaScript (Node) primary flow; Python appendix
- Dev machine: macOS or Linux; Docker optional

Before the session (prep checklist):
- [ ] Confirm customer has a Redwood API key with test credits
- [ ] Ensure developer machine has Node 18+ and npm/yarn installed
- [ ] Install quickwire-starter package globally (or have local copy): npm i -g @redwood/quickwire-starter
- [ ] Open Console and create an empty project: Console -> Projects -> Create -> \"quickwire-demo\"
- [ ] Have a short sample dataset or prompt ready (1–2 lines)

First-run lab (10 minute path):
1) Install and init (0–2 mins)
   - mkdir quickwire-demo && cd quickwire-demo
   - npx @redwood/quickwire-starter init --template=chat
   - copy .env.example -> .env and drop in REDWOOD_API_KEY (keep scope minimal)
2) Start a local dev harness (2–4 mins)
   - npm run dev (starts local harness which proxies requests to hosted endpoint)
   - Observe logs: Should see SDK client initialize and handshake with Hosted API
3) Make a simple call (4–6 mins)
   - curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/v1/generate -d '{\"prompt\":\"Hello from QuickWire\"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'
   - Expected response: 200 + JSON with text / tokens metadata
4) Enable streaming demo (6–8 mins)
   - Toggle streaming=true in local config; restart harness
   - Use the small web UI included in the template to show token-by-token streaming
5) Wrap up and explain next steps (8–10 mins)
   - Explain pinning model versions, telemetry toggles, and rollout options

Key SDK ops concepts to highlight during demo:
- Token accounting: per-request token count visible in response header X-Redwood-Tokens
- Retry policy: SDK client default -> exponential backoff, 3 retries; configurable via clientConfig.retries
- Local caching: prefix caching is enabled by default for identical prompt hashes; map to cache headers in logs
- Streaming vs non-streaming: streaming reduces perceived latency but needs client-side buffering for UI consistency
- Model fallbacks: how the client automatically downgrades to a gen-lite model when capacity alerts are observed

Telemetry & Console checks (what to show):
- Event: client.init -> Console shows apiKey, clientVersion, region (if present)
- Event: request.sent -> payload size, prompt tokens, model hint
- Event: request.completed -> latency_ms, token_cost, model_used
- Alert: high-retry-rate -> visible on project health page (filter by route)
- Trace: failed-request -> click through to full stack trace, request body, and KV cache hit/miss

Common failure modes and remediation (pairing-friendly):
- 401 / invalid_api_key:
  - Symptoms: immediate 401; client.init succeeds but request.sent shows 401
  - Fix: confirm REDWOOD_API_KEY in .env; check for accidental trailing spaces; rotate key if needed
- Long tail latency (~3–6s spikes):
  - Symptoms: occasional requests spike compared to median
  - Fix: show Console per-route latency sparks; enable batching override to 0 (no batching) to test if batching introduces queuing; check region mismatch (dev in us-west while project pinned to eu-central)
- Streaming connection drops:
  - Symptoms: partial responses, socket close; UI shows incomplete render
  - Fix: check client network path (corporate proxy), increase keepalive and enable reconnect logic in SDK config, demonstrate local fallback to non-streaming mode

Local flakiness repro steps (for engineering handoff):
- Repro script (node):
  - run: node repro/emit-latency.js --target=http://localhost:3000/v1/generate --rate=5
  - Script will send 5 RPS and print latencies; use while toggling batching flag to validate behavior
- Collect SDK logs (set LOG_LEVEL=debug) and capture one full request trace id shown in logs for triage

Rollout gating and canary notes (what to explain to PMs):
- Canary: start 5% traffic to model-v2 with automatic rollback on error-rate > 2% or latency increase > 30%
- Data collection: sample 1% inputs for quality evals (PII redaction enabled)
- Telemetry knobs: request_sampling_rate, token_cost_threshold, high_latency_alert_ms

Copyable snippets (for docs extraction):
- Minimal client init (Node):
  const {QuickWireClient} = require('@redwood/quickwire-sdk')
  const client = new QuickWireClient({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY, region: 'auto' })
  const res = await client.generate({ model: 'redwood-chat-1', prompt: 'Hello QuickWire' })
  console.log(res.text)

- Toggle streaming (Node):
  client.generate({ streaming: true }, (token) => process.stdout.write(token))

Troubleshooting quick commands: 
- Verify key: curl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY\" https://api.redwood.ai/v1/health
- Show last 50 logs (local harness): tail -n 200 logs/dev.log | grep \"request:\" | tail -n 50
- Force no-batching: export QUICKWIRE_BATCH_SIZE=1 && npm run dev

Notes to docs team (extracts needed):
- We need one short \"10-minute quickstart\" page with only the init + call + expected output blocks. Keep it < 120 words and copy/paste friendly.
- A second small section called \"Most likely failures\" with three entries (401, latency spikes, streaming drops) with command-line fixes.

Open questions / follow-ups (to resolve before finalizing):
- Should the starter template include both streaming and non-streaming UI toggles, or keep streaming behind a flag?
- What default sample rate should we recommend for request sampling in the hosted Console for initial projects (0.5% vs 1%)?
- Does the SDK automatically redact common PII patterns before sending sampling blobs to Console? Need security confirm from Privacy team.

Appendix: Python quick path (for reference)
- pip install redwood-quickwire
- sample code:
  from redwood_quickwire import QuickWireClient
  client = QuickWireClient(api_key=os.environ['REDWOOD_API_KEY'])
  print(client.generate(prompt='hello from python'))

Quickwire lab artifacts (local):
- /repro/emit-latency.js - send synthetic load
- /templates/chat/index.html - tiny UI to show streaming tokens
- /scripts/collect-trace.sh - wrapper to call Console API and snapshot traces for a trace_id

Status and next steps:
- This is a working draft intended for pairing sessions.
- Action items: (Ava) finish short 10-min quickstart page; (Priya) confirm telemetry event names with backend; (Mateo) add the no-batching smoke script to template repo.

-- end of QuickWire working note --

title: Latency-aware GPU SKU routing and procurement notes
preview:
Purpose:
Quick working notes tying regional latency requirements and customer SLAs to SKU-level procurement decisions and routing policies. Trying to make procurement decisions more directly informed by the product-side latency sensitivity map so we avoid buying excess high-cost SKUs for low-latency-inflexible workloads and conversely avoid under-provisioning latency-critical customers.

Context / problem statement:
- We have heterogeneous SKU inventory across regions (A100-40GB, H100, L40, T4-refurb).
- Different customer routes have different latency targets: interactive-chat (p95 < 120ms), async-batch-embed (p95 < 2000ms), realtime-llm (p95 < 80ms).
- Procurement lead times vary by SKU and region (H100 orders: 12-18w; A100: 8-12w; L40: 6-10w; refurbished T4: 2-4w).
- Current procurement cadence is driven by burn-rate smoothing and price windows, not latency class needs, which creates mismatch during surge events.

High-level proposal:
1) Classify SKUs by latency capability and elasticity tier:
   - LATENCY-CRITICAL: H100 (best), A100 (good)
   - LATENCY-ACCEPTABLE: L40 (mixed)
   - LATENCY-FALLBACK: T4-refurb (cost-efficient, high latency)
2) Maintain a routed-reserve pool per region keyed by latency class (e.g., each region maintains N slots of LATENCY-CRITICAL capacity).
3) Use routing policy to prefer local LATENCY-CRITICAL for routes tagged realtime/interactive; fallback chain: local-critical -> local-acceptable -> cross-region-critical -> fallback-burst-reserve (leased)
4) Couple procurement triggers to lane-specific reorder points (not a single SKU-level ROP). ROP formula per lane: ROP_lane = (avg_daily_consumption_lane * lead_time_sku) + safety_buffer_lane
   - safety_buffer_lane = z * sigma_lane * sqrt(lead_time_sku) where z=1.65 for ~95% service level

Example calculation (rough):
- Realtime route consumes on average 400 H100 token-hours / day in us-west (consolidated to 5 instances/day depleting 2 H100-eq each)
- Lead time H100 -> 14w (~98 days) -> ROP = 5 * 98 + buffer; so naive ROP ~ 490 instance-days + buffer (~120) = ~610 instance-days -> means keep ~610 H100-instance-day equivalent in reserve (convert to units based on per-instance-day capacity)
- Operationally we express this as "slots" (1 slot = one H100 in region available for routing for a day) and then translate to number of GPUs to order when runway dips below 2 weeks.

Procurement playbook adjustments:
- Maintain a minimum per-region critical-slot floor (configurable per region by SLA weight). Default: us-east/us-west/emea each keep 30 critical slots.
- For long-lead SKUs (H100), place rolling pipeline orders every 6 weeks for staggered delivery to smooth arrival schedule.
- Introduce "latency stretch" policy: if critical-slot availability < 15% for a region and predicted consumption > +25% in 4w forecast, trigger emergency leasing window and reprioritize shipments from non-critical pools.
- Implement SKU-swap auto-rules: e.g., if A100 availability > 40% and H100 shortfall detected, route some realtime workloads to A100 with conservative throttles and monitor QoE.

Routing policy changes (implementation notes):
- Add metadata to model routes: latency_class (realtime/interactive/async), elasticity (elastic/inelastic).
- Router will consult regional SKU inventory API that returns {slots_by_latency_class, horizon_delivery_calendar} (API draft - separate doc)
- Router fallback must be auditable and include reason codes (e.g., ROUTE_FALLBACK_NO_CRITICAL_LOCAL, ROUTE_FALLBACK_CROSS_REGION_COSTS) so we can tie QoE regressions to inventory events.

Monitoring signals to drive procurement decisions:
- Critical-slot utilization (7d rolling), forecasted consumption delta (1w/4w), arrival windows (calendar of scheduled shipments), open orders and uncertainty score (supplier lead variance), price volatility index
- New metric: Latency Degradation Events (LDEs) per route = count of fallbacks that increased p95 above SLA by > 10% in a week. Use LDEs to weight procurement prioritization in next cycle.

Risk matrix (quick):
- Risk: Long lead time + demand surge (H100): Impact HIGH, Likelihood MED. Mitigation: rolling orders, lease buffer, cross-region rebalancing playbook.
- Risk: Overbuy A100 (price spike recedes): Impact MED, Likelihood MED. Mitigation: convertible leases/short contracts, resale channel planning.
- Risk: Network egress/regulatory constraints preventing cross-region fallback: Impact HIGH, Likelihood LOW-MED. Mitigation: pre-approve cross-region agreements for key customers; keep extra local fallback.

Open questions / discussion points:
- What is the minimum acceptable LDE rate before procurement budget is reallocated? (current guess: > 3 LDEs / route / month triggers exec escalation)
- Capacity accounting: do we express slots in "instance-days" or "GPU-seconds"? Ops prefers instance-days for human planning; cost team prefers GPU-seconds. Need a conversion standard.
- Should we tie procurement cadence to feature launches / marketing events? (e.g., major product release scheduled -> pre-buys)
- How to incorporate price windows and spot opportunities without breaking latency commitments? Do we allow spot-backed critical slots with rapid fallback?

Action items (owners / rough ETA):
- Diego: mockup inventory API response schema and router integration wireframes (ETA 2025-03-12)
- Priya: run retrospective on last 12 months of LDEs by route and region, produce baseline thresholds (ETA 2025-03-18)
- Liam: procurement negotiation notes for convertible-lease terms for A100/H100 suppliers (ETA 2025-03-22)
- Ava: synthesize this doc into a short exec slide (1-2 slides) tying LDE cost to expected procurement delta (ETA 2025-03-09)

Notes to self / meeting fragments:
- (2025-02-25 sync) Product asked whether we can promise p95 < 100ms globally for "realtime-llm" tier — currently impossible without >50% H100 saturation in some regions. Need to align SLAs.
- Mentioned potential temporary measure: reduce max sequence length for realtime routes during congestion to reduce per-request GPU-time and ease critical-slot pressure. Tradeoff: some model quality loss.

Quick appendix: sample daily dashboard KPIs we need to expose to procurement/product teams:
- critical_slot_fill_rate (per-region)
- 4w demand delta forecast (percent)
- open_long_lead_orders (count & ETA distribution)
- LDE_count_by_route
- average_fallback_distance_km (proxy for cross-region cost)

References & links:
- Jira INFRA-812 (routing rules revamp)
- confluence: procurement-cadence-v2 (pipeline staging playbook)
- github: repo/infra-pr#482 (router inventory API proto)

Unresolved: finalizing z value for safety buffer (1.65 vs 2.33) depending on acceptable stockout risk. Leaning 1.65 for 95% SLA but open to tightening for top-10 customers.

End of notes.
text:
Purpose:
Quick working notes tying regional latency requirements and customer SLAs to SKU-level procurement decisions and routing policies. Trying to make procurement decisions more directly informed by the product-side latency sensitivity map so we avoid buying excess high-cost SKUs for low-latency-inflexible workloads and conversely avoid under-provisioning latency-critical customers.

Context / problem statement:
- We have heterogeneous SKU inventory across regions (A100-40GB, H100, L40, T4-refurb).
- Different customer routes have different latency targets: interactive-chat (p95 < 120ms), async-batch-embed (p95 < 2000ms), realtime-llm (p95 < 80ms).
- Procurement lead times vary by SKU and region (H100 orders: 12-18w; A100: 8-12w; L40: 6-10w; refurbished T4: 2-4w).
- Current procurement cadence is driven by burn-rate smoothing and price windows, not latency class needs, which creates mismatch during surge events.

High-level proposal:
1) Classify SKUs by latency capability and elasticity tier:
   - LATENCY-CRITICAL: H100 (best), A100 (good)
   - LATENCY-ACCEPTABLE: L40 (mixed)
   - LATENCY-FALLBACK: T4-refurb (cost-efficient, high latency)
2) Maintain a routed-reserve pool per region keyed by latency class (e.g., each region maintains N slots of LATENCY-CRITICAL capacity).
3) Use routing policy to prefer local LATENCY-CRITICAL for routes tagged realtime/interactive; fallback chain: local-critical -> local-acceptable -> cross-region-critical -> fallback-burst-reserve (leased)
4) Couple procurement triggers to lane-specific reorder points (not a single SKU-level ROP). ROP formula per lane: ROP_lane = (avg_daily_consumption_lane * lead_time_sku) + safety_buffer_lane
   - safety_buffer_lane = z * sigma_lane * sqrt(lead_time_sku) where z=1.65 for ~95% service level

Example calculation (rough):
- Realtime route consumes on average 400 H100 token-hours / day in us-west (consolidated to 5 instances/day depleting 2 H100-eq each)
- Lead time H100 -> 14w (~98 days) -> ROP = 5 * 98 + buffer; so naive ROP ~ 490 instance-days + buffer (~120) = ~610 instance-days -> means keep ~610 H100-instance-day equivalent in reserve (convert to units based on per-instance-day capacity)

- For long-lead SKUs (H100), place rolling pipeline orders every 6 weeks for staggered delivery to smooth arrival schedule.
- Introduce "latency stretch" policy: if critical-slot availability < 15% for a region and predicted consumption > +25% in 4w forecast, trigger emergency leasing window and reprioritize shipments from non-critical pools.
- Implement SKU-swap auto-rules: e.g., if A100 availability > 40% and H100 shortfall detected, route some realtime workloads to A100 with conservative throttles and monitor QoE.

- Add metadata to model routes: latency_class (realtime/interactive/async), elasticity (elastic/inelastic).
- Router will consult regional SKU inventory API that returns {slots_by_latency_class, horizon_delivery_calendar} (API draft - separate doc)
- Router fallback must be auditable and include reason codes (e.g., ROUTE_FALLBACK_NO_CRITICAL_LOCAL, ROUTE_FALLBACK_CROSS_REGION_COSTS) so we can tie QoE regressions to inventory events.

Monitoring signals to drive procurement decisions:
- Critical-slot utilization (7d rolling), forecasted consumption delta (1w/4w), arrival windows (calendar of scheduled shipments), open orders and uncertainty score (supplier lead variance), price volatility index
- New metric: Latency Degradation Events (LDEs) per route = count of fallbacks that increased p95 above SLA by > 10% in a week. Use LDEs to weight procurement prioritization in next cycle.

Risk matrix (quick):
- Risk: Long lead time + demand surge (H100): Impact HIGH, Likelihood MED. Mitigation: rolling orders, lease buffer, cross-region rebalancing playbook.
- Risk: Overbuy A100 (price spike recedes): Impact MED, Likelihood MED. Mitigation: convertible leases/short contracts, resale channel planning.
- Risk: Network egress/regulatory constraints preventing cross-region fallback: Impact HIGH, Likelihood LOW-MED. Mitigation: pre-approve cross-region agreements for key customers; keep extra local fallback.

title: Onramp Toolchain: Mini-lab checklist + ops scratchpad
preview:
Purpose:
A compact, operational mini-lab for new SDK integrators (internal SEs, early pilot customers). Goal is to give a reproducible first 30–60 minute experience that: 1) shows a minimal happy path, 2) surfaces common failure modes, and 3) documents quick remediation steps for SRE/onsite support. Treat as a living scratchpad for ops/playbook items to fold into the canonical quickstart later.

Context / constraints:
- Lightweight: assume user has a dev laptop and access token only (no infra or Dedicated).
- Focus: Hosted API flows + local debugging patterns (SDK calls, streaming, embeddings).
- Timebox: ~45 minute guided run, plus 15 minutes of failure rehearsals.
- Audience: SDK adopters, SEs running workshops, and on-call engineers who will triage early pilot issues.

Mini-lab outline (high level):
1) Setup (5–8m)
   - Install: pip install redwood-sdk==0.9.x (or yarn for JS)
   - Export token: export REDWOOD_API_KEY=sk-...
   - Clone tiny-example repo: git clone https://github.com/redwood-inference/onramp-mini-labs && cd onramp-mini-labs
   - Run: python quickstart_textgen.py (should produce a short generated phrase)

2) Core tasks (20m)
   - Text generation: sample prompt -> verify streaming vs non-streaming behavior.
   - Chat/role calls: run chat_sample.py with system/instruction role switching.
   - Embeddings: run embed_demo.py and confirm vector shape + sample nearest-neighbor search in readme dataset.
   - Error handling: induce 401 (bad key), 429 (rate limit), and 500 (server error) to observe SDK error structures.

3) Ops & instrumentation checks (10m)
   - Verify telemetry: check local debug logs (REDWOOD_DEBUG=1) and a sample entry in the Console mock (link in repo).
   - Tracing: run with X-Request-ID header and search trace id in the console traces page.
   - Cost estimate: run token-count script (scripts/token_cost_estimate.py) on a sample workload file.

Failure scenarios to rehearse (quick scripts included):
- Bad token: how to detect and message to user (suggest next step: rotate key, test with curl).
- Network flakiness: emulate high latency (tc/netem) and confirm SDK retries/backoff and circuit-breaker triggers.
- Model mismatch: user requests an incompatible function-calling shape -> SDK validation error path and suggested remediation.
- Streaming disconnects: partial responses and how to resume vs fallback polling.
- Silent degradations: embedding drift (vocab changes) — quick heuristics to catch regression (embedding-space drift check script).

Quick commands (copy-paste):
- install (py): pip install redwood-cli redwood-sdk
- test call (curl): curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" -d '{"input":"Hello world"}' https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate | jq
- debug logs: REDWOOD_DEBUG=1 python quickstart_textgen.py 2>&1 | tee ~/mini-lab-logs.txt
- force 429 (dev): python simulate_rate_limit.py --rps 200

Example prompt snippets (to use in lab):
- Simple: "Summarize the README in one sentence."
- Chat: system: "You are a helpful assistant." user: "Draft a short onboarding email for a new SDK user."
- Function call (mock): "Extract name and company from this text and return JSON {name, company}" (script present in repo).

Telemetry expectations (what to watch in Console):
- Request latency distribution for calls from workshop region (p50/p95/p99).
- Token counts per request and bucketed cost estimate.
- Error rate spikes correlated with participant time windows.

Acceptance criteria for a successful mini-lab run:
- Participant runs quickstart and gets a valid generation within the timebox.
- Participants can reproduce a 401/429 and apply the suggested remediation.
- Local logs show request_id and latency; trace is viewable in Console.
- Embedding nearest-neighbor demo returns >70% topical relevance on curated test set.

Known rough edges / TODOs (ops-oriented):
- Add one-click repro script that rotates keys safely for demo accounts. (TODO @Diego)
- Improve inline error messages for function-calling shape mismatches (tracking PR #342).
- Add short video (2–3 min) showing how to open a trace and interpret slow token sources. (TODO @Priya)
- Align token-cost script with pricing changes expected in March 2025.

Open questions / discussion points:
- Should the mini-lab default to the nearest hosted region or let the user pick? Pros: lower latency vs extra friction.
- For workshops with >20 participants, recommend rate-limit sandboxing per seat — do we provide automated per-seat tokens?
- What telemetry retention is sufficient for debugging ephemeral workshop runs?

After-action / next steps (short):
1) Ship repo + one-click cloud runner (Samir owns).
2) Fold stabilized checklist into the canonical quickstart (track in confluence page linked above).
3) Run internal SE rehearsal and capture video for SE handbook.

Notes to self: keep language minimal and prescriptive in final copy. This scratchpad is intentionally messy — capture the failure modes and commands that save time in a live session.

Useful links and artifacts (quick):
- Mini-lab repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/onramp-mini-labs
- Trace viewer sandbox: https://console.redwood.ai/traces/demo-workshop
- Related linear ticket: SDK-214

Footer: created in a frantic whiteboarding session — move content into a two-column quickstart + a one-page ops runbook for on-call.

title: SIEM export SLAs, data contract & onboarding matrix
preview:
Purpose:
This working doc defines the customer-facing data contract, operational SLAs, security requirements (RBAC v2 expectations), audit log schema mappings, and evidence-automation behaviour for Redwood's SIEM export connectors (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs API, Azure Sentinel/Log Analytics). It is intended for Security, SRE, Solutions Eng, and customer onboarding teams.

Scope and goals:
- Provide a vendor-neutral data contract and vendor-specific mapping guidance so customers can validate integration without deep platform knowledge.
- Define export SLAs for latency, delivery guarantees, and throughput for contract language with Dedicated/Private customers.
- Prescribe RBAC roles and least-privilege requirements for connectors and onboarding playbooks.
- Document audit log schema and evidence automation behaviour so SIEM rules and retention policies can be configured pre-onboard.

High-level export modes:
1) Push (recommended for Hosted): Redwood -> SIEM HTTP endpoints (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, Azure Data Collector). Uses TLS1.2+ and API-key/HMAC auth.
2) Pull (customer-managed): Customer polls S3 manifests (for Private) and ingests events.
3) Transit via forwarder (customer-provided Splunk HeavyForwarder or Datadog Agent) for environments that require egress control.

Vendor-specific notes:
- Splunk (HEC):
  - Endpoint: /services/collector/event/1.0 or HEC batch endpoint.
  - Auth: HEC token (rotateable).
  - Required fields: time, host, source, sourcetype, index. Redwood will map internal event_type -> sourcetype by default; customer can override.
  - Max batch size: 5MB / 1000 events recommended.
  - Recommended sourcetypes: redwood:audit, redwood:telemetry, redwood:evidence.

- Datadog (Logs API):
  - Endpoint: https://http-intake.logs.datadoghq.com/v1/input
  - Auth: API key, optional Datadog forwarder key.
  - Required attributes: ddsource, ddtags, service. Redwood will set ddsource=redwood and include ddtags=customer:<org_id>.
  - Rate guidance: customers should reserve capacity for sustained high-throughput exports; Datadog enforces ingestion limits per account.

- Azure Sentinel / Log Analytics (HTTP Data Collector API):
  - Auth: Workspace ID + Shared Key or Managed Identity when Redwood is in-customer VNet (Private).
  - Format: JSON array of records; map to custom log type: Redwood_Audit_CL or Redwood_Evidence_CL.
  - Note: Azure enforces size/record limits; connector will chunk background uploads and report partial failures in audit logs.

Data contract (vendor-agnostic):
- Every exported event will contain the following canonical fields (internal -> exported name):
  - timestamp -> timestamp (RFC3339)
  - actor_id -> actor.id
  - actor_type -> actor.type (user | system | service)
  - action -> event.action (string, e.g., \"model.invoke\", \"policy.update\")
  - resource -> resource.id
  - resource_type -> resource.type (model|route|deployment|dataset)
  - outcome -> event.outcome (success|failure|partial)
  - correlation_id -> trace.correlation_id
  - request_id -> trace.request_id
  - tenant_id -> tenant.id
  - ip_address -> network.ip
  - user_agent -> network.user_agent
  - evidence_url -> evidence.artifact_url (optional, signed URL)
  - evidence_hash -> evidence.sha256 (optional)

- Additional metadata: deployment.cluster, region, model.version, quantization_profile (when applicable).

Audit log schema mapping examples (Splunk-formatted example):
{
  \"time\": \"2025-02-10T18:14:22Z\",
  \"host\": \"redwood-inference\",
  \"sourcetype\": \"redwood:audit\",
  \"index\": \"customer-1234\",
  \"event\": {
    \"actor\": {\"id\": \"u-9982\",\"type\":\"user\"},
    \"action\": \"model.rollout.start\",
    \"resource\": {\"id\":\"m-42\",\"type\":\"model\"},
    \"outcome\": \"success\",
    \"trace\": {\"correlation_id\":\"c-9f2a\",\"request_id\":\"r-44\"},
    \"evidence\": {\"artifact_url\": \"https://s3.amazonaws.com/…\",\"sha256\": \"abc123…\"}
  }
}

Evidence automation behaviour:
- When an event includes an evidence artifact (policy doc, signed manifest, model checksum), Redwood will:
  1) Persist artifact to customer-scoped evidence bucket (or customer-managed S3 for Private).
  2) Compute sha256 and sign the manifest with KMS (platform KMS for Hosted; customer KMS for Private when configured).
  3) Emit the signed artifact_url and evidence_hash in the event; include signed_expiry (ISO8601) if URL is time-limited.
  4) Attach an evidence.meta object with proof-of-origin: {signer: \"redwood-kms\", signature: \"base64...\", signed_at: \"2025-02-10T18:14:22Z\"}.
- For SIEMs that cannot accept nested objects, evidence fields are flattened: evidence_artifact_url, evidence_sha256, evidence_signer, evidence_signed_at.

RBAC v2 expectations for connector principals:
- Roles (minimum set for onboarding):
  1) siem-export-admin: configure endpoints, rotate keys, disable exports, view error logs.
  2) siem-export-operator: read-only on exports, retry failed batches, view metrics.
  3) siem-export-viewer: view metadata and sample events only.
- Principle of least privilege: Customer should provide one purpose-scoped API key/secret per environment (prod/stage). For Private deployments, prefer Managed Identity or service principal with limited RBAC to the workspace/ingest API.

Operational SLAs (recommended contract clauses):
- Latency: 95th percentile delivery latency from Redwood emission to SIEM ingestion endpoint acknowledgement <= 10s for Hosted -> Splunk/Datadog within same region. For cross-region or Private, specify region-aware SLA.
- Delivery guarantee: Best effort for Hosted public exports; Guaranteed-delivery backed by durable queues for Dedicated/Private with at-least-once semantics and at most 3 retries for transient failures.
- Backfill window: Redwood retains export-ready events for up to 7 days by default for Dedicated; can be extended to 30 days by contract. Backfill operations are rate-limited and scheduled.
- Throughput: baseline contract should define tokens/sec or events/sec per customer. Example: 5000 events/sec sustained with burst to 15k for 60s.
- Error budget & escalation: define SLO, and threshold for automated failover (route to alternate region or degrade to metadata-only exports).

Acceptance tests for onboarding (minimum):
1) Field presence: validate canonical fields present and correct types.
2) Vendor mapping: one sample event appears in vendor UI with expected sourcetype/ddsource/log_type.
3) Auth rotation: rotate token/key and validate connection remains stable after update within 5 minutes.
4) Throughput ramp: validate advertised throughput at 50%, 75%, 100% for 5-minute windows.
5) Evidence link resolution: evidence URL resolves and sha256 matches delivered artifact.

Failure modes and recovery guidance:
- 4xx from SIEM: treat as config/auth failures, alert customer and pause export; do not retry until fix.
- 5xx / 429: transient; retry with exponential backoff and queue; escalate if > 5% of events fail for 15 minutes.
- Signed URL expiry: auto-refresh on pre-expiry signal; if refresh fails, emit evidence_missing event for SIEM.

Operational metrics to expose to customer console:
- delivered_events_count, failed_events_count, avg_latency_ms_p95, current_queue_depth, last_error_code, backfill_progress_pct, evidence_signed_count.

Onboarding checklist (customer-facing):
- Confirm endpoint details and preferred ingest method (HEC/Datadog/ADC/forwarder).
- Provide API keys or grant managed identity with minimal permissions.
- Agree on index/log-type mapping and retention expectations.
- Define throughput profile and SLA (sustained and burst).
- Provide contact for on-call escalation and security review.
- If evidence artifacts are required, confirm customer storage options (customer S3 or Redwood-managed) and KMS configuration.

Open items for product/security team:
- Finalize evidence.meta format to reduce nested payloads for legacy SIEMs.
- Define standard sourcetypes/log_type names for out-of-the-box dashboards.
- Add automated compliance check that validates RBAC scoping for new customer API keys.

Appendix: example Datadog event (flattened):
{
  \"message\": \"model.invoke succeeded\",
  \"ddsource\": \"redwood\",
  \"service\": \"redwood-inference\",
  \"ddtags\": \"customer:customer-1234,env:prod\",
  \"timestamp\": \"2025-02-10T18:14:22Z\",
  \"actor_id\": \"u-9982\",
  \"action\": \"model.invoke\",
  \"resource_id\": \"m-42\",
  \"outcome\": \"success\",
  \"evidence_artifact_url\": \"https://s3.amazonaws.com/…\",
  \"evidence_sha256\": \"abc123...\"
}

Contact: Ava Chen (security) — for questions and sign-off.

Notes: This is intentionally a working document that combines customer-contract language with operational playbook. Finalized sections will be moved into the product playbook and customer onboarding template library after review.
text:
Purpose:
This working doc defines the customer-facing data contract, operational SLAs, security requirements (RBAC v2 expectations), audit log schema mappings, and evidence-automation behaviour for Redwood's SIEM export connectors (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs API, Azure Sentinel/Log Analytics). It is intended for Security, SRE, Solutions Eng, and customer onboarding teams.

Scope and goals:
- Provide a vendor-neutral data contract and vendor-specific mapping guidance so customers can validate integration without deep platform knowledge.
- Define export SLAs for latency, delivery guarantees, and throughput for contract language with Dedicated/Private customers.
- Prescribe RBAC roles and least-privilege requirements for connectors and onboarding playbooks.
- Document audit log schema and evidence automation behaviour so SIEM rules and retention policies can be configured pre-onboard.

High-level export modes:
1) Push (recommended for Hosted): Redwood -> SIEM HTTP endpoints (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, Azure Data Collector). Uses TLS1.2+ and API-key/HMAC auth.
2) Pull (customer-managed): Customer polls S3 manifests (for Private) and ingests events.
3) Transit via forwarder (customer-provided Splunk HeavyForwarder or Datadog Agent) for environments that require egress control.

Vendor-specific notes:
- Splunk (HEC):
  - Endpoint: /services/collector/event/1.0 or HEC batch endpoint.
  - Auth: HEC token (rotateable).
  - Required fields: time, host, source, sourcetype, index. Redwood will map internal event_type -> sourcetype by default; customer can override.
  - Max batch size: 5MB / 1000 events recommended.
  - Recommended sourcetypes: redwood:audit, redwood:telemetry, redwood:evidence.

- Datadog (Logs API):
  - Endpoint: https://http-intake.logs.datadoghq.com/v1/input
  - Auth: API key, optional Datadog forwarder key.
  - Required attributes: ddsource, ddtags, service. Redwood will set ddsource=redwood and include ddtags=customer:<org_id>.
  - Rate guidance: customers should reserve capacity for sustained high-throughput exports; Datadog enforces ingestion limits per account.

- Azure Sentinel / Log Analytics (HTTP Data Collector API):
  - Auth: Workspace ID + Shared Key or Managed Identity when Redwood is in-customer VNet (Private).
  - Format: JSON array of records; map to custom log type: Redwood_Audit_CL or Redwood_Evidence_CL.
  - Note: Azure enforces size/record limits; connector will chunk background uploads and report partial failures in audit logs.

Data contract (vendor-agnostic):
- Every exported event will contain the following canonical fields (internal -> exported name):
  - timestamp -> timestamp (RFC3339)
  - actor_id -> actor.id
  - actor_type -> actor.type (user | system | service)
  - action -> event.action (string, e.g., \"model.invoke\", \"policy.update\")
  - resource -> resource.id
  - resource_type -> resource.type (model|route|deployment|dataset)
  - outcome -> event.outcome (success|failure|partial)
  - correlation_id -> trace.correlation_id
  - request_id -> trace.request_id
  - tenant_id -> tenant.id
  - ip_address -> network.ip
  - user_agent -> network.user_agent
  - evidence_url -> evidence.artifact_url (optional, signed URL)
  - evidence_hash -> evidence.sha256 (optional)

- Additional metadata: deployment.cluster, region, model.version, quantization_profile (when applicable).

Audit log schema mapping examples (Splunk-formatted example):
{
  \"time\": \"2025-02-10T18:14:22Z\",
  \"host\": \"redwood-inference\",
  \"sourcetype\": \"redwood:audit\",
  \"index\": \"customer-1234\",
  \"event\": {
    \"actor\": {\"id\": \"u-9982\",\"type\":\"user\"},
    \"action\": \"model.rollout.start\",
    \"resource\": {\"id\":\"m-42\",\"type\":\"model\"},
    \"outcome\": \"success\",
    \"trace\": {\"correlation_id\":\"c-9f2a\",\"request_id\":\"r-44\"},
    \"evidence\": {\"artifact_url\": \"https://s3.amazonaws.com/…\",\"sha256\": \"abc123…\"}
  }
}

Evidence automation behaviour:
- When an event includes an evidence artifact (policy doc, signed manifest, model checksum), Redwood will:
  1) Persist artifact to customer-scoped evidence bucket (or customer-managed S3 for Private).
  2) Compute sha256 and sign the manifest with KMS (platform KMS for Hosted; customer KMS for Private when configured).
  3) Emit the signed artifact_url and evidence_hash in the event; include signed_expiry (ISO8601) if URL is time-limited.
  4) Attach an evidence.meta object with proof-of-origin: {signer: \"redwood-kms\", signature: \"base64...\", signed_at: \"2025-02-10T18:14:22Z\"}.
- For SIEMs that cannot accept nested objects, evidence fields are flattened: evidence_artifact_url, evidence_sha256, evidence_signer, evidence_signed_at.

RBAC v2 expectations for connector principals:
- Roles (minimum set for onboarding):
  1) siem-export-admin: configure endpoints, rotate keys, disable exports, view error logs.
  2) siem-export-operator: read-only on exports, retry failed batches, view metrics.
  3) siem-export-viewer: view metadata and sample events only.
- Principle of least privilege: Customer should provide one purpose-scoped API key/secret per environment (prod/stage). For Private deployments, prefer Managed Identity or service principal with limited RBAC to the workspace/ingest API.

Operational SLAs (recommended contract clauses):
- Latency: 95th percentile delivery latency from Redwood emission to SIEM ingestion endpoint acknowledgement <= 10s for Hosted -> Splunk/Datadog within same region. For cross-region or Private, specify region-aware SLA.
- Delivery guarantee: Best effort for Hosted public exports; Guaranteed-delivery backed by durable queues for Dedicated/Private with at-least-once semantics and at most 3 retries for transient failures.
- Backfill window: Redwood retains export-ready events for up to 7 days by default for Dedicated; can be extended to 30 days by contract. Backfill operations are rate-limited and scheduled.
- Throughput: baseline contract should define tokens/sec or events/sec per customer. Example: 5000 events/sec sustained with burst to 15k for 60s.
- Error budget & escalation: define SLO, and threshold for automated failover (route to alternate region or degrade to metadata-only exports).

Acceptance tests for onboarding (minimum):
1) Field presence: validate canonical fields present and correct types.
2) Vendor mapping: one sample event appears in vendor UI with expected sourcetype/ddsource/log_type.
3) Auth rotation: rotate token/key and validate connection remains stable after update within 5 minutes.
4) Throughput ramp: validate advertised throughput at 50%, 75%, 100% for 5-minute windows.
5) Evidence link resolution: evidence URL resolves and sha256 matches delivered artifact.

Failure modes and recovery guidance:
- 4xx from SIEM: treat as config/auth failures, alert customer and pause export; do not retry until fix.
- 5xx / 429: transient; retry with exponential backoff and queue; escalate if > 5% of events fail for 15 minutes.
- Signed URL expiry: auto-refresh on pre-expiry signal; if refresh fails, emit evidence_missing event for SIEM.

Operational metrics to expose to customer console:
- delivered_events_count, failed_events_count, avg_latency_ms_p95, current_queue_depth, last_error_code, backfill_progress_pct, evidence_signed_count.

Onboarding checklist (customer-facing):
- Confirm endpoint details and preferred ingest method (HEC/Datadog/ADC/forwarder).
- Provide API keys or grant managed identity with minimal permissions.
- Agree on index/log-type mapping and retention expectations.
- Define throughput profile and SLA (sustained and burst).
- Provide contact for on-call escalation and security review.
- If evidence artifacts are required, confirm customer storage options (customer S3 or Redwood-managed) and KMS configuration.

Open items for product/security team:
- Finalize evidence.meta format to reduce nested payloads for legacy SIEMs.
- Define standard sourcetypes/log_type names for out-of-the-box dashboards.
- Add automated compliance check that validates RBAC scoping for new customer API keys.

Appendix: example Datadog event (flattened):
{
  \"message\": \"model.invoke succeeded\",
  \"ddsource\": \"redwood\",
  \"service\": \"redwood-inference\",
  \"ddtags\": \"customer:customer-1234,env:prod\",
  \"timestamp\": \"2025-02-10T18:14:22Z\",
  \"actor_id\": \"u-9982\",
  \"action\": \"model.invoke\",
  \"resource_id\": \"m-42\",
  \"outcome\": \"success\",
  \"evidence_artifact_url\": \"https://s3.amazonaws.com/…\",
  \"evidence_sha256\": \"abc123...\"
}

Contact: Ava Chen (security) — for questions and sign-off.

Notes: This is intentionally a working document that combines customer-contract language with operational playbook. Finalized sections will be moved into the product playbook and customer onboarding template library after review.

title: GPU procurement sprint ledger & ops notes
preview:
Purpose:
Quick, operational sprint ledger for short-to-medium term GPU replenishment and region-level redistribution. This is a working tracker for upcoming orders, inbound ETAs, swap-lane candidates, and immediate mitigation actions if lead-times slip. Intentionally informal; use as single source for on-call and procurement syncs this sprint.

High-level summary (as of 2025-03-04):
- Net burn forecast next 8 weeks: 54 p100-equivalent GPU-weeks (adjusted for quantization and packing).
- Critical low regions: eu-west (on-demand reserve < 7 days), apac-sg (forecasted deficit in week of 2025-03-18).
- Current action cadence: 2x procurement touchpoints per week; daily swap-lane review during EMEA business hours.

Active order ledger (short view):
- Order 2025-01-28-A: 40x A2000 (region: us-west) — ETA 2025-03-22 (confirmed partial shipment 2025-03-02).
- Order 2025-02-07-B: 16x H100 (region: eu-west) — ETA rolling 2025-04-10 to 2025-04-25 (vendor slack: continental freight delays).
- Spot-reserve tranche 2025-02-25-C: 24x T4 (multiregional pool) — ETA 2025-03-12 (arrived 2025-03-05; allocated to staging).

SKU mix rationale notes:
- Short window: prefer smaller, cheaper cards to rapidly refill dev/test and internal eval pools (A2000/T4).
- Mid-term: H100/H200 tranche hedge for peak production inference capacity (pin to Dedicated customers), proceed only if vendor lead-times <= 60d.
- Avoid over-committing to older gen at scale; prioritize transit speed and availability over marginal per-card perf for upcoming 3-month window.

Region redistribution and swap lanes (operational tactics):
- Swap-lane candidate: move 12x A2000 from us-east staging to eu-west by 2025-03-07 (air freight vs sea; air recommended given eu-west < 7 day buffer).
- Cross-region buffer policy: maintain 10% of booked capacity as cross-region hot swap (target: 48 GPUs global).
- If H100 tranche delays exceed 21 days beyond vendor ETA, trigger fallback: repurpose 8x T4/H100-compatible quantized variants in eu-west + enable burst to us-west dedicated pool.

Lead-time risk matrix (quick):
- Low risk: on-hand staging, local resellers (lead-time 0–14d).
- Medium risk: standard vendor fulfillment (lead-time 15–45d) — watch shipping windows 2025-03-15 to 2025-03-28 (vendor blackout).
- High risk: large OEM batch orders (45+d) — require hedging with spot buys + reseller top-ups.

Burn and utilization assumptions used in forecast:
- Baseline token-driven inference load converted to GPU-weeks using microbenchmarks: p100-equiv metric (p100-eq).
- Spike allowance 25% for launch flakiness; we assume 3 days to spin up burst capacity for Dedicated customers.
- Autoscaler behavior: minimum per-region pools kept active (configurable threshold 50% CPU cap, 40% mem).

Open questions / TODOs (sorted):
- [ ] Confirm exact courier cutoff for eu-west expedited air on 2025-03-09 (Priya).
- [ ] Validate customs window for APAC-SG: need importer paperwork completed by 2025-03-10 to prevent 7–10d hold (Diego).
- [ ] Recalculate p100-eq conversion after latest microbench (Samir) — preliminary suggests A2000 nominally 0.18 p100-eq.
- [ ] Decide whether to bill Dedicated customers for short-term burst (product policy sync needed with sales).

Contingency playbook (brief):
- If inbound H100 tranche is delayed >21d: 1) increase quantization recommended by Optimize for selected heavy routes; 2) activate model-routing fallback to lighter variant; 3) short-term purchase of 12x H100 from reseller with expedited shipping (finance pre-approval required).
- If regional customs hold occurs: escalate to customs broker, route part of shipment via alternate port (extra cost estimate: +12% freight).

Quick table (text):
- Weeks 0-2: priority refill = dev/test + staging (A2000/T4); target arrival share >= 75% of short-window orders.
- Weeks 3-6: deploy H100 tranche as it arrives; gradually rebalance regional pools (move A2000 to dev/test after validation).

Meeting cadence / ownership:
- Procurement sync: Tue/Thu 09:00 PT (owner: Ava Chen) — update active ledger and open items.
- On-call ops: daily 08:30 PT quick triage when any ETA slips >3 days (owner: Priya Nair).
- Finance pre-approvals: ad-hoc, flagged by procurement when >= $250k incremental.

Notes to self (raw):
- Keep small list of resellers that can provide partial pallets — good for mitigating long OEM lead-times.
- Track SNs as soon as devices hit staging (barcode scan step missing in current runbook).
- Add quick link in console to show live pool counts by SKU & region (product ask: low effort, high value).

Change log (edits):
- 2025-02-10 created initial ledger and burn model baseline.
- 2025-02-25 added spot-reserve tranche and updated ETA for order A.
- 2025-03-02 marked partial shipment arrived for Order A and moved 24x T4 into staging.
- 2025-03-04 updated regional buffer policy and added contingency triggers.

Use this doc as the sprint-facing operational ledger. If anything needs escalation to exec or finance, convert to a short one-pager and ping on #infra-procurement channel.

End of note.

title: Sidecar LLM Adapter Onboarding Sprint Plan (pilot)
preview:
Purpose:
A compact sprint plan and quickstart outline for the Sidecar LLM Adapter pilot. Goal is to validate a 'sidecar' integration pattern for customer SDKs that keeps model serving concerns separate from app developers' runtime and reduces friction when upgrading runtime or switching deployment modes (hosted/dedicated/private).

Background and rationale:
- Customers ask for minimal code changes to adopt Redwood APIs; sidecar pattern can provide a local HTTP/gRPC adapter that exposes simple SDK-compatible endpoints while proxying/optimizing to Redwood runtime.
- Allows teams to ship faster, keep tokens/telemetry consistent, and support feature toggles (caching, batching, fallbacks) in the sidecar rather than app code.

Sprint goals (4 weeks):
1) MVP sidecar binary + container image: config-driven, minimal footprint.
2) Quickstart docs and one-page runbook for developers + example app using JS SDK and Python.
3) E2E test harness: CI job that boots sidecar + local server + smoke tests for generation and embeddings.
4) Instrumentation: basic telemetry, healthcheck, and local toggles for cache/batching.

Success metrics:
- Time to first successful request with example app <= 5 minutes (from git clone).
- SDK integration required <= 5 lines of code change in the host app.
- CI smoke tests passing in <10m with <2 flaky runs per 50.
- Developer feedback: >= 4/5 in pilot cohort survey (ease of onboarding).

Sprint timeline (high-level):
Week 0 (pre-sprint): design + RFC, agree on protocol (gRPC vs HTTP JSON), image naming and config schema.
Week 1: sidecar core (HTTP proxy, config loader), minimal logging, health endpoint.
Week 2: SDK adapters (node + python) + quickstart example apps. Docs draft.
Week 3: CI harness + telemetry + run smoke tests. Iterate docs with dev feedback.
Week 4: polish + small pilot with internal SDK users, fix blockers, prepare handoff.

Deliverables:
- sidecar-adapter v0.1 docker image (linux/amd64)
- node-adapter example: quickstart snippet + tiny wrapper library
- python-adapter example: context manager + minimal wrapper
- docs: "sidecar quickstart" one-pager + troubleshooting checklist
- CI: github actions workflow that runs smoke tests on PRs

Quickstart outline (one-pager flow):
1) Prereqs: docker, git, node/py runtime
2) git clone example repo
3) docker run --env REDWOOD_API_KEY=... redwood/sidecar-adapter:v0.1 --config /tmp/sidecar.yaml
4) npm install @redwood/sidecar-adapter
5) node example/app.js (example shows 3 lines to send a prompt)
6) verify response, check /health and /metrics endpoints

Example snippet (Node) - minimal wrapper:
const Sidecar = require('@redwood/sidecar-adapter');
const client = new Sidecar({ baseURL: 'http://localhost:8080' });
await client.generate({ prompt: 'Summarize release notes' });

Docs checklist (what to include):
- TL;DR one-liner, architecture diagram
- Install + run commands
- Env var list and config schema (timeouts, batch size, cache TTL)
- Common errors and fixes (port collisions, credentials, CORS)
- Troubleshooting: how to run with verbose logs, capture a request sample for support
- Security note: sidecar should be deployed in same tenant/network, avoid exposing to public internet; include KMS notes for private mode

Risks and mitigation:
- Sidecar introduces another surface to maintain. Keep API minimal and stable.
- Performance overhead if sidecar naïvely proxies synchronous calls. Mitigate: async edge, local batching and KV cache.
- Complexity for private deployments: provide config templates and automated installers.

Open questions (to resolve early):
- Must we support both gRPC and HTTP? (tradeoffs: gRPC binary efficiency vs HTTP ubiquity)
- How to surface model variants and rollouts in sidecar config? (local pin vs control-plane-driven)
- Telemetry: which tags must be forwarded vs redacted by default?

Action items & owners (initial):
- RFC + interface sketch — Marcus Rivera — due 2025-02-20
- PoC sidecar server skeleton — Ava Chen — due 2025-02-28
- Node + Python adapter examples — Priya Nambiar — due 2025-03-07
- CI harness and smoke tests — Ethan Park — due 2025-03-10
- One-pager quickstart + runbook — Ava Chen — due 2025-03-03

Pilot checklist for internal testers:
- [ ] Pull image, run sidecar locally
- [ ] Integrate node adapter in sample app, run tests
- [ ] Try failover: flip sidecar to fallback mode and confirm app behavior
- [ ] Measure latency/wall time for 3 prompt sizes
- [ ] Submit feedback in internal survey (link below)

Notes to self / TODOs:
- Keep docs concise; aim for a single terminal copy-paste path for first-time devs.
- Record short screencast for onboarding (30–60s) — high leverage.
- Consider bundling a minimal CLI for common ops (start, stop, config-check, logs)

Survey & feedback link (internal): https://forms.redwood.ai/s/sidecar-pilot-feedback

Raw links and references:
- RFC: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DX/Sidecar+Adapter+RFC
- Jira: DX-789
- Example repo (internal): https://github.com/redwood-inference/sidecar-adapter

End of doc — keep iterating; this is intentionally compact so we can paste into PR descriptions and the sprint board.

- Env var list and config schema (timeouts, batch size, cache TTL)

- Troubleshooting: how to run with verbose logs, capture a request sample for support
- Security note: sidecar should be deployed in same tenant/network, avoid exposing to public internet; include KMS notes for private mode

Risks and mitigation:
- Sidecar introduces another surface to maintain. Keep API minimal and stable.
- Performance overhead if sidecar naïvely proxies synchronous calls. Mitigate: async edge, local batching and KV cache.
- Complexity for private deployments: provide config templates and automated installers.

Open questions (to resolve early):
- Must we support both gRPC and HTTP? (tradeoffs: gRPC binary efficiency vs HTTP ubiquity)
- How to surface model variants and rollouts in sidecar config? (local pin vs control-plane-driven)
- Telemetry: which tags must be forwarded vs redacted by default?

Action items & owners (initial):
- RFC + interface sketch — Marcus Rivera — due 2025-02-20
- PoC sidecar server skeleton — Ava Chen — due 2025-02-28
- Node + Python adapter examples — Priya Nambiar — due 2025-03-07
- CI harness and smoke tests — Ethan Park — due 2025-03-10
- One-pager quickstart + runbook — Ava Chen — due 2025-03-03

Pilot checklist for internal testers:
- [ ] Pull image, run sidecar locally
- [ ] Integrate node adapter in sample app, run tests
- [ ] Try failover: flip sidecar to fallback mode and confirm app behavior
- [ ] Measure latency/wall time for 3 prompt sizes
- [ ] Submit feedback in internal survey (link below)

Notes to self / TODOs:
- Keep docs concise; aim for a single terminal copy-paste path for first-time devs.
- Record short screencast for onboarding (30–60s) — high leverage.
- Consider bundling a minimal CLI for common ops (start, stop, config-check, logs)

Survey & feedback link (internal): https://forms.redwood.ai/s/sidecar-pilot-feedback

Raw links and references:
- RFC: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DX/Sidecar+Adapter+RFC
- Jira: DX-789
- Example repo (internal): https://github.com/redwood-inference/sidecar-adapter

End of doc — keep iterating; this is intentionally compact so we can paste into PR descriptions and the sprint board.

title: Peak quarter prep and Townloop brief v0-1
preview:
Purpose:
This note is the working brief for the Peak Quarter prep window. It stitches together: a concise exec-update for FY Q2 planning, a fresh OKR draft to surface at the townloop (pre-all-hands cross-team sync), and the all-hands run-of-show and preflight checklist. Use this as the single source for leads preparing slides, demos, and the CEO update.

Context and goals (why now):
- We need a short, crisp narrative to align the company on where to concentrate engineering and GTM energy for the upcoming quarter.
- Townloop: a 60-minute cross-functional rehearsal focused on alignment, risk escalation, and decision signals before the public all-hands.
- All-hands: a 45–60 minute townhall with exec update, a product highlight demo, and Q&A. The goal is clarity on priorities, commitments, and measurable outcomes.

Key timeline (draft):
- Week -3 (March 17): Distributed deadline for slide decks + one-pager summaries from each org lead.
- Week -2 (March 24): Townloop rehearsal (60m). Execs, PM leads, eng leads, ops.
- Week -1 (March 31): Final slide freeze + speaker rehearsal. Post-deck lightning review w/ comms.
- Day 0 (April 7): Company all-hands (11:00–12:00 PT). Follow-up survey closes 48h after.

Townloop agenda (60m):
- 0–5m: Opening (Ava) — framing + expected outcomes for the all-hands.
- 5–20m: Exec update highlights (CEO/Head of Product) — 2 slides each, 3 minute limit.
- 20–35m: OKR draft walkthrough & ask for decisions (Product + Eng) — surface tradeoffs.
- 35–45m: Risks & mitigations (SRE/Infra/Product Ops) — top 5 risks.
- 45–55m: Comms & slide flow check (Comms lead) — confirm final order and backup slides.
- 55–60m: Fast decisions & actions (Document owners assigned).

All-hands run-of-show (45–60m):
- 0–3m: Welcome + housekeeping (HR/Comms).
- 3–12m: CEO narrative: The mission, market context, and 3 company-level asks. (Use 3 bullets, each tied to a measurable signal.)
- 12–25m: Product highlight demo (PM + Eng) — production demo of a Hosted API latency win + short customer quote.
- 25–35m: OKR snapshot (40–60s per objective) — 3 company-level OKRs with top KRs.
- 35–50m: People & culture (hires, promotions, shoutouts).
- 50–60m: Live Q&A (moderated, up to 10 questions).

Draft Company-level OKRs (Q2 2025) — for townloop decisioning:
Objective A: Make inference predictable and economical for tier-1 production apps.
- KR A1: Achieve 99.5% of production requests within 120ms tail-latency on Redwood Hosted (p95 target).
- KR A2: Reduce median per-token cost for standard text-gen workloads by 18% vs. Q1 baseline via batching + quantization profiles.
- KR A3: Customer impact: onboard 6 customers to Redwood Dedicated pilot with SLA commitments.

Objective B: Ship the Redwood Private automated installer and first on-prem customer reference.
- KR B1: Complete private installer beta with two supported OS images and KMS integration.
- KR B2: Deliver an on-prem reference deployment and run three enterprise acceptance tests end-to-end.
- KR B3: Publish compliance playbook (SOC2-lite checklist for installer customers).

Objective C: Improve visibility and dev experience for platform users.
- KR C1: Ship token-level cost dashboard and per-route perf traces to GA (>=80% of paid accounts).
- KR C2: Reduce mean time to troubleshoot (MTTT) by 25% via instrumented traces and runbooks.

Notes on metrics & measurement:
- Baselines: use the product analytics dashboards and billing data from Feb 2025. Each KR must include owner and data source.
- Measurement cadence: weekly OKR dashboard sync; monthly exec review.

Open questions for townloop (decision/assignment needed):
- Which of the three objectives should be prioritized for company PR at the all-hands? (Recommend A + C.)
- Are the latency and cost KR targets achievable without additional hardware commitments for Dedicated? Eng leads to confirm by Townloop.
- Who signs off on the customer quote used in the product demo? CS + legal to confirm.

Risks and mitigation (top 5):
1) Dedicated capacity shortages during demo window — mitigation: pre-record demo + automatic fallback to Hosted.
2) Metrics volatility from billing pipeline — mitigation: freeze KR definitions; use last 30-day rolling average.
3) On-prem installer regressions — mitigation: limit first installs to sandboxed customer and run ops runbook.
4) All-hands slide overrun — mitigation: enforce strict speaker timers; one backup slide deck for extended Q&A.
5) Survey response bias — mitigation: incentivize feedback and allow anonymous submissions.

Preflight checklist (owners in parentheses):
- Slides received and reviewed by comms (Comms: Leah).
- Demo runbook + recording ready (Eng: Jonah).
- OKR owners assigned and KRs validated with data sources (Product Ops: Samantha).
- Legal/PR clearance for customer quote (Legal: Marco).
- Townloop attendees confirmed and calendar invites locked (Ava).

Communication artifacts (deliverables):
- Exec one-pager for execs + board (1 page).
- All-hands slides (master deck + backup).
- Short blog post skeleton for product highlight post (publishable after approval).
- Post-event survey link + summary template.

Post-all-hands follow-up plan:
- 48h: close survey; leads to summarize top feedback and any new asks.
- 7 days: publish final OKR sheet and assign action items.
- 14 days: cross-team SCRUM of owners to begin KR sprint planning.

Quick comms draft (CEO opener):
"We built a playbook last quarter for predictable serving — in Q2 we double down on measurable economics and reliability. Short-term, that means hitting latency targets while reducing cost per token; medium-term, it means giving customers a private option that is easy to deploy and safe to run. Today you’ll hear where we are, the risks we’re watching, and the concrete asks for each team.

Ready-made decisions for townloop: 
- Confirm top-priority objective (A/C recommended).
- Lock latency and cost targets or widen tolerances by +10% if infra needs change.
- Approve demo backup plan and legal sign-off path for customer quote.

Action items (immediate):
- Jonah: upload demo runbook + S3 link by 2025-03-10.
- Samantha: publish OKR baseline spreadsheet (data sources annotated) by 2025-03-12.
- Leah: first pass on slides due 2025-03-14.
- Marco (Legal): confirm customer quote clearance by 2025-03-20.

Appendix: Helpful links and references:
- Product roadmap PR: https://github.com/redwood/roadmap/pull/412
- OKR template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/OKR-TEMPLATE
- Hosting SLA draft: JIRA-7890

Notes to self / scratchpad (informal):
- Consider adding a short customer video clip instead of live demo if infra flaky.
- Keep OKR language plain; execs should be able to read KRs in <=10s each.
- Use the townloop to force small binary decisions; avoid open-ended debates there.

End of brief. Please add comments here with edits, owners, and any blocker flags.

title: Arg parser fuzz corpus for model onboarding (v1)
preview: A lightweight annotated fuzzing corpus and protocol intended to stress incoming model checkpoints during onboarding. Focused on argument parsing, ambiguous tool selection prompts, multi-step call sequences and partial KV cache state. Purpose is to catch brittle arg extraction and hallucinated tool calls before a rollout to staging.

title: Microbridge adapter — decknotes and quick execution plan
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact speaker-notes style doc for the Microbridge adapter — intended to be a one-slide-length narrative + executable checklist to hand to an integrator or SE. It pairs minimal concept sketches with a short, reliable execution path (30–60 minutes) for getting a local PoC running against Redwood hosted API and a sandbox router.

Audience:
- Solutions engineers doing a hands-on demo
- Developer advocates running quick workshops
- Integrators writing the first adapter between an existing orchestration layer and Redwood’s SDK

High-level promise:
- Show an end-to-end loop: local adapter -> Redwood client -> hosted model -> streamed response -> basic retry/fallback.
- Demonstrate cost/latency knobs (batching, max_tokens, model variant) with one change and one smoke test.

What this doc contains (quick):
1) Minimal architecture sketch (text)
2) 8-step quickstart checklist to run a demo in ~30 minutes
3) Smoke tests and failure-mode notes
4) Quick debugging checklist and rollback/cleanup
5) Suggested slide bullet list for a 5-minute demo intro

1) Minimal architecture (one-paragraph):
Microbridge is a thin adapter that translates an application-level request shape into Redwood’s runtime calls. For the demo we run a local adapter process that accepts a simple HTTP request (/generate) and forwards to the Redwood API client with a small router shim that can choose: hosted-default, hosted-lowcost (quantized), or local-sim (stub). The adapter exposes an admin endpoint (/telemetry) that reports request/response latency and token counts.

2) 8-step quickstart (do this in order):
- Prereq: local node or python env, Redwood API key (sandbox), Docker optional for stub server.
- Step A (clone): git clone https://github.com/redwood-inference/microbridge && cd microbridge/demo
- Step B (env): cp .env.example .env; set REDWOOD_API_KEY to sandbox key; set ADAPTER_PORT=8088
- Step C (install): npm ci  # or pip install -r requirements.txt (repo contains both; pick one)
- Step D (run): npm run start:adapter  # starts adapter on localhost:8088; shows admin URL in logs
- Step E (smoke): curl -X POST http://localhost:8088/generate -d '{"prompt":"Summarize: product launch notes"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'
  Expected: streaming fragments OR a single JSON payload with \"text\" + \"usage.tokens\"
- Step F (toggle): hit admin to switch routes: curl http://localhost:8088/admin/route?target=hosted-lowcost
  Re-run Step E to show cost/latency tradeoff.
- Step G (failure demo): stop network to Redwood client (or set the stub), call /generate and show fallback to local-sim variant.
- Step H (cleanup): npm run stop && docker-compose down --remove-orphans (if used).

3) Smoke tests (quick list):
- Basic success: POST /generate returns 200 + tokens < 200 for a short prompt.
- Streaming: if streaming is enabled the demo should emit at least 2 chunks within 300ms of first token.
- Fallback: disconnect outbound (simulate 502) and verify adapter returns pre-defined fallback text and records metric error_count=1.
- Telemetry: /telemetry shows last_request.latency_ms and last_request.tokens as numbers.

4) Failure-mode notes (what to expect and callouts):
- 429s under repeat load: show rate-limit header X-RateLimit-Reset and demonstrate backoff (exponential, capped at 2s) in the adapter.
- Partial stream drop: how the adapter surfaces incomplete chunks (it returns \"incomplete: true\" in the response).
- Bad API key: Redwood returns 401; the adapter should translate to 403 for the customer-facing API and log full 401 to admin only.
- Token budget surprise: emphasize demo uses tiny prompts; production must include token budget alerts tied to per-route quotas.

5) Quick debugging cheats (50–200 words):
- If you see all 502s: check outbound routing table in .env (ROUTER_HOST), validate network egress on port 443.
- Latency too high: enable adapter-level batching window to 10ms (ADAPTER_BATCH_WINDOW_MS=10) and re-run.
- Streaming not working: confirm the SDK is using response_mode=stream and the demo server is using chunked transfer encoding.
- Telemetry missing: confirm the adapter started with METRICS_ENABLED=true and that /telemetry returns JSON content-type.

6) Roll-forward / rollback guidance after a failed demo:
- Rollforward: If a model variant showed quality regressions, change route to hosted-default and rerun patch (no code deploy needed for routing change).
- Rollback: remove adapter from load balancer or swap DNS to the prior adapter instance. Keep a lightweight health-check endpoint (/healthz) to automate LB decisions.

7) Suggested 5-minute slide bullets (use these as the single slide):
- \"What we built\": Microbridge adapter — 3 endpoints, 2 routes, admin telemetry
- \"Why it matters\": same API for hosted/dedicated/private, fast toggle between cost/latency profiles
- \"Demo ask\": watch for latency change after toggling route; observe fallback when simulating outage
- \"Next steps\": add A/B gating and eval set tied to prompts; add per-route cost meter

Appendix (short snippets):
- Example minimal request to adapter:
  POST /generate
  Body: {"prompt":"Fix the following bug: \"date parsing on ETL\"","max_tokens":64}
- Expected minimal response (non-stream):
  {"text":"Patch: add timezone-aware parsing...","usage":{"tokens":58},"route":"hosted-lowcost"}

Notes to self / open questions (scratch):
- Add a short AB test harness that flips between hosted-default and hosted-lowcost and records correctness score against a 10-example eval set.
- Consider a one-click toggle UI in the admin that shows cost delta before switching.
- Need a short blurb on customer-facing error copy for 429 vs 503.

End — keep this one-pager in pocket for SEs. If useful, expand to a 2-page quickstart with annotated logs and explicit env var list.
